{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The Pharmaceutical Research and Manufacturers of America (PhRMA) is the collective voice of America’s leading biopharmaceutical research companies, committed to discovering and developing innovative new medicines that save lives, improve health outcomes, and advance biomedical science. Our member companies invest billions annually in a complex, multi-year research and development (R&D) process, bringing forward an average of $2.284 billion per asset to develop new therapies [4]. We appreciate the opportunity to provide comments on the agency’s proposed suite of new compliance requirements, FDA-2024-XXXX, designed to modernize and standardize oversight of regulated entities.

PhRMA shares the agency’s goal of enhancing data quality and record-keeping to improve safety monitoring and compliance. However, we are deeply concerned that the proposed requirements, particularly their implementation timelines and divergence from internationally harmonized standards, will inadvertently create significant operational burdens, delay patient access to life-saving medicines, and threaten the long-term innovation incentives essential for sustained R&D investment.

[BACKGROUND]
The agency’s proposal focuses on three primary areas: (1) revised reporting obligations, (2) specific implementation timelines, and (3) new documentation standards. While we support the principle of continuous improvement in regulatory processes, we believe the current proposal underestimates the systemic impacts of these changes on the biopharmaceutical innovation ecosystem, which operates under investment recovery timelines of 10-15 years per drug [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8]. Our comments will address each of these three areas, outlining the specific challenges and offering constructive recommendations to achieve the agency’s objectives without compromising the timely development and availability of new therapies.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**1. Unrealistic Implementation Timelines Threaten R&D Continuity and Patient Access**
PhRMA is deeply concerned that the proposed implementation timelines for transitioning to new reporting and documentation standards are fundamentally incompatible with the 10-15 year R&D investment and regulatory planning cycles that govern pharmaceutical drug development [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8]. A drug entering Phase II clinical trials today was designed under regulatory assumptions made 5-7 years ago. Abrupt transitions to new documentation and reporting standards mid-development would require companies to retroactively restructure clinical data packages, potentially invalidating existing datasets, delaying submissions, and forcing costly protocol amendments. For a single late-stage asset, these disruptions could cost tens of millions of dollars and delay patient access by 12-24 months [1].

Our members have clinical trials currently in execution under existing data management plans, IRB-approved protocols, and contractual agreements with clinical research sites that specify data collection and submission procedures. Revised reporting obligations that change frequency, format, or content mid-trial require sponsors to amend data management plans, revalidate electronic systems under 21 CFR Part 11, renegotiate site agreements, and in many cases submit protocol amendments for IRB review. Each of these steps introduces timeline delays. For trials in late-phase development—where patent and exclusivity period dependencies make every month of delay financially material—mid-trial compliance retrofits can translate directly into delayed NDA/BLA submissions and, ultimately, delayed patient access. The agency’s own data show that clinical development productivity reached a composite success rate of 10.8% in 2023—the highest since 2018—a fragile gain that could be reversed if regulatory uncertainty forces companies to slow or restructure active programs [1].

**2. Divergent Documentation Standards Increase Operational Burden and Hinder Global Development**
PhRMA is deeply concerned that the proposed documentation standards, as currently drafted, diverge from internationally harmonized frameworks established through ICH and other multilateral regulatory bodies, threatening to fragment the global regulatory environment that member companies depend upon to efficiently bring medicines to patients worldwide. PhRMA member companies operate under a unified global regulatory strategy, submitting Common Technical Documents (CTDs) and electronic CTDs (eCTDs) across dozens of jurisdictions simultaneously [4]. When a single major agency like this one introduces documentation standards that deviate from ICH-harmonized formats—such as the eCTD v4.0 framework that has been under coordinated international development since ICH Step-4 in 2015 [5]—it forces companies to maintain parallel documentation systems. This directly burdens our members' clinical trial and regulatory pathway coordination operations, which are already managing R&D investment timelines of 10–15 years and average per-asset development costs of approximately $2.284 billion [4]. Duplicative documentation infrastructure adds cost without improving safety outcomes, and those costs ultimately delay or deter investment in new therapies.

Our members operate global clinical trial portfolios—GlobalData reports 12,837 company-sponsored trials initiated in 2024 alone, a 23% increase from 2019 [5]—and must simultaneously satisfy documentation requirements from FDA, MHRA, Health Canada, EMA, and other regulators [8]. The proposed standards, if not harmonized with evolving international GCP frameworks, would require sponsors to maintain parallel documentation systems: one for existing international obligations and a separate, agency-specific format for U.S. regulatory review presentation. For a single Phase III trial spanning multiple countries, this could require duplicative audit-readiness infrastructure across dozens of investigative sites, multiplying the site burden that Tufts CSDD has already identified as a critical constraint on trial execution. Given that drug development timelines run 10–15 years and R&D investment recovery depends on maximizing productive use of patent and exclusivity periods, diverting resources to redundant documentation compliance directly erodes the capital available for innovation.

**3. Underestimated Compliance Costs Threaten R&D Investment Decisions**
PhRMA is deeply concerned that the proposed revisions to reporting frequency and format requirements for clinical trial data submissions will impose substantial, underestimated compliance costs on member companies that threaten to distort R&D investment decisions, particularly for complex multi-site and multi-national trials. PhRMA member companies operate under R&D investment recovery timelines of 10–15 years per drug, during which every incremental compliance cost must be absorbed against uncertain revenue outcomes. Revised reporting obligations—particularly changes to submission frequency, data format standards (e.g., mandatory E2B gateway transmissions for IND safety reports), and content requirements—require significant reconfiguration of existing electronic data management systems that are already subject to 21 CFR Part 11 compliance obligations. For a single large Phase III trial spanning hundreds of sites, the cost of re-engineering data capture, validation, and submission pipelines to meet new format mandates can run into the tens of millions of dollars [3]. Smaller member companies and those conducting rare disease trials with limited patient populations face disproportionate per-patient compliance cost burdens. The agency specifically requested comments on expected costs, and PhRMA responds that the proposal as drafted fails to account for the layered, cumulative cost of simultaneous format, frequency, and content changes imposed on systems that are already under active regulatory scrutiny.

In direct response to the agency's request for comments on expected costs and operational impacts, PhRMA notes that the pharmaceutical regulatory affairs and compliance function is already under significant resource strain. Industry-wide compliance investment reached $50 billion globally in 2023 [6]. For PhRMA members with active pipelines spanning multiple development stages, transitioning to new documentation standards simultaneously across all programs—rather than prospectively for new programs—would require substantial investments in regulatory operations staff, IT systems for data reformatting, legal review of existing clinical agreements, and CRO contract amendments. Smaller biopharmaceutical companies, which now account for a growing share of the innovation pipeline, lack the regulatory infrastructure to absorb these costs without diverting resources from active R&D programs, potentially causing pipeline attrition at precisely the stage where clinical productivity has been improving.

**4. Ambiguity in Orange Book Patent Listing Threatens Exclusivity Periods**
PhRMA is deeply concerned that the proposed revised reporting obligations regarding data submission content will create dangerous ambiguity in Orange Book patent listing practices, threatening the integrity of exclusivity period management at a time when R&D cost recovery is already under severe strain. PhRMA member companies coordinate clinical trial timelines, regulatory submissions, and patent prosecution strategies across 10-15 year development horizons. Revised data submission requirements that introduce new or ambiguous standards for what patent information must be disclosed—or how it must be characterized—create immediate legal exposure for Orange Book listings. As noted in recent legal analysis, Orange Book patent listing practices are already under heightened FTC antitrust scrutiny and active litigation regarding the scope of listable patents [6]. Superimposing new reporting obligations on this already contested landscape risks triggering Paragraph IV challenges prematurely or invalidating existing listings, collapsing exclusivity windows that are essential to recovering average R&D costs of $2.23 billion per asset [6]. The Prozac precedent illustrates the catastrophic market consequences of losing even two years of exclusivity—a $35 billion single-day market capitalization loss and 90% prescription shift to generics [6]—demonstrating that exclusivity period integrity is not a marginal concern but a foundational element of the innovation financing model.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To mitigate these critical impacts and ensure the agency’s modernization efforts do not inadvertently delay drug approvals, hinder innovation, or disproportionately burden regulated entities, PhRMA urges the agency to adopt the following recommendations:

**A. For Implementation Timelines & Streamlined Review Pathways:**
1.  **Adopt Phased, Risk-Stratified Timelines:** Replace uniform implementation deadlines with a tiered, milestone-based transition framework. Specifically, new INDs filed after the rule's effective date must comply immediately; products in Phase III or pre-NDA/BLA stages receive a minimum 36-month transition period; and products already under active review or in post-approval commitments receive a 48-60 month accommodation period [1]. This aligns timelines with clinical development milestones rather than arbitrary calendar deadlines.
2.  **Establish a Regulatory Continuity Safe Harbor:** Create a formal 'regulatory continuity' safe harbor allowing companies to complete development under the standards in effect at IND filing, consistent with the principle of regulatory predictability that underpins long-term R&D investment [1].
3.  **Grandfather Ongoing Clinical Trials:** Explicitly grandfather all clinical trials with an IND in effect at the time of the final rule's publication, applying new requirements only to trials initiated after a defined effective date at least 24 months post-publication [7].
4.  **Streamline Protocol Amendments:** For trials that voluntarily seek to comply early, provide a streamlined protocol amendment pathway that does not require full IRB re-review of data management plan changes that are purely administrative in nature [7].
5.  **Issue Clear Transition Guidance:** Issue detailed transition guidance, including model data management plan language, to reduce the interpretive burden on sponsors and sites navigating the changeover [7].

**B. For Documentation Standards & International Harmonization:**
1.  **Align with ICH, WHO, and eCTD v4.0:** Explicitly align the proposed documentation standards with existing ICH guidelines, WHO frameworks, and eCTD v4.0 specifications before finalizing any new requirements [3, 4, 5].
2.  **Conduct and Publish a Gap Analysis:** Conduct a side-by-side gap analysis comparing the proposed standards against ICH M2, M8, and M11–M15 working group outputs, and publish that analysis for public comment [4].
3.  **Coordinate with International Regulators:** Commit to adopting harmonized language wherever the proposed standards diverge without a compelling, evidence-based safety rationale, and coordinate with FDA, EMA, PMDA, and Health Canada to ensure any novel requirements are introduced through the ICH process rather than unilaterally [3, 4, 8].
4.  **Align with ICH E6(R3) GCP and EU-CTR:** Explicitly align the new documentation standards with the ICH E6(R3) GCP framework and the EU Clinical Trials Regulation (EU-CTR) 536/2014 transparency standards, both of which have undergone recent revision to modernize record-keeping expectations [5, 8].
5.  **Accept eTMF Formats:** Accept electronic Trial Master File (eTMF) formats that already meet international standards as presumptively compliant with the new requirements [5].
6.  **Convene Public Workshop with International Counterparts:** Convene a public workshop with international regulatory counterparts before finalizing the rule to identify and eliminate divergences [5, 8].
7.  **Assess and Commit to ICH Process for New Standards:** Formally assess whether the proposed new standards are consistent with or diverge from current ICH guidelines before finalizing any implementation timeline, and commit to submitting any materially divergent requirements to the ICH process for international harmonization prior to domestic implementation [1, 2, 8].
8.  **Adopt Interim Compliance Pathway:** Where immediate domestic action is deemed necessary, adopt an interim compliance pathway that accepts ICH-compliant submissions as satisfying the new requirements during a harmonization transition period of no less than 48 months [1, 2, 8].

**C. For Reporting Obligations, Innovation Incentives & Cost Recovery:**
1.  **Conduct Rigorous Cost-Benefit Analysis:** Publish a detailed regulatory impact analysis disaggregating compliance costs by trial phase, company size, and therapeutic area before finalizing the rule, and adopt a phased implementation schedule that allows at least 36 months for system reconfiguration and validation, consistent with the FDA's own experience with E2B gateway implementation timelines [2, 6].
2.  **Convene Public Workshop on Costs:** Convene a public workshop with industry IT and regulatory operations teams to validate cost estimates prior to the final rule [2].
3.  **Clarify Orange Book Patent Eligibility:** Explicitly state in the final rule that revised data submission content requirements are procedural in nature and do not alter the substantive legal standards for Orange Book patent eligibility [6].
4.  **Provide Safe Harbor for Existing Patent Listings:** Provide a safe harbor period of no less than 24 months before any new submission format requirements apply to previously listed patents [6].
5.  **Issue Guidance on Paragraph IV:** Issue concurrent guidance clarifying how revised submission standards interact with existing Paragraph IV certification procedures [6].
6.  **Convene Working Group on Patent/Data Harmonization:** Convene a working group with industry, FDA, and USPTO representatives to harmonize data submission standards with patent listing legal frameworks before finalizing any changes [6, 7].
7.  **Establish Small Business Accommodation:** Establish a small business accommodation provision that extends implementation timelines by an additional 24 months for companies with fewer than 500 employees or annual revenues below $500 million [6].
8.  **Create Technical Assistance Program:** Create a no-cost technical assistance program to help smaller companies understand and implement the new standards, consistent with the agency's stated goal of balancing data quality needs with operational practicalities [6].

[CONCLUSION]
PhRMA and its member companies are committed to supporting science-based regulatory modernization that enhances data quality and patient safety. However, we are deeply concerned that the proposed implementation timelines and documentation standards, as currently drafted, will inadvertently delay drug approvals, create significant operational burdens, and threaten the long-term innovation incentives that drive the discovery of new therapies. By adopting the recommendations outlined above, the agency can achieve its objectives while preserving a predictable, harmonized, and science-driven regulatory environment that supports timely patient access to life-saving medicines and fosters continued biopharmaceutical innovation. We appreciate the opportunity to provide these comments and look forward to continued engagement with the agency on this important rulemaking.

### References
[1] https://www.pharmainbrief.com/2024/01/pharma-in-briefs-2023-year-in-review-and-trends-for-2024/
[2] https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12299679/
[3] https://msfaccess.org/sites/default/files/2024-04/Transparency%20CORE%20toolkit_manual.pdf
[4] https://www.pharmafocusamerica.com/strategy/how-regulatory-changes-are-shaping
[5] https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12500574/
[6] https://www.morganlewis.com/pubs/2025/01/drugs-and-biologics-at-fda-reflections-on-2024-activity-shed-light-on-the-road-ahead
[7] https://www.fda.gov/media/166049/download
[8] https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12616134/

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
Public Citizen Health Research Group submits these comments in strong support of the proposed suite of new compliance requirements designed to modernize and standardize the oversight of regulated industry. As an independent, non-profit consumer advocacy organization dedicated to improving public health and safety, we have a long-standing interest in the quality, consistency, and accessibility of data that underpins the U.S. regulatory system. We appreciate the opportunity to provide feedback on this important proposal.

[BACKGROUND]
The agency is proposing significant revisions across three primary areas: the revision of existing reporting obligations, the establishment of specific implementation timelines for new mandates, and the introduction of more rigorous documentation standards. These changes are intended to address "perceived gaps in current data collection and record-keeping practices" and to "improve the quality of information it receives, thereby enhancing its ability to monitor safety and compliance." We agree that such gaps exist and that the proposed changes are necessary to ensure effective public health oversight. Our comments will focus on how these provisions impact public interest research and advocacy, and we will urge the agency to finalize the rule with enhancements that prioritize patient safety and public transparency over industry convenience.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
As a small-staff advocacy organization that relies almost entirely on FOIA requests to obtain agency records, adverse event data, inspection reports, and internal communications needed to evaluate drug and device safety, Public Citizen Health Research Group is directly affected by the quality and consistency of underlying documentation. When regulated entities maintain records in inconsistent formats, use non-standardized terminology, or store data in proprietary or non-searchable systems, our FOIA requests generate voluminous, poorly organized document productions that require disproportionate staff time to review [1]. This operational burden effectively limits our capacity to monitor safety issues across a broad regulatory portfolio — a constraint that ultimately harms the public interest we serve.

Responding directly to the agency's question on 'operational impacts,' we emphasize that inadequate documentation standards impose significant operational costs on public interest researchers — costs that are systematically undercounted in standard regulatory impact analyses that focus exclusively on industry compliance burdens. When underlying documentation is inconsistent — for example, when adverse event narratives use non-standardized terminology or when records lack date and version metadata — the resulting FOIA productions require extensive manual review that can consume weeks of staff time per request [1]. For a small organization covering a broad regulatory portfolio, this is not a marginal inconvenience; it is a structural barrier to effective public oversight.

Our ability to conduct evidence-based safety advocacy is directly constrained by the quality and consistency of underlying documentation. When records are created in inconsistent formats, stored in proprietary systems, or presented in ways that obscure key data fields, our FOIA-obtained documents require extensive manual processing before they can support any analytical work. The proposed documentation standards, if implemented with public accessibility in mind, would dramatically reduce this burden and allow our limited staff to spend more time on substantive safety analysis rather than data cleaning and format conversion [2, 3].

We strongly support the revised reporting obligations requiring more frequent, standardized, and content-rich data submissions, because enhanced data quality and transparency are essential preconditions for our evidence-based petition filings seeking drug withdrawals and label changes. The current patchwork of inconsistent reporting formats materially impairs our ability to identify and document safety signals. Our small staff routinely files citizen petitions under 21 CFR § 10.30 requesting drug withdrawals and label changes, work that depends entirely on our ability to analyze postmarketing adverse event data, clinical trial datasets, and safety surveillance records. When manufacturers submit data in inconsistent formats, at irregular intervals, or with incomplete content fields, we must expend disproportionate staff time — often supplemented by FOIA requests — simply reconstructing a coherent safety picture before we can even begin substantive analysis [4]. Standardized, more frequent submissions would reduce the information asymmetry between industry and public interest advocates like us, directly improving the evidentiary foundation of our petitions [8]. Responding directly to the agency's question on operational impacts: the current non-standardized reporting environment imposes significant hidden costs on civil society organizations that serve as a check on FDA accountability, costs that are invisible in any industry-focused cost-benefit analysis.

Our organization files petitions under 21 U.S.C. § 355(e) and related authorities requesting drug withdrawals and label changes. The quality of those petitions depends entirely on the completeness and accessibility of post-market safety data submitted by manufacturers. When reporting obligations are lax—allowing infrequent, inconsistently formatted, or content-deficient submissions—our staff must file FOIA requests, parse incompatible data structures, and often wait months for usable information [6, 7]. This operational drag means that dangerous drugs remain on the market longer than necessary while we assemble the evidentiary record. Standardized, higher-frequency submissions would allow us to identify safety signals faster and file better-supported petitions with less resource expenditure, directly serving the public health mission the agency shares.

We strongly support the more rigorous documentation standards in the proposed rule and specifically urge the agency to ensure that enhanced documentation requirements apply with equal force to postmarketing adverse event reporting. Gaps in postmarketing safety documentation are the single most common evidentiary obstacle we encounter when petitioning for drug label changes. Responding directly to the agency's question on expected costs: the agency's cost framing appears to focus primarily on compliance costs borne by regulated industry, while omitting the substantial public health costs imposed by inadequate documentation [3]. When manufacturers fail to maintain adequate records — a recurring finding in FDA inspections (citing 21 CFR 312.57(a) 'Lack of adequate records for IP' as a top 483 observation theme) — the downstream effect is that safety signals go undetected or undocumented, petitions for label changes lack the evidentiary support needed to compel agency action, and patients continue to be exposed to inadequately characterized risks. Under 21 CFR § 314.80 and related provisions [7], adverse event reporting obligations already exist — but documentation quality standards determine whether those reports are actionable.

We support the proposed documentation standards and specifically urge the agency to address the unintended consequence that poorly scoped standards could allow regulated industry to satisfy technical compliance while still obscuring safety-critical information — a risk that would undermine the very purpose of the rule and harm our capacity for evidence-based safety advocacy. In our experience filing petitions for drug withdrawals and label changes, we have repeatedly encountered situations where companies technically maintained records but structured them in ways that made adverse event patterns difficult to detect — burying safety signals in voluminous, inconsistently formatted submissions [2, 3]. If the new documentation standards focus only on form (e.g., that a record exists and is filed) rather than substance (e.g., that safety-relevant data fields are complete, consistent, and comparable across submissions), regulated entities may achieve compliance without improving the actual quality of information available for safety review. This would leave our advocacy work no better supported than it is today, despite the regulatory burden imposed on industry.

Finally, we strongly support the proposed implementation timelines as a necessary mechanism to ensure regulated entities cannot indefinitely delay compliance with modernized safety reporting standards, and we urge the agency to resist industry pressure to extend deadlines that would leave patients exposed to preventable harms during prolonged transition periods. As a small advocacy organization with a limited staff covering a broad regulatory portfolio—including drug and device safety advocacy, FDA accountability campaigns, and petition filings—Public Citizen Health Research Group has direct experience monitoring how extended implementation timelines create enforcement gaps that harm patients [8, 9]. When regulated entities are granted lengthy transition periods, our staff must divert scarce resources from proactive safety advocacy to tracking whether industry is actually meeting phased milestones, effectively subsidizing industry's compliance delay with our own organizational capacity. Every month a new reporting or documentation standard is not in effect is a month during which safety signals may go uncaptured or underreported, undermining the very purpose of this rulemaking.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To ensure the proposed rule effectively addresses safety gaps and supports robust public oversight, we recommend the following enhancements:

1.  **Regarding Documentation Standards:** The agency should amend the proposed documentation standards to include an explicit requirement that records be maintained in machine-readable, non-proprietary formats that are compatible with standard FOIA processing and e-discovery tools [1]. Specifically, the agency should (1) mandate structured metadata tagging for all required records so that responsive documents can be identified and produced efficiently; (2) prohibit the use of proprietary file formats that impede electronic search and review; and (3) require that document retention systems used by regulated entities be capable of producing records in formats compatible with the agency's own FOIA processing infrastructure. Additionally, the agency should explicitly require that all records subject to the new documentation standards be maintained in open, non-proprietary, machine-readable formats (e.g., structured XML, JSON, or CSV where applicable) and that FOIA-responsive productions of these records default to these formats rather than scanned PDFs or redacted paper copies [2, 3]. The agency should also specify that documentation standards include mandatory metadata fields — such as date of creation, responsible party, version history, and data source — that are preserved intact in any regulatory submission or FOIA production.
2.  **Regarding Revised Reporting Obligations:** The agency should adopt the revised reporting obligations as proposed, and should additionally require that all postmarketing safety data submissions be made publicly accessible in machine-readable, standardized formats (consistent with FDA's own Data Standards Strategy FY2023–FY2027 [5]) within a defined timeframe after submission [4]. The agency should explicitly reject any industry request to narrow the content requirements or extend submission intervals, as such accommodations would disproportionately harm public interest advocates who rely on complete, timely data. We further recommend the agency establish a public-facing dashboard aggregating standardized submission data to reduce the FOIA burden on under-resourced advocacy organizations. The agency should adopt mandatory structured data formats (such as E2B(R3) electronic Individual Case Safety Report standards, already finalized by FDA in August 2024 per the FDA Regional Implementation Guide for E2B(R3)) for all post-market safety submissions, and should require that these structured reports be submitted no less frequently than quarterly. The agency should further require that all submitted safety data be made publicly accessible in machine-readable format within 30 days of receipt, so that public interest organizations like ours can conduct independent signal detection without relying solely on FOIA [6, 7]. This would reduce our per-petition research burden by an estimated 30–50% and accelerate the timeline from signal identification to formal petition filing.
3.  **Regarding Documentation Standards for Postmarketing Adverse Event Reporting:** The agency should explicitly extend the enhanced documentation standards to postmarketing adverse event reporting and should require that documentation be maintained in formats that are accessible to public interest petitioners through FOIA requests without requiring extensive reformatting [3, 4]. The agency should also conduct and publish a full social cost-benefit analysis that accounts for the public health costs of documentation failures — including delayed label changes, continued patient exposure to inadequately characterized risks, and the advocacy costs borne by organizations like Public Citizen in reconstructing safety records that manufacturers should have maintained. This analysis should be made available for public comment before the rule is finalized.
4.  **Regarding Operational Impacts of Documentation Standards:** In its final regulatory impact analysis, the agency should explicitly quantify the operational benefits of improved documentation standards for FOIA requesters and the public, not only for the agency and regulated industry [1]. The agency should commission or cite empirical data on FOIA processing times and costs attributable to poor documentation quality, and should include these benefits in its cost-benefit calculus. Additionally, the agency should establish a stakeholder advisory process — including public interest organizations — to evaluate whether proposed documentation standards are practically accessible to non-industry users of agency records.
5.  **Regarding Preventing Obscuration of Safety Information:** In response to the agency's specific request for comments on operational impacts and alternatives, we recommend the agency adopt a two-tier documentation standard: (1) a baseline structural standard governing format, metadata, and retention, and (2) a substantive completeness standard specifying which data fields are mandatory and non-waivable for safety-relevant record categories [2, 3]. The agency should draw on the Joint Commission's model of integrated, audit-ready reporting (as reflected in 2023 Patient Safety Goals) as a benchmark for what 'complete' documentation looks like in practice. The agency should also commit to publishing annual summary statistics on documentation deficiency findings so that public advocates can assess whether the standards are achieving their intended purpose.
6.  **Regarding Implementation Timelines:** The agency should establish firm, non-extendable implementation deadlines with interim milestone checkpoints (e.g., at 30%, 60%, and 90% of the transition period) that are publicly reported [8, 9]. The agency should explicitly reject requests for blanket timeline extensions and instead create a narrow, evidence-based hardship waiver process requiring documented proof of good-faith compliance efforts. This structure would allow the agency to monitor progress transparently and allow advocacy organizations like ours to hold both the agency and regulated entities accountable without requiring us to maintain continuous FOIA-dependent surveillance of an open-ended transition.

[CONCLUSION]
Public Citizen Health Research Group strongly supports the agency's efforts to modernize and standardize reporting and documentation requirements. These changes are crucial for enhancing the agency's ability to monitor safety and compliance, and equally important, for enabling meaningful public oversight. We urge the agency to finalize this rule with the recommended enhancements, explicitly designing the standards with downstream FOIA accessibility and evidence-based safety advocacy in mind. Prioritizing robust documentation and timely reporting is not merely a matter of regulatory compliance; it is a fundamental precondition for safeguarding patient safety and public health. We thank the agency for the opportunity to comment.

### References
[1] https://www.justice.gov/oip/united-states-department-justice-2024-chief-foia-officer-report
[2] https://www.hceglobal.org/joint-commission-2023-patient-safety-goals
[3] https://www.hrsa.gov/sites/default/files/hrsa/optn/assessment-report-2024-patient-safety-data-analysis.pdf
[4] https://cytel.com/perspectives/new-fda-data-submission-requirements-and-substantial-changes/
[5] https://www.fda.gov/media/110928/download
[6] https://rjionline.org/news/reporting-on-treatment-for-addiction-where-is-the-data/
[7] https://www.wtwco.com/en-us/insights/2024/02/cms-releases-revised-rxdc-reporting-instructions-for-2023
[8] https://www.troutmanprivacy.com/2026/05/regulation-s-p-compliance-for-small-firms-preparing-for-the-upcoming-compliance-deadline/
[9] https://smallbusiness.house.gov/uploadedfiles/05.22.2024_-_house_committee_on_small_business_rfa_report.pdf

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
[Date]

[Agency Contact Information - e.g., Division of Dockets Management (HFA-305), Food and Drug Administration, 5630 Fishers Lane, Room 1061, Rockville, MD 20852]

**Re: Docket No. FDA-2024-XXXX, [Title of Proposed Rule]**

Dear Sir or Madam:

Johnson & Johnson (J&J) is a global healthcare company committed to improving the health and well-being of people worldwide. With operations spanning pharmaceuticals, biologics, medical devices, and consumer health products, we are uniquely positioned to provide comprehensive feedback on the proposed rule [Title of Proposed Rule]. We appreciate the opportunity to comment on the agency's efforts to modernize and standardize oversight of regulated industry.

While we support the agency's stated goals of enhancing safety and compliance through improved data collection and record-keeping, we are deeply concerned that the proposed requirements, as currently structured, will generate substantial operational burdens, compliance costs, and innovation barriers without commensurate public health benefit. Specifically, our comments focus on the critical need for regulatory clarity across product categories, harmonization with international standards, and a phased implementation strategy for new manufacturing requirements. We urge the agency to adopt a more flexible, risk-based approach that recognizes the complexities of global healthcare manufacturing and avoids unnecessary disruption to patient access.

[BACKGROUND]
The agency is proposing a suite of new compliance requirements designed to modernize and standardize the oversight of its regulated industry. The core regulatory changes focus on three primary areas: the revision of existing reporting obligations, the establishment of specific implementation timelines for new mandates, and the introduction of more rigorous documentation standards. This proposal is being introduced to address perceived gaps in current data collection and record-keeping practices.

Johnson & Johnson's comments will address:
*   The **revision of existing reporting obligations**, particularly regarding content and format requirements for data submissions.
*   The **establishment of specific implementation timelines** for transitioning to new compliance standards.
*   The **introduction of more rigorous documentation standards** governing how records are created, maintained, and presented.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**1. Need for Regulatory Clarity and Harmonization Across FDA Centers (CDER, CBER, CDRH) for Reporting Obligations**
Johnson & Johnson is deeply concerned that the proposed revised reporting obligations, as currently structured, impose disproportionate and unquantified compliance costs on large multi-division pharmaceutical and biologics manufacturers without demonstrating a commensurate improvement in safety outcomes. The agency has specifically requested data on expected costs, and J&J urges the agency to conduct a rigorous cost-benefit analysis before finalizing any new reporting frequency or format mandates.

As a company operating across CDER, CBER, and CDRH-regulated product lines—including NDAs, BLAs, ANDAs, biologics, and combination products—J&J faces multiplicative compliance burdens when reporting obligations are revised without harmonization across FDA centers. A single change to post-market surveillance submission formats, for example, requires parallel updates to our pharmacovigilance IT infrastructure, safety database configurations, regulatory affairs staffing, and global reporting workflows. FDA's own FY2024 data shows that even after CARES Act implementation, BLA compliance with drug amount reporting reached only 71% and NDA compliance only 65% industry-wide—indicating that the current reporting framework already strains industry capacity [1]. Imposing additional revised obligations before the existing framework is fully absorbed risks further compliance fragmentation, particularly for complex biologics and combination products that span multiple FDA centers.

**2. Need for Harmonization with International Standards and Phased Implementation for Timelines**
Johnson & Johnson is deeply concerned that the proposed implementation timelines for transitioning to new compliance standards are insufficiently calibrated to the realities of global pharmaceutical and medical device manufacturing, and urges the agency to adopt a phased, risk-tiered timeline of no less than 36 months for full compliance, with interim milestones that account for multi-jurisdictional harmonization obligations.

As a multi-division enterprise operating pharmaceutical, biologics, and medical device manufacturing facilities across dozens of countries, Johnson & Johnson must simultaneously satisfy regulatory requirements from FDA (CDER, CBER, CDRH), EMA, MHRA, PMDA, and other sovereign regulators. The proposed timeline creates a direct conflict: compliance changes mandated by this rule may contradict or run ahead of parallel harmonization efforts underway through ICH and other international bodies. For example, ICH's ongoing reflection on continuous process verification and advanced manufacturing technologies explicitly identifies that 'global harmonisation on regulatory expectations and clarity' remains an active, unresolved work item. Forcing J&J's global manufacturing network to lock in documentation and reporting standards on the agency's proposed schedule, before ICH guidance is finalized, risks creating irreconcilable compliance divergences across our 60+ manufacturing sites. This is not a theoretical concern: our quality systems teams estimate that a premature timeline would require site-by-site remediation across multiple regulatory jurisdictions, generating duplicative validation work, potential supply disruptions, and significant capital expenditure that would not be necessary under a better-sequenced approach.

Many of J&J's global manufacturing facilities are already subject to rigorous inspection and certification by EMA, MHRA, PMDA, and other stringent regulatory authorities operating under ICH-harmonized standards. Requiring these facilities to independently comply with the proposed U.S. timeline — on a separate schedule, with separate documentation artifacts — does not improve safety outcomes; it creates redundant compliance infrastructure that consumes quality system resources that would otherwise be directed toward genuine risk management. The OSHA HazCom amendment aligning with GHS Rev. 7 [2], [3] demonstrates that the U.S. regulatory system is capable of adopting equivalency-based approaches that recognize international standards rather than requiring duplicative domestic compliance — a model the agency should replicate here. Absent such an alternative, J&J's global quality teams will be forced to maintain parallel compliance tracks for the same manufacturing processes, increasing the risk of documentation inconsistencies that themselves create compliance vulnerabilities.

**3. Need for Regulatory Clarity and Harmonization Across FDA Centers (CDER, CBER, CDRH) for Documentation Standards**
Johnson & Johnson is deeply concerned that the proposed documentation standards, as currently drafted, fail to account for the structural reality of multi-center regulated entities, imposing divergent and potentially irreconcilable record-keeping requirements across CDER, CBER, and CDRH that will generate substantial duplicative compliance costs without commensurate public health benefit.

As a company operating simultaneously under CDER (pharmaceutical drugs), CBER (biologics and combination products), and CDRH (medical devices), Johnson & Johnson maintains parallel regulatory compliance infrastructures for each FDA center. The proposed documentation standards appear to have been developed without sufficient cross-center harmonization, meaning our teams would be required to maintain materially different record formats, retention schedules, and audit trail architectures for products that may share manufacturing platforms, clinical data, or supply chain documentation. For example, a combination product involving a biologic drug-device interface would require our teams to satisfy CDER study data standards (CDISC/SDTM formats per FDA's existing study data submission requirements), CBER-specific ICSR and HL7 FHIR reporting structures [4], [5], and CDRH's distinct submission and quality system documentation requirements — all simultaneously, with no unified compliance pathway. Our internal estimate is that maintaining three non-harmonized documentation systems across a global manufacturing footprint of this scale would require significant new FTE investment, IT infrastructure upgrades, and ongoing validation costs, in addition to the risk of inadvertent non-compliance at the seams between center jurisdictions.

**4. Need for Harmonization with International Standards for Medical Device Documentation**
Johnson & Johnson is concerned that the proposed documentation standards, as currently framed, will create duplicative and conflicting compliance burdens across our medical device R&D and regulatory submission operations by failing to harmonize with internationally recognized frameworks—particularly the IMDRF Non-IVD Regulatory Submission Table of Contents (IMDRF/RPS WG/N9 FINAL:2024, Edition 4) and FDA's own eSTAR electronic submission format—thereby imposing significant redundant costs without commensurate safety benefit.

As a large, multi-division manufacturer operating across FDA's CDRH and CBER centers, Johnson & Johnson maintains parallel regulatory submission pipelines for 510(k) clearances, PMAs, and combination product pathways. Our regulatory affairs teams already invest substantial resources to align documentation with FDA's eSTAR format (mandatory as of October 1, 2023) and with IMDRF's globally harmonized submission structure. If the proposed documentation standards introduce a third, agency-specific layer of formatting and presentation requirements that diverges from these existing frameworks, we estimate significant additional FTE hours per submission cycle across our device portfolio—costs that will ultimately slow the pace at which safe, effective devices reach patients. The agency specifically requested comment on expected costs and operational impacts, and we respond directly: without harmonization, our internal estimate is that each major PMA or De Novo submission could require an additional 200–400 hours of reformatting and cross-referencing work, multiplied across dozens of active submissions annually.

**5. Need for Phased Implementation for Documentation Standards**
Johnson & Johnson urges the agency to extend the proposed implementation timeline for the new documentation standards by a minimum of 24 months for multi-center regulated entities, and to phase in requirements by product category, because the current timeline is operationally unachievable for large, globally integrated manufacturers without creating unacceptable risks of supply disruption and inadvertent non-compliance.

Johnson & Johnson's global manufacturing network spans dozens of facilities producing pharmaceutical, biologic, and device products under a single integrated quality management system. Implementing new documentation standards requires not only policy revision but validated IT system changes, retraining of thousands of quality and regulatory personnel across multiple time zones, and re-negotiation of documentation requirements with contract manufacturers and suppliers. The agency's own CBER-CDER Data Standards Program has been iterating since January 2023 and is still issuing quarterly updates as recently as March 2026 (Version 2.2) [4], [5], demonstrating that even FDA's internal systems require multi-year development cycles to implement new data standards. Imposing a compressed compliance timeline on industry — while FDA's own cross-center standards remain in flux — creates a scenario where Johnson & Johnson would be required to invest in systems that may need to be rebuilt again when FDA finalizes its own internal harmonization. Furthermore, CDRH processed over 20,000 submissions in 2024 alone [6], and any documentation non-conformance triggered by rushed implementation could delay critical product approvals, harming patients who depend on timely access to our medical devices and therapies.

**6. Litigation Risk from Expanded Reporting Obligations**
Johnson & Johnson is deeply concerned that the proposed revised reporting obligations—specifically the expanded content requirements for data submissions—will create a compelled disclosure regime that systematically exposes confidential regulatory communications to civil discovery in ongoing and future product liability litigation, fundamentally undermining the company's ability to engage candidly with the agency and threatening the integrity of the regulatory process itself.

As a company operating across pharmaceutical, biologic, medical device, and consumer health product lines, J&J is simultaneously subject to oversight from multiple FDA centers and is a defendant in numerous active product liability proceedings. The proposed rule's requirement to submit expanded datasets, internal safety analyses, and detailed methodological documentation to the agency creates a substantial and foreseeable risk that plaintiffs' counsel will seek these submissions through civil discovery under Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(b)(1), which defines discoverable material broadly as 'any non-privileged matter that is relevant to any party's claim.' Because regulatory submissions generally do not enjoy attorney-client privilege or work-product protection, the expanded content mandates would effectively transform J&J's internal safety assessments into a publicly accessible litigation record. This chilling effect is not hypothetical: the 2024 litigation trends environment is characterized by increasingly aggressive discovery practices and a growing volume of mass tort and product liability claims targeting consumer goods and pharmaceutical manufacturers [8], [9]. Requiring J&J to submit granular internal data analyses, adverse event narratives with expanded contextual detail, and comparative safety benchmarking as part of routine reporting obligations would hand litigants a roadmap to internal decision-making that was never intended for adversarial use.

**7. Operational Impacts on Post-Market Clinical Studies and Pharmacovigilance**
Johnson & Johnson is concerned that the proposed changes to clinical trial data submission content and format requirements will significantly disrupt ongoing post-market clinical studies and pharmacovigilance operations, creating operational impacts the agency may have underestimated. The agency has specifically requested comment on operational impacts, and J&J submits that mid-study format changes impose costs and risks that are qualitatively different from—and more severe than—those affecting pre-study planning.

J&J currently manages a global portfolio of post-market clinical commitments, post-approval studies, and ongoing pharmacovigilance data streams across multiple therapeutic areas. According to a Tufts Center for the Study of Drug Development impact report cited in recent industry literature, 70% of global investigative site staff report that trials have become significantly more difficult to manage over the past five years, driven in large part by increasing regulatory complexity. Imposing revised data submission content requirements mid-study would require J&J to retrofit existing clinical data management systems, renegotiate data collection agreements with contract research organizations, retrain site personnel, and potentially amend study protocols—each of which introduces timeline delays, cost overruns, and potential data integrity risks. For biologics post-market studies in particular, where patient populations are small and data collection windows are narrow, any disruption to submission workflows could compromise the completeness of safety signals reaching the agency.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
In light of the significant operational burdens, compliance costs, and potential patient access impacts outlined above, Johnson & Johnson respectfully offers the following recommendations:

**Regarding Regulatory Clarity and Harmonization Across FDA Centers:**
*   **Revised Reporting Obligations:**
    *   The agency should publish a detailed regulatory impact analysis quantifying per-submission cost increases for NDA, BLA, and combination product holders separately.
    *   Delay implementation of revised reporting obligations until existing CARES Act reporting requirements achieve at least 85% industry-wide compliance, as evidenced by FDA's own metrics [1].
    *   Convene a cross-center working group (CDER/CBER/CDRH) to harmonize any new format or content requirements so that multi-division companies like J&J are not required to maintain divergent reporting systems for functionally similar data.
*   **Documentation Standards:**
    *   Prior to finalizing any new documentation standards, convene a formal cross-center harmonization working group — inclusive of CDER, CBER, and CDRH — to identify and resolve conflicts between center-specific documentation requirements.
    *   The final rule should include a unified documentation framework or, at minimum, an explicit safe harbor provision for multi-center regulated entities that establishes a single master record standard acceptable across all three centers.
    *   Issue a combined guidance document that maps how the new standards interact with existing center-specific requirements, including the CBER-CDER Data Standards Program Action Plan, so that industry can plan compliance investments with certainty [4], [5].

**Regarding Harmonization with International Standards:**
*   **Implementation Timelines:**
    *   Extend the primary compliance deadline to a minimum of 36 months from the rule's effective date.
    *   Establish a formal coordination mechanism with ICH, EMA, and MHRA to sequence this rule's implementation timelines against parallel international harmonization workstreams.
    *   Create a tiered compliance schedule that prioritizes highest-risk product categories first, allowing manufacturers to sequence remediation efforts rationally.
    *   Commit to issuing binding guidance on international equivalency determinations before the compliance deadline, so that manufacturers operating under harmonized foreign standards are not required to duplicate compliant systems solely to satisfy divergent U.S. formatting or documentation requirements.
    *   Establish a formal equivalency determination process by which manufacturing sites certified by ICH-member stringent regulatory authorities within the preceding 24 months may demonstrate compliance through submission of foreign inspection reports and gap analyses, rather than full independent remediation on the domestic timeline [2], [3].
    *   Align the final rule's implementation schedule with the next ICH Q10/Q12 revision cycle to ensure that domestic compliance investments are durable.
*   **Documentation Standards (Medical Devices):**
    *   Explicitly anchor the new documentation standards to the IMDRF/RPS WG/N9 FINAL:2024 (Edition 4) submission table-of-contents structure and FDA's existing eSTAR template as the baseline presentation framework.
    *   Where the agency identifies genuine gaps not addressed by these frameworks, publish a narrow, clearly delineated addendum specifying only the incremental requirements, with a side-by-side crosswalk document showing how existing IMDRF and eSTAR elements satisfy each new requirement.

**Regarding Phased Implementation:**
*   **Implementation Timelines (Documentation Standards):**
    *   Adopt a tiered implementation schedule: (1) an initial 12-month period for publication of final, harmonized guidance; (2) a subsequent 12-month voluntary compliance period with technical assistance; and (3) mandatory compliance beginning no sooner than 24 months post-finalization, with an additional 12-month extension available for multi-center entities demonstrating good-faith implementation progress [4], [5], [6].
    *   Commit to freezing the underlying data standards specifications for a minimum of 18 months prior to the mandatory compliance date.
    *   Create a voluntary early-compliance certification pathway with extended timelines for sites that commit to phased remediation milestones.
*   **Revised Reporting Obligations (Clinical Trial Data):**
    *   Grandfather all clinical studies that have already initiated data collection under existing format and content standards, applying new requirements only to studies initiated after a defined effective date.
    *   Provide a minimum 24-month implementation runway for post-market surveillance systems to allow for IT infrastructure upgrades and vendor contract modifications.
    *   Issue detailed technical specifications and structured data templates in advance of the effective date.

**Regarding Litigation Risk:**
*   **Revised Reporting Obligations (Content Requirements):**
    *   Explicitly codify in the final rule that data submissions made pursuant to these revised reporting obligations are designated as confidential commercial information under 21 C.F.R. Part 20 and are not subject to public disclosure absent a specific statutory mandate.
    *   Work with the Department of Justice to develop formal guidance affirming that expanded regulatory submissions are protected from civil discovery to the maximum extent permitted by law [8], [9].
    *   Narrow the content requirements to limit submissions to aggregated, anonymized safety signals rather than granular case-level narratives or internal deliberative analyses.
    *   Establish a formal pre-submission consultation process allowing companies to flag specific data elements that present acute litigation privilege concerns.

[CONCLUSION]
Johnson & Johnson remains committed to working collaboratively with the agency to achieve its goals of modernizing and standardizing regulatory oversight. However, we believe that the current proposal, without significant modification, will create disproportionate burdens on regulated entities, particularly large, multi-division, and globally integrated manufacturers, potentially hindering innovation and patient access.

We urge the agency to adopt a final rule that provides greater regulatory clarity across product categories, harmonizes with internationally recognized standards, and implements new requirements through a phased, risk-tiered approach that accounts for the operational realities of global healthcare manufacturing. We stand ready to provide further input and data as needed.

Sincerely,

[Signature]

[Name]
[Title]
Johnson & Johnson

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
As a dietary supplement safety researcher who has dedicated years to analyzing stimulant and weight-loss supplement ingredients, I strongly support the Food and Drug Administration's (FDA) proposed suite of new compliance requirements outlined in FDA-2024-XXXX. These revisions to reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards are critical and long overdue steps towards modernizing and standardizing the oversight of an industry that, despite its significant public health implications, has operated with persistent evidentiary gaps. My comments are rooted in extensive peer-reviewed research documenting the widespread adulteration of dietary supplements and the profound challenges independent scientists face in obtaining the basic data necessary for rigorous safety evaluation and effective post-market surveillance.

[BACKGROUND]
The FDA's proposal to revise existing reporting obligations, establish specific implementation timelines, and introduce more rigorous documentation standards directly addresses perceived gaps in current data collection and record-keeping practices. From the perspective of an academic researcher, these gaps are not merely "perceived" but are fundamental barriers to understanding and mitigating the risks associated with a market approaching $70 billion. The current regulatory framework, particularly regarding adverse event reporting and new dietary ingredient (NDI) notifications, produces data that is too sparse, inconsistently formatted, and infrequently submitted to enable meaningful safety surveillance or to support robust pre-market assessment. This has created an environment where adulterated products can persist on the market, often for years, before sufficient evidence accumulates to trigger regulatory action. My research has repeatedly demonstrated that these evidentiary deficits force independent scientists to undertake costly, time-consuming, and retrospective investigations, thereby extending publication timelines and delaying crucial public health warnings.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
My laboratory's peer-reviewed safety evaluations of supplement ingredients are routinely constrained by the absence of systematic, standardized industry-reported data. When manufacturers are not required to report ingredient-level safety signals in a consistent format or on a regular schedule, I must independently source, purchase, and chemically analyze commercial products to establish facts about composition and dosage that manufacturers already know. This creates duplicative costs, extends publication timelines, and means my findings are always retrospective—published after consumers have already been exposed to potentially unsafe ingredients [1, 2].

The core challenge in my research on stimulant supplement adulteration is that adverse event reports filed under the current system typically reference only the labeled product, not its actual chemical composition. When a consumer is hospitalized after taking a weight-loss supplement adulterated with an undisclosed stimulant—a scenario my published research has documented repeatedly—the adverse event report filed by the manufacturer references the label, not the adulterant [4, 5]. This means the agency's revised reporting obligations, however well-intentioned, will continue to generate systematically misleading safety data unless documentation standards explicitly require manufacturers to report known discrepancies between labeled and actual composition. The current system's gaps mean that by the time sufficient adverse event data accumulates to confirm a hazard I have already identified analytically, consumers have been exposed for months or years.

My research on supplement adulteration also routinely encounters situations where manufacturer records are incomplete, inconsistent, or formatted in ways that resist systematic comparison. When I attempt to correlate adverse event reports with specific product lots or formulations, the absence of standardized documentation forces my team to spend significant time reconstructing records from disparate sources, delaying publication timelines that are already constrained by peer-review cycles and limited grant funding [2, 3]. Standardized documentation would allow my research to more efficiently identify patterns of adulteration across products and manufacturers, strengthening the evidentiary basis for regulatory action.

Furthermore, my research on stimulant and weight-loss supplements has repeatedly identified ingredients that entered the market through NDI notifications or without any notification at all, yet for which no publicly accessible safety dossier exists in a usable format [1]. The FDA's 2024 final guidance on NDI notification procedures and timeframes (89 FR 16003) updated procedural requirements but did not mandate that the substantive safety data within those notifications be made available in a structured, searchable format. This means that even when manufacturers comply with notification requirements, the resulting data is not operationally useful for independent safety researchers. My peer-review publication timelines are extended by months when I must file Freedom of Information Act requests or reconstruct safety profiles from scattered sources rather than accessing a centralized, standardized repository.

My laboratory has published peer-reviewed analyses identifying undisclosed stimulants, controlled substances, and novel psychoactive compounds in weight-loss and sports supplements. Under current reporting structures, these findings enter the regulatory record inconsistently and without a standardized pathway [7]. The proposed revised reporting obligations create an opportunity to formalize how independent academic safety data is submitted to and used by the agency. Without explicit recognition of academic submissions, my research — and that of similarly situated investigators — risks being deprioritized relative to industry-sponsored data, even when our findings directly document consumer harm. This asymmetry undermines the very safety monitoring goals the agency states it is trying to achieve.

In direct response to the agency's request for data on expected costs and operational impacts, I support the proposed documentation standards and assert that the compliance costs to responsible manufacturers are modest relative to the public health benefits. However, my published research has repeatedly demonstrated that the supplements most likely to be adulterated—particularly those marketed for weight loss and athletic performance—are often produced by manufacturers who already disregard existing 21 CFR Part 111 documentation requirements [2, 3]. New documentation standards, if enforced only through paper compliance rather than active verification, will impose costs on legitimate manufacturers without meaningfully reducing adulteration. This dynamic undermines the research environment I operate in: when adulterated products remain on the market despite ostensibly stricter rules, it erodes the credibility of regulatory science and makes it harder to publish findings that drive policy change.

My laboratory's research on stimulant and weight-loss supplement adulteration operates on peer-review timelines that routinely span 6–12 months from submission to publication—and often longer for high-impact journals where scrutiny of supplement safety findings is intense due to industry opposition [8, 9]. If the agency's implementation timelines require industry compliance before my peer-reviewed findings on specific ingredients can be formally published and entered into the regulatory record, the agency will be locking in standards based on incomplete safety evidence. For example, if a new documentation standard for a stimulant ingredient becomes effective 12 months after the rule's finalization, but my peer-reviewed analysis identifying that ingredient's adulteration risk is still under review at month 10, the agency and regulated entities will have no opportunity to incorporate that evidence before compliance is required.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To ensure the revised reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards achieve their stated goals of improving safety oversight and enabling more robust pre-market assessment and post-market enforcement against adulteration, I offer the following specific recommendations:

**Regarding Revised Reporting Obligations:**
*   **Ingredient-Level Specificity and Machine-Readable Format:** The agency should ensure that the revised reporting obligations require ingredient-level specificity in submissions—not merely product-level disclosures—and that submitted data be made available in a structured, machine-readable format accessible to academic researchers through a public or researcher-credentialed database. This would allow independent safety evaluators to cross-reference industry-reported data against adverse event signals without duplicating analytical work [1].
*   **Enhanced Adverse Event Reporting for High-Risk Categories:** The agency should require that adverse event reports for stimulant-containing and weight-loss dietary supplements include structured, machine-readable fields capturing: (1) the specific product name and lot number, (2) all labeled and detected ingredients where known, (3) the nature and severity of the adverse event using standardized MedDRA terminology, and (4) whether the product was independently tested. The agency should also increase submission frequency from the current 15-business-day window for serious adverse events to include mandatory quarterly aggregate summary reports for product categories with known stimulant ingredients. This would allow researchers and the agency to detect clustering of adverse events around specific product lines far earlier than the current episodic reporting model permits [4, 5].
*   **Dedicated Pathway for Academic Safety Evaluations:** The agency should establish a dedicated submission pathway within the revised reporting framework for peer-reviewed academic safety evaluations of supplement ingredients, including a standardized format for submitting analytical findings, adverse event correlations, and ingredient identification data. This pathway should carry explicit evidentiary weight in the agency's safety review process, comparable to how FDA's own External Dietary Supplement Toolkit (EDT) underwent external peer review per Information Quality Act standards before being publicly released [7].
*   **Timely Disclosure of NDI Safety Data:** The agency should use the revised reporting obligations to establish a requirement that safety data submitted as part of NDI notifications—and any revised periodic safety reports—be formatted according to a standardized data schema and deposited into a publicly accessible repository within a defined timeframe after submission. A viable alternative to a full public database would be a researcher-credentialed access portal, similar to models used in pharmaceutical post-market surveillance, which would balance transparency with any legitimate confidential business information concerns [1].

**Regarding Documentation Standards:**
*   **Mandatory Ingredient Identity and Purity Records:** The agency should adopt documentation standards that explicitly require manufacturers to record: (1) the identity and concentration of every active ingredient per lot, using scientifically validated analytical methods as described under 21 CFR § 111.73; (2) chain-of-custody records for raw ingredients traceable to country of origin; and (3) quality control sign-off documentation that is machine-readable and exportable for regulatory review [2, 3].
*   **Disclosure of Adulterated/Undisclosed Ingredients at AE Reporting:** The agency should amend the proposed documentation standards to require that adverse event reports for stimulant-containing and weight-loss supplements include a certification by the responsible party regarding whether the reported product has undergone third-party compositional testing, and if so, the results. Where the agency has conducted its own laboratory analysis of a product associated with adverse events, it should be required to annotate the corresponding adverse event records in its database. The agency should also consider requiring manufacturers in these high-risk categories to submit certificate-of-analysis documentation as part of their revised reporting obligations, creating a contemporaneous record that can be compared against adverse event reports [4, 5].
*   **Risk-Tiered Enforcement and Electronic Submission:** The agency should pair the new documentation standards with a risk-tiered enforcement mechanism that directs audit resources toward product categories with demonstrated adulteration histories—specifically stimulant-containing, weight-loss, and bodybuilding supplements. The agency should also require that documentation be submitted electronically in a standardized format at the time of product listing, not merely maintained on-site, so that regulators and authorized researchers can conduct real-time post-market surveillance rather than relying solely on reactive inspections [2, 3].

**Regarding Implementation Timelines:**
*   **Synchronization with Academic Peer-Review Cycles:** The agency should (1) build a minimum 18-month implementation window into the timelines for documentation and reporting standards affecting ingredient safety classifications, explicitly to allow peer-reviewed independent research to enter the public record before compliance obligations crystallize; (2) establish a formal 'rolling evidence intake' mechanism during the implementation period, analogous to a living docket, so that peer-reviewed studies published after the rule's finalization but before the compliance deadline can be formally considered and, where warranted, trigger targeted amendments to the standards without restarting the full rulemaking process; and (3) commit to publishing a summary of newly available peer-reviewed evidence at the midpoint of each implementation window so that regulated entities can make informed compliance decisions [8, 9].

[CONCLUSION]
In conclusion, the proposed revisions to reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards represent a vital and necessary step towards modernizing dietary supplement oversight. As an academic researcher, I have witnessed firsthand the public health consequences of regulatory gaps that permit adulterated and dangerous products to reach consumers. By incorporating the recommendations outlined above, the FDA can leverage the proposed rule to create a more robust evidentiary framework that supports effective pre-market assessment, strengthens post-market enforcement, and ultimately protects public health. Integrating independent academic research and requiring more transparent, standardized data submissions will not only enhance the agency's oversight capacity but also reduce the operational burden on researchers who currently must expend significant resources to establish basic safety facts that industry already possesses.

[Signature removed for privacy]
Dr. Pieter Cohen
Dietary Supplement Researcher

### References
[1] https://www.fda.gov/food/guidance-regulation-food-and-dietary-supplements/guidance-documents-regulatory-information-topic-food-and-dietary-supplements
