{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
As a Type 1 diabetes patient who has depended on continuous glucose monitors (CGMs) and insulin pumps since childhood, I write to express my strong support for the agency's proposed suite of new compliance requirements, specifically the revisions to reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards. These changes have the potential to modernize and standardize the oversight of the regulated industry, which is critical for ensuring the safety, accessibility, and affordability of life-sustaining diabetes devices and therapies. My personal experience navigating fragmented regulatory and payer landscapes has shown me firsthand how inconsistent data collection and record-keeping practices contribute to delays in care, coverage gaps, and unnecessary patient burdens. I urge the agency to design these new requirements in a way that explicitly prioritizes patient outcomes, addresses the insulin cost burden, and accelerates the adoption of device interoperability standards.

[BACKGROUND]
The agency's proposal outlines core regulatory changes focusing on three primary areas: "revised reporting obligations," the establishment of specific "implementation timelines" for new mandates, and the introduction of more rigorous "documentation standards." These provisions aim to improve the quality of information the agency receives, thereby enhancing its ability to monitor safety and compliance. As a patient, I see these as vital steps toward creating a more transparent and accountable system that can better respond to the real-world challenges faced by individuals managing complex chronic conditions like diabetes.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
My support for these proposed changes is grounded in the direct impact they could have on my daily life and the lives of millions of other insulin-dependent individuals.

**Documentation Standards:**
I currently navigate a fragmented and burdensome documentation landscape every time I seek coverage for my CGM or insulin pump supplies. Medicare's existing CGM documentation checklist (effective April 2023) already requires Standard Written Orders containing specific clinical elements, physician attestations, and medical necessity justifications [2]. My insurer similarly requires Treatment Authorization Requests documenting condition severity, glucose testing frequency, and clinical rationale [1]. When documentation standards are inconsistent across payers and manufacturers, my care is delayed — I have experienced gaps in CGM sensor coverage lasting weeks because a physician's note did not use the precise language a payer required. Standardized documentation requirements, if well-designed, would reduce these gaps and create a clearer evidentiary record that supports both my safety and my access to devices the American Diabetes Association (ADA) 2023 and 2024 Standards of Care now recommend for a broad population of insulin-dependent patients, including those on basal-only regimens [5, 8, 9].

However, I also recognize a critical unintended consequence: overly prescriptive or burdensome documentation requirements could slow the regulatory review of next-generation CGM and insulin pump innovations, ultimately delaying patient access to safer, more effective devices and deepening existing health equity disparities in diabetes technology access. As a patient advocate and long-term CGM and insulin pump user, I have witnessed how regulatory documentation bottlenecks translate directly into delayed device approvals and prolonged insurance non-coverage periods. The insulin delivery device market is growing rapidly — projected to reach $43.11 billion by 2033 — driven by smart pumps, automated insulin delivery systems, and integrated CGM platforms [4]. If new documentation standards impose record-keeping formats that are incompatible with the data architectures of these emerging systems, manufacturers may face compliance costs that slow innovation pipelines or concentrate market power among large incumbents who can absorb compliance overhead, leaving patients with fewer choices and higher prices. Research on digital health equity further demonstrates that technology access disparities already disadvantage lower-income and minority diabetes patients; documentation-driven delays in device approvals would compound these inequities [6].

**Revised Reporting Obligations:**
My CGM generates a glucose reading every five minutes — roughly 288 data points per day — and my insulin pump logs every bolus, basal rate adjustment, and alarm event. If the agency's revised reporting obligations impose a rigid, low-frequency submission format (e.g., monthly or quarterly batch reporting) designed for traditional industry compliance, it will be fundamentally misaligned with the real-time, continuous nature of diabetes management data. Conversely, if the agency mandates overly granular, high-frequency submissions without standardized interoperable formats, it will create an enormous operational burden on device manufacturers, potentially slowing the software updates and interoperability improvements that patients like me depend on for safety. I currently face device interoperability limitations — my CGM and pump do not always communicate seamlessly with my care team's electronic health record — and poorly designed reporting mandates could entrench these silos rather than resolve them.

In my volunteer advocacy work, I regularly help other Type 1 diabetes patients navigate insurance denials for CGMs and insulin pumps, and I track patterns in coverage gaps and affordability crises. Currently, the data I collect informally — through patient stories, helpline contacts, and case tracking — has no standardized pathway into the regulatory record. Revised reporting obligations that require regulated entities (insurers, manufacturers, pharmacy benefit managers) to submit more frequent, detailed, and standardized data on patient access issues would validate and amplify what patient advocates observe on the ground. The Patient Advocate Foundation's 2024 Annual Impact Report documents that case managers helped 12,275 patients address 20,623 distinct issues in a single year, securing over $17 million in debt relief — evidence that the current system generates enormous, documentable patient harm that improved reporting could help the agency quantify and address [7].

**Implementation Timelines:**
I currently use a CGM and insulin pump, both of which require seamless integration with affordable insulin access. When my insurer delayed coverage updates for my CGM supplies due to vague 'transition period' justifications, I faced months of out-of-pocket costs exceeding $400/month. Vague or extended implementation timelines give insurers and PBMs the same cover to defer compliance with affordability-related mandates, directly widening the coverage gaps I experience. Without firm, enforceable milestones, the compliance schedule becomes a roadmap for delay rather than reform [3]. My insulin pump and CGM are only effective when used together, yet my insurer has repeatedly treated them as separate, unrelated product categories subject to different prior authorization processes and coverage tiers. This artificial separation is sustained in part by the absence of standardized reporting requirements that would force insurers to document how they adjudicate integrated diabetes device claims. The proposed documentation standards, if applied to device coverage decisions, could for the first time create an auditable record of insurer behavior that I and other advocates could use to challenge unjustified denials.

The ADA's 2024 Standards of Care introduced multiple individualized, patient-centered updates — including revised insulin therapy recommendations applicable at any stage of disease progression regardless of other medications — that depend on robust real-world data to validate and refine [5]. As a patient who has used insulin therapy since childhood, I have a direct stake in ensuring that the data infrastructure supporting these guideline updates is sound. However, I have experienced firsthand how device interoperability limitations — my CGM data does not automatically populate my endocrinologist's EHR — create gaps in the longitudinal record that undermine both my care and the research base. If the agency sets implementation timelines that are too compressed, manufacturers will implement workarounds rather than genuine interoperability solutions, perpetuating the data silos that harm patients.

**Connecting to Biosimilar Approval Pathways:**
While the proposed rule does not directly address biosimilar approval pathways, I believe that improved reporting and documentation standards, particularly around real-world evidence and patient outcomes, will create a more robust data environment that *enables* faster, more transparent, and safer biosimilar approval pathways. By modernizing and standardizing oversight, the agency can reduce the data gaps and inconsistencies that currently complicate the evaluation of biosimilar efficacy and safety. This would ultimately benefit patients by expanding access to more affordable insulin options, a critical concern given the ongoing burden of insulin costs.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To ensure the agency's proposed compliance requirements achieve their intended benefits for patients while mitigating potential harms, I offer the following specific recommendations:

1.  **For Documentation Standards:**
    *   Explicitly align new documentation standards with the clinical terminology and data elements already established in the ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2023/2024) [5, 8, 9] and existing Medicare CGM documentation checklists [2], so that a single compliant record satisfies multiple regulatory and payer requirements rather than multiplying paperwork.
    *   Require that documentation standards be machine-readable and interoperable with CGM and insulin pump data platforms, so that device-generated glucose data (time-in-range, hypoglycemia events, pump delivery logs) can be incorporated directly into regulatory records without manual transcription.
    *   Include an explicit patient-impact assessment requirement, mandating that manufacturers and providers demonstrate how their documentation practices affect patient access timelines and coverage continuity.
    *   Adopt a tiered documentation standard that scales requirements to device risk classification, avoiding one-size-fits-all mandates that impose enterprise-level compliance costs on smaller innovators developing interoperability solutions [1, 2].
    *   Create a safe harbor for documentation submitted in standardized digital formats (e.g., HL7 FHIR-compliant data exports from CGM platforms), recognizing that device-generated data is often more accurate and complete than manually compiled records.
    *   Conduct a formal health equity impact analysis before finalizing standards, assessing whether compliance costs will reduce the diversity of available devices and disproportionately harm underserved patient populations who already face access barriers.

2.  **For Revised Reporting Obligations:**
    *   Explicitly require that revised reporting submissions include patient-facing outcome metrics — such as rates of prior authorization denials for diabetes devices, average time-to-coverage decisions, and out-of-pocket cost data broken down by device type and insulin formulation [7].
    *   Ensure these data points are submitted at least quarterly and made publicly accessible in machine-readable formats so that patient advocacy volunteers and organizations can cross-reference regulatory submissions against lived patient experience.
    *   Establish a tiered reporting framework that distinguishes between (1) real-time safety signal reporting for device malfunctions or adverse events, (2) standardized periodic aggregate data submissions aligned with clinical care cycles (e.g., 90-day A1C-equivalent reporting windows consistent with ADA Standards of Care) [5], and (3) interoperability-enabling data format standards (such as FHIR-based open APIs) that allow patient data to flow across devices and care settings without proprietary lock-in.

3.  **For Implementation Timelines:**
    *   Structure the implementation timeline with hard interim checkpoints—at 6, 12, and 24 months—at which regulated entities must submit documented evidence of specific affordability and coverage actions taken (e.g., updated formulary placements, reduced prior authorization burdens for insulin and diabetes devices) [3, 4].
    *   Explicitly prohibit blanket 'transition period' waivers that allow entities to defer patient-facing obligations while completing back-office reporting changes. Compliance with documentation standards should not be accepted as a substitute for substantive operational change.
    *   Include within its implementation timeline a specific requirement that regulated entities report, on a standardized form, all coverage determinations for insulin delivery devices (pumps, CGMs, and associated supplies) as an integrated diabetes management system—not as discrete, unrelated products. This reporting requirement should be phased in at the 12-month milestone.
    *   Adopt a phased implementation schedule: Phase 1 (12 months) — manufacturers must demonstrate data format compatibility with at least one open interoperability standard (e.g., HL7 FHIR); Phase 2 (24 months) — full reporting obligation compliance with standardized submission formats; Phase 3 (36 months) — agency review of data quality outcomes with patient-representative input before any additional documentation requirements take effect. This phased approach mirrors the iterative, evidence-based model used by the ADA in its annual Standards of Care updates and would allow the agency to course-correct based on real-world implementation experience rather than locking in requirements that may prove unworkable.

[CONCLUSION]
In conclusion, I strongly support the agency's goals of modernizing and standardizing regulatory oversight through revised reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards. These changes are essential for enhancing patient safety, improving access to critical diabetes technologies, and fostering a more transparent and accountable healthcare system. However, the true success of this proposal will depend on its design and implementation — specifically, how well it addresses the practical realities of patient care, mitigates affordability barriers, and promotes device interoperability. I urge the agency to adopt the patient-centric recommendations outlined above, ensuring that the compliance schedule becomes a roadmap for meaningful reform rather than a mechanism for delay or added burden on the patients who depend on these regulations for their health and well-being.

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
Corner Pharmacy of Greenville is an independent, locally-owned community pharmacy serving a mixed urban-rural catchment area. We provide essential healthcare services, including prescription dispensing, medication therapy management (MTM), and specialized compounding services for local physicians, often serving as the sole accessible healthcare provider for many of our patients. We are deeply concerned that the proposed suite of new compliance requirements, focusing on revised reporting obligations, specific implementation timelines, and more rigorous documentation standards, fails to adequately consider the disproportionate operational and financial burden these changes will impose on small, independent pharmacies like ours. Without significant modifications, these proposals risk undermining patient access to critical pharmacy services, particularly in underserved communities, and accelerating the closure of independent pharmacies.

[BACKGROUND]
The agency's proposal seeks to modernize and standardize oversight by revising existing reporting obligations, establishing specific implementation timelines for new mandates, and introducing more rigorous documentation standards. While we recognize the agency's goal of improving data quality and enhancing monitoring of safety and compliance, we believe the current proposal does not sufficiently balance this goal with the practical realities of industry operations, especially for small entities. Specifically, the revised reporting obligations, new documentation standards, and proposed implementation timelines will significantly impact our medication therapy management (MTM) programs, compounding services, and overall administrative processes.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
Corner Pharmacy of Greenville operates with three pharmacists and four technicians, all of whom are engaged in direct patient care, compounding, and compliance activities. We do not have dedicated IT, data analytics, or compliance staff. This operational structure makes us particularly vulnerable to new, unfunded mandates that increase administrative overhead.

Firstly, **revised reporting obligations for MTM programs** pose a significant administrative burden. Our MTM services, including comprehensive medication reviews (CMRs) and targeted medication reviews (TMRs), are already delivered at thin margins. Research published in the pharmacy economics literature shows that at a typical reimbursement rate of $50 per encounter, a pharmacy must deliver 43 patient encounters per year just to break even on MTM service delivery [7]. Any increase in administrative overhead—such as more frequent data submissions, reformatted reporting templates, or expanded documentation fields—directly erodes that margin by consuming pharmacist time that would otherwise be spent on billable patient care. If our pharmacists must redirect hours toward compliance reporting, we face a binary choice: reduce the number of MTM encounters we offer, or absorb unsustainable losses. Either outcome harms patients, particularly elderly Medicare beneficiaries managing multiple chronic conditions who depend on pharmacist engagement [1]. CMS’s own National Impact Assessment documents that CMR completion rates improved dramatically from 19.1% to 40.1% in Medicare FFS and from 61.3% to 83.3% in Medicare Advantage between 2016 and 2020, driven by pharmacist engagement [1]. If revised reporting obligations require our pharmacists to spend significantly more time on documentation and submission compliance, CMR completion rates at independent pharmacies like ours will decline—reversing hard-won public health gains.

Secondly, **new documentation standards for compounding services** create significant compliance costs and potential operational unworkability. Our compounding operation for local physicians already requires us to maintain records compliant with USP <795> (non-sterile compounding), applicable state pharmacy board rules, and PBM credentialing documentation requirements. South Carolina, like many states, has been actively amending its compounding requirements—a trend confirmed across multiple states in 2024 and continuing into 2025 [4], [6]. Each new layer of documentation obligation requires our pharmacists to reconcile what must be recorded, in what format, and for how long, across multiple regulatory authorities simultaneously. If the agency's new documentation standards require record presentation formats or data fields that differ from what our state board or USP standards already mandate, we will be forced to maintain parallel record sets—doubling administrative burden without improving safety outcomes. For a small operation where pharmacists are already responsible for dispensing, compounding, medication therapy management, and compliance, this is an immediate operational threat to our ability to continue offering compounding services. We estimate that reformatting our existing compounding records to meet new presentation standards would require approximately 200-300 hours of pharmacist and technician time in the first year alone, at a cost of $15,000–$25,000, which is not recoverable through current reimbursement structures [5].

Thirdly, **specific implementation timelines** for transitioning internal administrative processes and data management systems impose a disproportionate and potentially insurmountable operational burden on small independent pharmacies. Transitioning our internal administrative and data management systems to meet new documentation and reporting standards within the proposed timeline would require us to simultaneously: (1) procure and implement new or upgraded pharmacy management software capable of meeting the new data standards; (2) retrain all staff on revised workflows; and (3) maintain uninterrupted dispensing and compounding services for our patient community. We estimate this transition would require a minimum of 6–12 months of parallel system operation, vendor negotiation, and staff retraining—time the proposed schedule does not appear to accommodate. Compounding this pressure, we are already absorbing significant compliance costs related to USP compounding standards and facing severe PBM reimbursement rate compression, leaving us with extremely limited financial and human capital to absorb a rapid systems overhaul [3], [6].

Finally, the **cumulative regulatory burden** created by these proposals, combined with existing PBM reimbursement pressures and compounding compliance costs, poses an existential threat to small independent pharmacies and risks accelerating the loss of patient access in underserved communities. Our pharmacy serves a mixed urban-rural catchment area in Greenville where we are one of two independent pharmacies within a 12-mile radius. Many of our patients are elderly, Medicaid-enrolled, or have limited transportation. If compliance costs from revised reporting obligations and documentation standards force us to reduce hours, eliminate compounding services, or ultimately close, these patients will face significantly longer travel distances to access prescription medications and the face-to-face counseling that our pharmacists provide. This is not a hypothetical: the 2024 NCPA Digest documents the loss of 448 independent pharmacy locations in a single year. A Virginia legislative study on pharmacy access confirms that pharmacy closures disproportionately affect rural and underserved communities. The agency’s stated goal of improving data quality will be self-defeating if the reporting burden it creates eliminates the community pharmacies whose dispensing data it seeks to collect [2], [3], [5].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To address these critical concerns and ensure that regulatory modernization does not inadvertently compromise patient access and the viability of small independent pharmacies, we urge the agency to consider the following recommendations:

1.  **For Revised Reporting Obligations (MTM & General)**:
    *   **Establish a Small Pharmacy Exemption or Simplified Pathway**: For pharmacies with fewer than 10 FTE staff, allow annual rather than more frequent data submissions for MTM providers below a defined volume threshold. Provide standardized, pre-built reporting templates at no cost to reduce the technical burden of format compliance [1].
    *   **Conduct a Formal Small Business Impact Analysis**: Before finalizing any format or content changes, conduct a formal Small Business Impact Analysis specifically disaggregating independent community pharmacies from chain pharmacies and PBMs [2], [3].
    *   **Provide Technical Assistance and Financial Support**: Offer direct technical assistance grants or subsidized software tools to small pharmacies to offset transition costs, similar to the meaningful use incentive structure used during EHR adoption.

2.  **For Documentation Standards (Compounding)**:
    *   **Harmonize with Existing Standards**: Conduct a formal harmonization review—specifically mapping the proposed requirements against existing USP <795>, <797>, and <800> standards, state pharmacy board record-keeping rules, and PCAB accreditation documentation requirements. Where the agency's proposed standards overlap with existing requirements, the final rule should explicitly state that compliance with the existing standard satisfies the federal requirement [4], [6].
    *   **Adopt Tiered Documentation Requirements**: Establish a tiered documentation presentation framework that distinguishes between high-volume 503B outsourcing facilities and small-volume 503A community pharmacies like ours. Allow small pharmacies compounding fewer than a defined threshold of preparations per month to use simplified record presentation formats and provide model templates [5].
    *   **Extend Implementation Timeline**: Extend the implementation timeline for small pharmacies by at least 24 months to allow for phased adoption.

3.  **For Implementation Timelines (General)**:
    *   **Establish a Tiered Implementation Schedule**: Explicitly grant small businesses—defined by SBA size standards—a minimum 24-month compliance window from the rule's effective date, compared to a shorter window for large entities [8], [9].
    *   **Convene a Small Business Technical Assistance Program**: Help independent pharmacies assess system readiness and identify low-cost compliant solutions before the deadline arrives.

4.  **Overarching Principles**:
    *   **Conduct a Cumulative Burden Analysis**: Include a cumulative burden analysis that accounts for the interaction of these new reporting obligations with existing MTM program requirements, PBM market dynamics, and compounding oversight costs [2], [3], [5].
    *   **Recognize Community Pharmacy Access Role**: Explicitly assess pharmacy access impacts as part of the regulatory impact analysis, including mapping proposed reporting burden against pharmacy desert designations. Adopt a tiered compliance framework that scales reporting obligations to pharmacy size and dispensing volume, exempting or simplifying requirements for pharmacies below a defined threshold [2], [3], [5].

[CONCLUSION]
Corner Pharmacy of Greenville firmly believes that regulatory modernization must be implemented in a manner that is proportional to the size and capacity of regulated entities. The proposed suite of compliance requirements, while well-intentioned, risks creating an insurmountable operational and financial burden for small independent pharmacies, ultimately undermining patient access to essential pharmacy services in communities that depend on us. We urge the agency to adopt a more nuanced approach, incorporating tiered compliance pathways, extended implementation timelines, and robust technical assistance programs to ensure that the pursuit of data quality does not come at the cost of community pharmacy viability and patient care. We stand ready to collaborate with the agency to develop practical and effective solutions that achieve regulatory goals without jeopardizing the critical role independent pharmacies play in the nation's healthcare system.

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The California Department of Public Health (CDPH) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the proposed rule FDA-2024-XXXX, which seeks to modernize and standardize oversight of regulated industry through revised reporting obligations, specific implementation timelines, and more rigorous documentation standards. As the primary agency responsible for protecting the health and well-being of California's diverse population of over 39 million residents, CDPH strongly supports the agency's goal of improving the quality and consistency of data collection and record-keeping. These efforts are critical for enhancing public health monitoring, compliance assurance, and effective regulatory coordination between federal and state agencies, particularly in the context of California's comprehensive Proposition 65 chemical safety program.

[BACKGROUND]
CDPH administers numerous public health initiatives, including the implementation and enforcement of California's Safe Drinking Water and Toxic Enforcement Act of 1986, commonly known as Proposition 65. Proposition 65 requires the state to publish a list of chemicals known to cause cancer or reproductive toxicity and mandates warnings for exposures to these chemicals. Effective enforcement of Proposition 65 relies heavily on access to timely, accurate, and comprehensive data regarding chemical use, exposure pathways, and compliance status from regulated entities.

The proposed rule's focus on three core regulatory changes—the revision of existing reporting obligations, the establishment of specific implementation timelines, and the introduction of new documentation standards—directly impacts CDPH's ability to effectively manage Proposition 65 chemical safety programs. Currently, inconsistencies in federal reporting formats, frequencies, and content create significant reconciliation burdens for CDPH staff, diverting resources from direct public health protection. Modernized federal requirements that are harmonized with California's data infrastructure would significantly enhance our operational efficiency and public health responsiveness.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]

**Revised Reporting Obligations:**
CDPH strongly supports the proposed revision of existing reporting obligations that will change the frequency, format, or content of data submissions required from regulated entities. Standardized, higher-frequency data submissions are essential to strengthen our ability to coordinate Proposition 65 chemical safety implementation with federal oversight frameworks, closing critical data gaps that have historically limited our enforcement capacity. CDPH administers Proposition 65 chemical safety programs across a population of over 39 million Californians, requiring continuous monitoring of chemical exposures across diverse demographic and geographic contexts. Currently, inconsistent federal reporting formats and submission frequencies create significant reconciliation burdens when CDPH attempts to cross-reference federal chemical data against California's Proposition 65 chemical list—which includes over 900 listed substances. The 2024 amendments to Proposition 65 warning requirements (effective January 1, 2025, with a three-year phase-in) have already increased the volume and complexity of compliance data CDPH must process [2, 3]. Revised federal reporting obligations that standardize format and increase submission frequency would reduce the manual data-matching labor our staff currently performs, freeing resources for direct enforcement and public health outreach. A 2024 peer-reviewed study in Environmental Health Perspectives demonstrated measurable reductions in biomonitored chemical exposures in California following Proposition 65 implementation, validating that robust data-driven enforcement produces real public health outcomes—outcomes that depend on timely, high-quality data inputs [3].

CDPH also strongly supports the proposed revision of data submission content as a necessary modernization measure that will improve chemical safety monitoring, and specifically endorses content requirements that capture more granular exposure pathway and population-level data. California's Proposition 65 enforcement depends on precisely the kind of detailed, standardized chemical exposure data that current federal submissions fail to provide consistently. In responding to the agency's specific request for comments on operational impacts: CDPH's Proposition 65 program currently operates with significant data gaps because federal chemical safety submissions often lack the exposure pathway specificity, demographic disaggregation, and dose-response detail that California's stricter standards require. When CDPH must independently generate or supplement this data, it diverts staff resources from enforcement and public notification activities. The CDC's Public Health Data Strategy Year-End Lookback Report demonstrates that modernizing electronic case and chemical reporting—including automating data flows from regulated entities to public health authorities—has "markedly decreased the reporting workload" for public health agencies. CDPH would experience analogous operational benefits if revised federal submission content requirements produce richer, more actionable chemical safety data that can be directly ingested into Proposition 65 assessment workflows [4, 5].

In direct response to the agency's request for comments on expected costs and operational impacts, CDPH notes that revised reporting obligations impose two categories of costs on our operations that the agency may not have fully accounted for. First, there are system integration costs: CDPH's data systems must be updated to ingest new formats and frequencies, requiring IT investment at a time when our resources are already strained by the concurrent implementation of the 2025 Proposition 65 warning amendments. Second, there is a transition-period data gap risk: if regulated entities shift to new reporting formats before state systems are updated to receive them, or if historical data is not migrated into the new format, CDPH will face a period of degraded data availability. This is not hypothetical—the three-year phase-in for the 2025 Proposition 65 short-form warning amendments [2, 3] was specifically designed to prevent exactly this kind of compliance discontinuity at the state level. A similar phased approach is essential here. California's often-stricter chemical safety standards mean we cannot afford enforcement gaps that a poorly managed federal reporting transition could create.

**Specific Implementation Timelines:**
The California Department of Public Health strongly supports the proposed implementation timelines and urges the agency to maintain a structured, phased schedule, but recommends that the timeline explicitly account for the resource allocation challenges faced by state public health agencies serving large, geographically and demographically diverse populations such as California's 39+ million residents. CDPH is responsible for implementing Proposition 65 chemical safety standards across a vast and diverse population spanning urban centers, rural agricultural communities, and everything in between. A uniform, single-phase implementation timeline would strain CDPH's capacity to simultaneously train staff, update IT systems, coordinate with local health departments, and communicate new compliance requirements to regulated entities across this heterogeneous landscape. The agency's federal-state regulatory coordination requirements further complicate rapid transitions, as CDPH must align new federal standards with California's often-stricter Proposition 65 thresholds—a reconciliation process that itself requires dedicated staff time and legal review before any outward-facing compliance work can begin [6, 7].

**New Documentation Standards:**
CDPH supports the proposed documentation standards but urges the agency to carefully calibrate compliance cost estimates for regulated entities, as underestimating these costs risks generating industry opposition that could delay implementation and undermine the public health benefits California depends upon. The agency's cost estimates must be realistic and transparent to ensure the rule survives legal and political challenge and to allow CDPH to plan for the state-level technical assistance burden that will accompany any new federal documentation mandate. CDPH's Proposition 65 implementation experience demonstrates that when compliance cost estimates are perceived as unrealistically low, regulated industries mount sustained legal and administrative challenges that delay rule effectiveness. As documented in the paint industry's response to OEHHA's short-form warning amendments, industry cost estimates can exceed agency estimates by orders of magnitude—one American Coatings Association member estimated $800,000 for 500 products versus OEHHA's estimate of $4,273 per business [1, 2]. When such disputes arise, CDPH staff are drawn into extended stakeholder engagement processes, diverting resources from direct public health work. Accurate cost estimates also allow CDPH to anticipate the volume of compliance assistance requests we will receive from smaller California businesses subject to both federal and Proposition 65 obligations simultaneously.

The California Department of Public Health (CDPH) strongly supports the proposed documentation standards as a necessary modernization of record-keeping practices, and urges the agency to design these standards with explicit federal-state interoperability in mind so that California's Proposition 65 implementation infrastructure can align without duplicative burden. CDPH administers Proposition 65, one of the nation's most comprehensive chemical safety disclosure programs, requiring continuous coordination with federal agencies on chemical listings, exposure data, and enforcement records. California's standards are frequently more stringent than federal baselines, meaning CDPH already maintains robust documentation systems. However, if the new federal documentation standards are not designed to accommodate state-level data formats and reporting architectures, CDPH will face a costly parallel compliance obligation—maintaining one documentation system for federal review and a separate system for California's own regulatory requirements. This dual-track burden would divert staff resources from direct public health protection activities across California's population of nearly 40 million residents [8, 9].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]

In response to the agency's specific requests for comments on expected costs, feasibility, and operational impacts, CDPH offers the following recommendations:

1.  **Cost Calibration and Tiered Compliance for Documentation Standards:** CDPH recommends the agency conduct independent third-party cost verification before finalizing its regulatory impact analysis, using industry-segmented cost modeling that accounts for the difference between large manufacturers and small businesses. The agency should also consider a tiered compliance timeline—with larger entities subject to earlier deadlines and small businesses receiving an extended phase-in period—to reduce the aggregate compliance burden and minimize the litigation risk that could delay the rule's public health benefits [1, 2]. This directly addresses the potential for industry opposition due to underestimated compliance costs.

2.  **Interoperable Reporting Formats and Data Sharing Protocol:** The agency should adopt reporting formats that are explicitly interoperable with California's existing Proposition 65 data infrastructure and OEHHA's chemical hazard databases. Specifically, we recommend the agency require submissions in machine-readable, structured formats (e.g., XML or JSON schemas) that align with the chemical identifiers (CAS numbers, DTXSID codes) already used in California's regulatory systems. We further recommend the agency establish a formal federal-state data-sharing protocol so that revised submissions are automatically transmitted to state health agencies, eliminating duplicative reporting burdens on regulated entities while ensuring California receives timely data to support our stricter state standards [3]. This will reduce the reconciliation burden on CDPH staff and strengthen enforcement capacity.

3.  **Aligned Content Requirements and Federal-State Coordination for Reporting:** The agency should (1) explicitly align revised data submission content requirements with established public health data interoperability standards, such as those referenced in the Improving DATA in Public Health Act (S.3545, 118th Congress), which requires content and form requirements to align with HHS-adopted standards and implementation specifications; (2) publish a crosswalk document mapping new submission content fields to California's existing Proposition 65 data intake schema so state agencies can prepare system updates in advance of the compliance deadline; and (3) establish a formal federal-state coordination mechanism—such as a technical working group—that includes state public health agencies in the content design process before requirements are finalized [4, 5]. This will prevent the proposed content changes from creating a new innovation barrier for California's regulatory systems.

4.  **Transition Period and Data Migration Guidance for Reporting Obligations:** Responding directly to the agency's questions on expected costs and alternatives: the agency should (1) provide a minimum 24-month transition period before new reporting formats become mandatory, with parallel acceptance of both old and new formats during the transition; (2) publish a comprehensive data migration guide that allows state agencies to map legacy federal data to the new schema without loss of historical comparability; and (3) establish a dedicated federal-state coordination workgroup—inclusive of CDPH and peer state health agencies—to identify and resolve interoperability issues before the new obligations take effect. The agency should also consider whether small-volume chemical manufacturers, who may lack resources to comply immediately, could be granted a tiered implementation schedule similar to the phase-in structure used in the 2025 Proposition 65 amendments [3]. This will prevent a temporary data gap that could impair Proposition 65 enforcement continuity.

5.  **Granular Exposure Data Content for Reporting:** The agency should require that revised data submissions include: (a) chemical exposure data disaggregated by population subgroup (including age, pregnancy status, and occupational exposure), consistent with the populations Proposition 65 is designed to protect; (b) standardized dose-response and NOAEL/LOAEL fields that map to California's Proposition 65 risk assessment methodology; and (c) machine-readable submission formats (e.g., structured JSON or XML schemas) rather than narrative or PDF-based reports, enabling automated ingestion by state public health data systems [4, 5]. These content enhancements would directly address the agency's stated goal of improving data quality while simultaneously reducing the operational burden on state agencies that must currently extract and reformat federal submissions manually.

6.  **Phased Implementation Timeline:** The agency should adopt a phased implementation timeline—similar in structure to the multi-year Medicaid integrated implementation calendar (2024–2030) documented in federal guidance—that allows large state agencies to sequence their internal system updates, staff training, and public outreach in discrete, manageable stages [6]. Specifically, CDPH recommends: (1) a 6-month pre-implementation period dedicated exclusively to federal-state regulatory reconciliation; (2) a tiered rollout that prioritizes highest-risk chemical categories first; and (3) explicit milestone checkpoints at which the agency solicits state-level feedback before advancing to subsequent phases [7]. This structure would preserve the integrity of the timeline while preventing resource bottlenecks that could undermine compliance quality and strain CDPH's capacity.

7.  **Federal-State Interoperability Framework for Documentation Standards:** The agency should explicitly incorporate a federal-state interoperability framework into the final documentation standards, including: (1) adoption of machine-readable, standardized data formats (such as those aligned with ONC health IT certification standards, as referenced in recent HHS alignment policy work) that can interface with existing state regulatory databases; (2) a formal safe harbor provision confirming that states meeting or exceeding the federal documentation standards through their own programs are deemed compliant without additional federal filings; and (3) a joint federal-state working group to develop technical guidance on data exchange protocols before the compliance deadline takes effect [8, 9]. This will avoid costly parallel compliance obligations for CDPH and prevent the diversion of staff resources from public health protection.

[CONCLUSION]
CDPH strongly supports the agency's efforts to modernize and standardize reporting and documentation requirements. These changes are essential for improving the quality of information available to federal and state regulators, enhancing compliance, and ultimately safeguarding public health. However, successful implementation of this rule, particularly in states with robust existing regulatory frameworks like California's Proposition 65, will depend on explicit attention to federal-state interoperability, realistic cost calibration, and flexible implementation timelines that account for the unique operational challenges faced by large, diverse state public health agencies. By incorporating CDPH's recommendations, the agency can ensure that the proposed rule achieves its intended public health benefits while minimizing unnecessary burdens on both regulated entities and state regulatory partners.

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The American Medical Association (AMA) appreciates the opportunity to comment on the Food and Drug Administration's (FDA) proposed rule, FDA-2024-XXXX, which seeks to modernize and standardize oversight of its regulated industry through revised reporting obligations, specific implementation timelines, and more rigorous documentation standards. As the nation's largest physician organization, representing over 300,000 physicians and medical students, the AMA strongly supports the agency's overarching goals to enhance patient safety through improved safety labeling, strengthen evidence-based medicine via robust clinical evidence requirements, and ensure responsible prescribing practices through effective prescriber education mandates. These objectives are fundamental to our mission of promoting the art and science of medicine and the betterment of public health.

[BACKGROUND]
The AMA recognizes that the proposed revisions to reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards are intended to address perceived gaps in current data collection and record-keeping practices. We believe that well-designed data submission and documentation frameworks are essential for advancing regulatory oversight and public health. However, the success of this proposal hinges on its practical implementation, particularly its impact on the nation's physicians who serve at the front lines of patient care. Physicians and their practices are already grappling with overwhelming administrative burdens, including complex electronic health record (EHR) documentation, prior authorization requirements, and constantly evolving regulatory mandates. It is critical that the agency design these new requirements in a manner that minimizes incremental administrative and financial strain on physician practices, which are the primary conduits for much of the data required by regulatory bodies.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**1. Impact of Revised Reporting Obligations on Safety Labeling and Clinical Evidence:**
The proposed "revised reporting obligations," which will change the frequency, format, or content of data submissions, directly intersect with the agency's goals for safety labeling improvements and clinical evidence requirements. While the AMA supports the standardization of data content for advancing evidence-based medicine standards [8, 9], we are deeply concerned about the potential for these changes to exacerbate administrative burden on physicians. AMA data from 2024 reveals that physicians average a 57.8-hour workweek, with only 27.2 hours devoted to direct patient care, and 43.2% report at least one symptom of burnout [1]. New or more frequent data submission requirements, particularly for adverse event reporting or clinical trial data, will further compress the time available for patient care, worsening burnout and threatening practice viability.

Furthermore, changes to reporting formats that are not natively compatible with major certified EHR systems will impose both direct costs (IT vendor fees, staff retraining) and indirect costs (workflow disruption, temporary productivity loss) on physician practices. The CAQH Index documents significant cost and time savings opportunities from transitioning partially electronic and manual transactions to fully electronic formats [1]. Physician practices that lack the infrastructure to submit data electronically in a new standardized format will face higher per-transaction costs and greater staff time expenditure. For multi-specialty practices and large health systems, format changes not aligned with existing EHR data architectures will require costly custom interfaces. For solo and small group practices, which represent a significant share of AMA's membership, the burden is even more acute because they cannot amortize IT development costs across a large patient volume.

**2. Impact of Documentation Standards on Clinical Evidence and Prescriber Education:**
The introduction of new "documentation standards" governing how records are created, maintained, and presented for regulatory review presents both opportunities and significant challenges related to clinical evidence and prescriber education mandates. The AMA strongly supports the proposed documentation standards as a necessary modernization that aligns regulatory requirements with evidence-based clinical documentation practices essential to patient safety [6, 7]. Standardized documentation requirements, if well-designed, would reduce duplicative record-keeping burdens, improve care continuity, and align regulatory expectations with clinical best practices.

However, without deliberate design choices to minimize physician-facing administrative burden, these new documentation requirements risk exacerbating the physician burnout crisis and reducing patient care time [2]. The AMA's own 2024 National Physician Survey found that 94% of physicians report that administrative requirements delay necessary care, and that administrative burden is a primary driver of physician burnout [5]. Adding new data management compliance obligations on top of existing EHR documentation burdens, prior authorization workflows, and continuously changing regulatory rules will further erode the time physicians spend with patients. If physicians and their staff must redirect clinical hours to compliance activities, as the NIH DMS cost analysis framework identifies as a core 'opportunity cost' [3], patient outcomes and practice sustainability will both suffer.

For prescriber education mandates, the new documentation standards will require physicians and their practices to implement systems for tracking education completion and reporting compliance. This will impose significant, quantifiable compliance costs on physician practices that are already operating under severe financial and administrative strain [3]. MGMA data from 2023 confirms that medical practice operating costs are still rising in 2025, with primary care practices in particular reporting increased staffing-driven expenses. Requiring these practices to simultaneously overhaul data management systems and administrative workflows — without targeted support — risks accelerating practice closures, consolidation into hospital systems, and reduced patient access to independent physician care. The GAO's 2025 report on health care consolidation already documents a steep decline in independent physician practices, particularly in rural areas, a trend that new compliance cost burdens will worsen.

**3. Impact of Implementation Timelines on all Requirements:**
The establishment of specific "implementation timelines" that dictate the schedule by which entities must transition to the new standards will have a profound impact across all areas of revised reporting and documentation. The AMA supports the agency's proposed implementation timelines as a necessary modernization step but strongly urges the agency to adopt a specialty-differentiated, phased timeline structure that accounts for the vastly different operational realities across physician specialties. AMA research shows that in 2025, 41.9% of physicians reported at least one symptom of burnout, with specialty-specific disparities remaining significant [5]. Specialties such as psychiatry, gastroenterology, and cardiology—which already require more than 135 days to recruit a single physician—face compounded operational constraints: they cannot rapidly hire compliance staff, retrain existing personnel, or absorb new documentation workflows without directly displacing patient care time [4]. For the 42.2% of physicians still in physician-owned private practices, there is no large administrative infrastructure to absorb transition costs. A single, agency-wide deadline treats a solo rural psychiatrist identically to a large hospital system, which is neither equitable nor operationally sound.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To ensure that the proposed rule successfully achieves its goals of improved safety labeling, robust clinical evidence requirements, and effective prescriber education mandates without imposing undue administrative burden on physicians, the AMA offers the following specific recommendations:

**A. Regarding Revised Reporting Obligations (Safety Labeling & Clinical Evidence):**
1.  **Administrative Burden Assessment:** The agency should conduct a formal physician-specific administrative burden assessment before finalizing any changes to reporting frequency or format, using the AMA's Physician Practice Benchmark Survey as a baseline [1].
2.  **EHR Compatibility:** Require that any new data submission format be natively compatible with major certified EHR systems to avoid bespoke integration costs [1].
3.  **Net-Zero Burden Principle:** Adopt a 'net-zero burden' principle whereby any new reporting obligation is offset by the elimination or consolidation of an existing one [2, 3].
4.  **Implementation Runway:** Provide a minimum 18-month implementation runway for format or frequency changes to allow EHR vendors and practices to adapt without disrupting patient care operations [1].
5.  **Standardized Electronic Formats:** Mandate that all revised reporting formats conform to existing interoperability standards (e.g., HL7 FHIR) so that data can be extracted directly from certified EHR systems without manual re-entry or custom development [8, 9].
6.  **Content Harmonization:** Explicitly harmonize the revised content requirements with existing federal health data standards, specifically the ONC's United States Core Data for Interoperability (USCDI v5) and HL7 implementation guides already approved for adverse event reporting [8, 9]. This alignment would reduce duplicative data entry burdens on physician practices while improving the quality and comparability of submitted data.

**B. Regarding Documentation Standards (Clinical Evidence & Prescriber Education):**
1.  **Clinical Workflow Integration:** Require that all new documentation standards be designed for direct integration into existing certified EHR technology (CEHRT) platforms so that data capture occurs as a byproduct of clinical workflow rather than as a separate administrative task [2, 3].
2.  **Physician Usability Advisory Panel:** Convene a physician usability advisory panel—inclusive of primary care, specialty, and rural practice representatives—to review proposed data fields and reporting formats before finalization [2].
3.  **'Administrative Burden Hours' Cap:** Establish a maximum annual 'administrative burden hours' cap per physician FTE for all new requirements combined, with a formal burden offset mechanism requiring the agency to retire or streamline existing requirements when new ones are added [2, 3].
4.  **Prohibit Physician Data Entry:** Explicitly prohibit implementation designs that require physicians themselves (rather than administrative staff or automated systems) to perform data entry for compliance reporting [2].
5.  **Alignment with Validated Clinical Frameworks:** The agency should explicitly align its required documentation formats with established, evidence-validated clinical frameworks—such as SOAP and SBAR—rather than creating novel regulatory documentation structures that conflict with existing clinical workflows and increase physician cognitive burden [6, 7].

**C. Regarding Implementation Timelines (Overall Impact):**
1.  **Specialty-Differentiated, Phased Timeline:** Replace any single uniform compliance deadline with a tiered, specialty-sensitive phased implementation schedule. Specifically:
    *   Tier 1 (large health systems and hospital-employed physicians): compliance within 12 months of the final rule.
    *   Tier 2 (mid-size group practices): compliance within 18 months.
    *   Tier 3 (solo and small physician-owned practices, and specialties with documented workforce shortages such as psychiatry, gastroenterology, and cardiology): compliance within 24–30 months, with interim safe harbor provisions [4, 5].
2.  **Small Practice Economic Impact Analysis:** Conduct a formal small-practice economic impact analysis disaggregated by practice size and specialty before finalizing implementation timelines, considering the rising medical practice operating costs [2, 3].
3.  **Financial Assistance:** Create a federal grant or subsidy program analogous to the HITECH Act's meaningful use incentives to offset IT infrastructure costs for qualifying small and independent practices [2, 3].
4.  **Standardized Technical Specifications:** Publish standardized, interoperable technical specifications so that practices can leverage existing EHR vendor relationships rather than procuring entirely new systems [2].

[CONCLUSION]
The American Medical Association strongly supports the FDA's commitment to modernizing and standardizing regulatory oversight to enhance patient safety, strengthen clinical evidence, and ensure responsible prescribing practices. We believe these goals are critically important for the health of our nation. However, the operational realities of physician practices must be carefully considered in the design and implementation of this proposed rule. By adopting the AMA's recommendations, the agency can achieve its public health objectives while mitigating the administrative burden on physicians, thereby protecting patient access to care and safeguarding the well-being of the healthcare workforce. We urge the agency to collaborate closely with the medical community to ensure that the final rule is both effective and sustainable.

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
[Date]
[Docket Number: FDA-2024-XXXX]
[Agency Contact Address]

Re: Comments on Proposed Rule: [Title of Proposed Rule]

Dear [Agency Contact or Relevant Official],

The Association for Accessible Medicines (AAM) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the proposed rule [Title of Proposed Rule]. AAM is the national trade association representing the U.S. generic and biosimilar pharmaceutical industry, committed to promoting patient access to safe, effective, and affordable medicines. Our member companies are responsible for developing, manufacturing, and distributing nearly 90% of all prescription medicines dispensed in the United States, saving patients and the healthcare system an estimated $467 billion in 2024 alone [9]. We strongly support the agency's efforts to modernize and standardize oversight of the regulated industry, particularly through the revision of reporting obligations, establishment of implementation timelines, and introduction of more rigorous documentation standards. However, we urge the agency to carefully consider the unique operational and compliance challenges faced by generic and biosimilar applicants to ensure that these new requirements do not inadvertently create barriers to patient access to lower-cost alternatives.

[BACKGROUND]
The proposed rule seeks to enhance the quality of information received by the agency, thereby improving its ability to monitor safety and compliance. The core regulatory changes focus on three primary areas: the revision of existing reporting obligations, the establishment of specific implementation timelines for new mandates, and the introduction of more rigorous documentation standards. AAM believes that while these objectives are laudable, their implementation must be carefully calibrated to avoid imposing disproportionate burdens on generic and biosimilar manufacturers, who operate within a highly competitive and complex regulatory environment. Our comments will address each of these three areas, outlining specific concerns and offering constructive recommendations to ensure that the final rule supports, rather than hinders, the timely approval and market entry of affordable medicines.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
AAM’s analysis of the proposed rule focuses on several critical intersections with the generic and biosimilar approval pathways and the broader competitive landscape.

**Revised Reporting Obligations:**
AAM strongly supports revised reporting obligations that create greater transparency around REMS program administration and shared system negotiations, which are frequently misused to delay generic market entry. Brand-name manufacturers routinely exploit REMS with Elements to Assure Safe Use (ETASU) as a pretext to deny generic applicants access to reference listed drug (RLD) samples necessary for bioequivalence testing, and to refuse shared REMS system negotiations on commercially unreasonable terms [3, 4]. Without standardized, mandatory data submissions documenting these interactions, FDA lacks the systematic visibility needed to identify patterns of abuse, directly delaying ANDA approvals for our members—sometimes by years—inflating costs and postponing patient access to affordable medicines. Furthermore, brand manufacturers often file REMS modifications at strategic moments that reset or complicate shared system negotiation timelines with generic applicants. Because current reporting obligations do not require brand manufacturers to disclose the business context or competitive landscape at the time of a REMS modification submission, FDA cannot readily distinguish legitimate safety-driven modifications from those timed to obstruct generic entry, costing patients access to lower-cost alternatives and costing the healthcare system billions in avoidable brand-drug expenditures [3, 4].

While AAM supports the agency's modernization of reporting obligations for ANDA and biosimilar submissions, we urge the agency to align any revised data submission frequency and format requirements with existing GDUFA performance commitments and CDISC standards already being phased in—rather than layering duplicative or conflicting mandates that would compound the operational burden on generic and biosimilar applicants. AAM member companies collectively submitted 740 ANDAs in FY2024 alone, with monthly submission volumes ranging from 26 to 140 applications [9]. Any increase in reporting frequency—for example, shifting from annual to quarterly data submissions—would require member companies to dedicate significant additional staff, IT infrastructure, and compliance resources to meet new deadlines, diverting capacity away from the core work of developing and filing new generic and biosimilar applications. This is particularly acute for smaller generic manufacturers operating on thin margins in competitive markets where generics and biosimilars already saved patients $467 billion in 2024 [9]. Simultaneously, the March 2024 effective date for CDISC SENDIG-AR v1.0 support—with full requirement applicability for studies starting after March 15, 2027—demonstrates that the agency already has a phased data standardization framework underway. Imposing additional, non-harmonized reporting obligations on top of this transition would force companies to maintain parallel compliance tracks, multiplying costs without proportionate safety or data quality benefit.

**Implementation Timelines:**
AAM supports the proposed modernization rule but strongly urges the agency to adopt phased, staggered implementation timelines for ANDA applicants that are explicitly sequenced after—not concurrent with—FDA's resolution of its existing generic drug review backlog. If the proposed implementation timelines require ANDA sponsors to simultaneously overhaul reporting systems, documentation practices, and compliance infrastructure while also responding to FDA information requests on pending applications, the operational bandwidth of both industry and the agency will be stretched beyond capacity. Recent data confirm that FDA drug reviews held steady in 2025 only because review divisions absorbed historic staffing losses through extraordinary effort, and the agency approved fewer novel and generic drugs in 2025 than in prior years [8, 9]. For generic manufacturers—many of whom are small or mid-sized companies operating on thin margins in a $425 billion global market [5, 6]—compliance transition costs are not trivial. Diverting technical and regulatory affairs staff to meet new documentation mandates will slow the preparation and prosecution of pending ANDAs, compounding the existing backlog problem the agency itself has acknowledged under GDUFA III commitments [9].

**Documentation Standards:**
AAM supports the agency's effort to modernize documentation standards for biosimilar regulatory review, but urges FDA to design these standards in a way that does not disproportionately increase the already-substantial cost burden on biosimilar applicants, which would undermine the very market competition the BPCI Act was designed to foster. AAM member companies already face development costs of $100 million to $300 million per biosimilar, with over half of that time and spending consumed by clinical trials and regulatory documentation requirements [1]. New documentation standards that add layers of record-keeping complexity—without commensurate reductions in duplicative data requests or clearer evidentiary standards—will further strain the economics of biosimilar development. For smaller generic and biosimilar manufacturers operating on thin margins, incremental compliance costs can tip a pipeline program from viable to abandoned, directly reducing the number of biosimilars that reach patients. The U.S. biosimilars market grew at a 21% CAGR from $4.5 billion in 2019 to $9.3 billion in 2023 [7]; new documentation burdens risk slowing this momentum precisely when market expansion is accelerating.

AAM also strongly supports the proposed documentation standards as applied to Orange Book and Purple Book patent listing challenges, and urges the agency to explicitly extend these standards to require brand-name manufacturers to maintain and produce comprehensive, auditable records substantiating the statutory basis for each listed patent—particularly device-related and formulation patents that have been the subject of FTC warning letters for improper listing [5]. AAM member companies filing ANDAs and biosimilar applications routinely face patent thickets composed of dozens of incrementally differentiated patents—many of questionable statutory validity—that trigger automatic 30-month stays and force costly Paragraph IV litigation [6]. Without standardized documentation requirements imposed on brand-name filers, generic applicants bear asymmetric compliance burdens: they must produce exhaustive bioequivalence records and patent certification documentation, while brand-name manufacturers face no equivalent obligation to document the factual and legal basis for each Orange Book or Purple Book patent listing [6]. This asymmetry directly delays generic market entry, increasing litigation costs for AAM members and prolonging consumer exposure to monopoly pricing. A 2023 Rutgers University study documented a 265% increase in Purple Book patent continuations between 2014 and 2020, a pattern consistent with strategic thicketing rather than genuine innovation [6]. The FTC's 2023–2025 warning letter campaign confirms that improperly listed patents are not an isolated problem but a systemic one that standardized documentation requirements could help correct [5].

Furthermore, AAM supports new documentation standards only if they are harmonized with FDA's existing biosimilar guidance framework—including the agency's 2024 postapproval manufacturing changes Q&A guidance (Docket No. FDA-2024-D-2581)—so that manufacturers are not required to maintain duplicative or conflicting record sets that create regulatory uncertainty and delay approvals [1, 2]. AAM member companies operating under the biosimilar approval pathway must simultaneously comply with multiple overlapping FDA guidance documents governing analytical comparability, manufacturing changes, labeling, and interchangeability [1]. If the new documentation standards introduce record-keeping requirements that are inconsistent with or additive to the 2024 postapproval manufacturing changes guidance, manufacturers will face conflicting obligations. This is particularly acute for companies managing post-approval manufacturing changes, where documentation errors or inconsistencies can trigger Complete Response Letters, adding 12–18 months to approval timelines and millions in additional costs [2]. The FDA review backlog for biosimilar applications already represents a significant operational constraint for AAM members.

Finally, AAM supports the proposed documentation standards but urges the agency to account for—and offset—the disproportionate compliance cost burden these standards will impose on generic and biosimilar applicants who are already navigating patent thicket litigation, and to adopt tiered or streamlined documentation pathways that reduce duplicative recordkeeping without sacrificing data quality. For AAM members, the compliance cost calculus is fundamentally different from that of brand-name manufacturers. Generic applicants already invest heavily in ANDA documentation—a 2023 study cited by DrugPatentWatch found that 20% of ANDA rejections stem from inadequate bioequivalence data, reflecting the already-high documentation bar generic filers must clear [5]. Layering additional documentation standards on top of existing ANDA requirements, without corresponding obligations on brand-name patent listers, risks compounding the cost disadvantage generic manufacturers face. When a single brand-name product may be protected by 20, 40, or even 100 patents—as documented in patent thicket research—each requiring separate Paragraph IV certifications and potentially separate litigation tracks, the administrative and legal costs of compliance with new documentation standards multiply accordingly [5, 6]. These costs are ultimately borne by patients through delayed access to lower-cost generics.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
In light of the potential impacts outlined above, AAM offers the following specific recommendations for the final rule:

**Regarding Revised Reporting Obligations:**
*   FDA should design the revised reporting obligations to specifically require brand-name REMS holders to submit quarterly data disclosures covering: (1) the number and outcome of all generic applicant requests for RLD sample access under REMS programs; (2) the terms offered and rejected in shared REMS system negotiations; and (3) any modifications to REMS requirements that coincide with generic ANDA filing or approval milestones. This targeted data collection would give FDA the evidentiary foundation to act swiftly under its existing authority to require shared REMS systems and to refer bad-faith conduct to the FTC [3, 4].
*   FDA should amend the content requirements for REMS modification submissions (both minor and major) to include a mandatory disclosure of: (1) whether any ANDA or biosimilar application referencing the RLD is pending or has received tentative approval at the time of the modification; (2) the anticipated impact of the modification on any existing or pending shared REMS system negotiations; and (3) a certification that the modification is not being submitted for the purpose of delaying shared system implementation [3, 4]. FDA should further commit to a defined review timeline for modifications submitted during active shared-system negotiations, preventing indefinite delay through iterative minor changes.
*   The agency should (1) explicitly confirm that any revised reporting obligations for ANDA and biosimilar submissions are fully harmonized with the CDISC electronic submission standards already being implemented, avoiding duplicative or conflicting format requirements; (2) retain annual rather than more frequent submission cycles for routine compliance data, reserving higher-frequency reporting for post-market safety signals or specific risk-based triggers; and (3) publish a consolidated compliance calendar that maps all new reporting obligations against existing GDUFA and PDUFA commitments so applicants can plan resource allocation without uncertainty [9]. The agency specifically requested comment on expected costs and operational impacts—AAM urges the agency to conduct a formal Small Business Regulatory Enforcement Fairness Act (SBREFA) analysis quantifying the per-application cost increase before finalizing any frequency changes.

**Regarding Implementation Timelines:**
*   The agency should structure implementation timelines in two distinct phases: Phase 1 (Years 1–2) should apply only to new ANDA submissions filed after the rule's effective date, allowing existing pending applications to proceed under current standards without disruption [9]. Phase 2 (Years 3–4) should apply to legacy applications and existing approval holders, timed to coincide with FDA's own internal milestones for reducing the ANDA review backlog as tracked under GDUFA III performance goals. The agency should also publish a concurrent resource plan demonstrating that FDA review staff will not be redeployed to compliance oversight functions during the transition window, protecting review throughput [8, 9].

**Regarding Documentation Standards:**
*   In direct response to the agency's request for comments on expected costs and operational impacts, FDA should (1) publish a detailed cost-benefit analysis specific to biosimilar applicants before finalizing any new documentation standards, disaggregated by company size; (2) grandfather applications already in active review under existing documentation frameworks to avoid mid-stream disruption; and (3) establish a tiered compliance schedule that gives biosimilar sponsors at least 24 months to implement new standards, with small-business accommodations consistent with the Small Business Regulatory Enforcement Fairness Act [1, 7].
*   The agency should: (1) explicitly apply the proposed documentation standards to require brand-name manufacturers to create and maintain contemporaneous records—at the time of listing—demonstrating that each Orange Book or Purple Book patent meets the statutory 'claiming the drug' or 'claiming a method of using the drug' standard; (2) require that such records be made available to the agency upon request and, in the context of patent listing disputes, to generic or biosimilar applicants within a defined timeframe; and (3) establish a standardized submission format for patent listing certifications analogous to the documentation formats already required of ANDA filers, so that the agency can efficiently audit listing accuracy [5, 6]. The agency should clarify in the final rule that these documentation standards apply symmetrically across all regulated parties in the drug approval ecosystem, not solely to generic applicants.
*   In response to the agency's request for comments on alternatives, AAM recommends that FDA (1) conduct a formal cross-reference mapping exercise between the proposed documentation standards and all existing biosimilar-specific guidance documents before finalization, publishing a reconciliation table in the Federal Register; (2) explicitly state in the final rule which existing guidance documents are superseded, modified, or unaffected; and (3) establish a single, consolidated documentation framework for biosimilar applications rather than layering new standards atop existing ones [1, 2]. This 'one-stop' approach would reduce compliance costs, minimize ambiguity, and accelerate FDA's own review efficiency.
*   In response to the agency's specific question on expected costs and alternatives, AAM recommends the agency: (1) conduct a formal cost-benefit analysis that disaggregates compliance costs between brand-name and generic/biosimilar applicants, rather than treating the industry as monolithic; (2) adopt a tiered documentation framework that allows generic applicants to incorporate by reference documentation already submitted in prior ANDA or biosimilar filings for the same reference listed drug, avoiding duplicative recordkeeping across related applications; (3) establish a safe harbor from enhanced documentation requirements for generic applicants who are actively contesting Orange Book or Purple Book patent listings that are the subject of FTC challenge or agency review, recognizing that the underlying patent record is itself in dispute; and (4) set implementation timelines that account for the FDA review backlog for generic applications, ensuring that new documentation requirements do not further delay already-pending ANDAs [5, 6, 9].

[CONCLUSION]
AAM commends the agency's commitment to modernizing and standardizing oversight of the regulated industry. As the leading advocate for patient access to affordable, high-quality generics and biosimilars, we urge the agency to finalize a rule that achieves its stated objectives without creating unintended barriers to market competition and patient access. By implementing the recommendations outlined above, the agency can ensure that the revised reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards advance public health goals by supporting the timely approval and availability of affordable medicines.

We appreciate the opportunity to provide these comments and look forward to continued collaboration with the agency on this important issue.

Sincerely,

[Signature]

[Name]
[Title]
Association for Accessible Medicines (AAM)
