{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
As a biosecurity expert and researcher whose work directly informs medical countermeasure (MCM) regulatory pathway design, Emergency Use Authorization (EUA) frameworks, and pandemic preparedness strategies, I strongly support the agency's proposed suite of new compliance requirements, specifically the revision of existing reporting obligations, the establishment of specific implementation timelines, and the introduction of more rigorous documentation standards. These changes are critical steps toward modernizing and standardizing the oversight of regulated industries, which is essential for strengthening our national biosecurity and improving our ability to respond effectively to future public health emergencies.

[BACKGROUND]
The proposed rule addresses perceived gaps in current data collection and record-keeping practices, aiming to improve the quality and timeliness of information received by the agency. The core regulatory changes focus on three primary areas: "revised reporting obligations," "implementation timelines," and "documentation standards." These provisions are directly relevant to enhancing the regulatory agility and data infrastructure necessary for efficient MCM development, robust pandemic preparedness, and effective EUA decision-making. The experience of the COVID-19 pandemic highlighted significant vulnerabilities in our existing regulatory landscape, underscoring the urgent need for the enhancements proposed in this rule.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]

**Revised Reporting Obligations and Data Submission Content:**
I strongly support the proposed "revised reporting obligations" because standardized, higher-frequency data submissions will close critical intelligence gaps that have historically delayed emergency authorization decisions during public health crises. The COVID-19 EUA experience demonstrated that FDA's ability to act with speed and confidence is directly proportional to the quality and timeliness of pre-existing data infrastructure from regulated entities [1]. My research on MCM regulatory pathways depends on analyzing real-world compliance and safety data to model how regulatory frameworks perform under emergency conditions. Under the current fragmented reporting regime, I and other academic researchers face significant gaps in longitudinal MCM data, making it difficult to evaluate whether EUA frameworks are functioning as intended or to identify systemic bottlenecks before the next crisis. The proposed revised reporting obligations—by standardizing format and increasing submission frequency—would generate a richer, more consistent dataset that directly enables the kind of comparative regulatory analysis needed to strengthen future emergency response architecture [1].

Furthermore, I strongly support the revised reporting obligations and updated data submission content requirements because standardized, high-quality data submissions are precisely the infrastructure gap that crippled early COVID-19 EUA decision-making and delayed life-saving countermeasure deployment [8, 9]. My research on EUA framework lessons from COVID-19 has documented firsthand how inconsistent, incomplete, and non-standardized data submissions from manufacturers and clinical sites forced FDA reviewers to spend critical weeks reconciling incompatible datasets rather than evaluating safety and efficacy signals. In a public health emergency, every day of administrative friction translates directly into preventable mortality. The current non-standardized reporting environment that this rule seeks to correct is not merely an inconvenience—it is a national security vulnerability. When the next pandemic or bioterrorism event triggers EUA activation, the agency's ability to rapidly assess incoming data will depend entirely on whether that data arrives in a structured, comparable, and machine-readable format. The proposed revised content standards for data submissions directly address this gap [8, 9].

However, responding directly to the agency's request for comments on operational impacts and alternatives, while I support the revised reporting obligations, I urge the agency to recognize that the operational burden of new documentation standards will fall disproportionately on smaller MCM developers and academic medical centers conducting translational research—entities that are often the earliest-stage innovators in the MCM pipeline and least equipped to absorb compliance costs. Failing to account for this asymmetry risks chilling precisely the research activity that feeds the MCM development pipeline at its most critical early stage [1]. My work at the intersection of national security and regulatory science regularly involves interaction with academic spinouts, small biotechs, and university-based research programs developing novel countermeasures against CBRN and pandemic threats. These entities operate under constrained administrative budgets and lack the dedicated regulatory affairs infrastructure of large pharmaceutical manufacturers. The PHEMCE 2023-2027 Strategic Implementation Plan identifies diversification of the MCM industrial base as a national security priority—yet imposing uniform reporting burdens without tiered accommodation could consolidate MCM development among only the largest, most compliance-capable firms, undermining that strategic goal [1].

**Implementation Timelines:**
I strongly support the proposed "implementation timelines" as a mechanism to close longstanding gaps in biosecurity data collection and reporting, because the absence of standardized, time-certain compliance schedules has historically allowed regulatory modernization to stall indefinitely—a national security vulnerability that my research has documented as a direct contributor to pandemic preparedness failures [2, 3]. In my research on medical countermeasure regulatory pathways, the absence of enforceable transition deadlines has been a persistent obstacle to achieving the data quality necessary for rapid countermeasure development. When reporting and documentation standards are aspirational rather than time-bound, the agency receives inconsistent, incomparable data sets that impair its ability to identify emerging biological threats and authorize countermeasures at speed. The COVID-19 EUA experience revealed that the FDA and partner agencies were working with fragmented baseline data precisely because prior modernization efforts lacked binding implementation schedules. Definite timelines create the compliance certainty that enables regulated entities to invest in infrastructure upgrades, which in turn produces the high-quality data the agency needs for national security-relevant decision-making [2, 3].

While supporting these timelines, I urge the agency to build in explicit national security carve-outs that preserve emergency regulatory agility—particularly the ability to accelerate or suspend transition schedules during declared public health emergencies or credible biological threat events [2, 3]. My research on EUA frameworks during COVID-19 demonstrated that rigid, calendar-driven compliance schedules created critical bottlenecks when agencies needed to pivot rapidly to authorize novel countermeasures. Fixed implementation timelines, if applied uniformly without emergency override mechanisms, risk locking regulated entities and the agency itself into procedural postures that are incompatible with the speed required during a biological crisis. In my operational research context, I have observed that entities mid-transition to new standards are often in their most vulnerable compliance state—neither fully operating under old frameworks nor fully equipped under new ones—precisely the moment when a biological threat may demand maximum regulatory flexibility [2, 3].

**Documentation Standards:**
I strongly support the proposed "documentation standards" because harmonized, rigorous record-keeping is a foundational prerequisite for effective international health security coordination—a gap that has repeatedly undermined pandemic response. Inconsistent documentation practices across jurisdictions created critical intelligence failures during COVID-19, and these new standards represent a necessary corrective that will strengthen the U.S. position in global health security frameworks [4, 5]. My research on EUA frameworks has demonstrated that one of the most significant bottlenecks in international health security coordination is the incompatibility of regulatory documentation between the U.S. and partner nations. When the FDA and counterpart agencies such as the EMA, WHO prequalification bodies, or CEPI-affiliated regulators cannot readily interpret each other's records, the result is duplicative review processes, delayed countermeasure deployment, and erosion of the mutual recognition arrangements that underpin rapid response. The 2024 U.S. Global Health Security Strategy explicitly identifies 'strategic and coordinated efforts at all levels' as essential to protecting national security, and standardized documentation is the connective tissue that makes such coordination operationally viable [4, 5]. Without it, even well-resourced international partnerships produce inefficiencies, duplication of effort, and dangerous gaps—precisely the failure modes identified in the Biden Administration's Global Health Security Annual Report regarding health emergency financing and coordination [4, 5].

Moreover, I strongly support the proposed documentation standards and revised reporting obligations because standardized, high-quality data submissions are foundational to the regulatory agility that MCM development demands—particularly under Emergency Use Authorization (EUA) frameworks where speed and evidentiary rigor must coexist. The COVID-19 pandemic exposed critical gaps in the consistency and completeness of data submitted during accelerated review processes, and these proposed standards directly address those vulnerabilities [6, 7]. My research on MCM regulatory pathways—conducted in collaboration with BARDA's Division of Regulatory and Quality Affairs and informed by ASPR's preparedness mission—depends on the quality and comparability of regulatory submission records. Inconsistent documentation formats across MCM developers have made it extraordinarily difficult to conduct cross-product analyses of regulatory timelines, safety signals, and approval outcomes. The proposed documentation standards would create a structured evidentiary baseline that my research program, and the broader academic biosecurity community, can use to evaluate pathway efficiency and identify systemic bottlenecks before the next public health emergency [6, 7].

Finally, I support the proposed documentation standards and specifically recommend the agency treat international health security coordination as a distinct use case requiring tailored provisions within the final rule—including explicit provisions for rapid documentation access and sharing under emergency conditions. Failure to address this use case risks creating a rule that is well-optimized for routine oversight but inadvertently impedes the very emergency response agility that biosecurity demands [4, 5]. My work at the intersection of national security and regulatory science has consistently identified a structural tension in regulatory design: standards built for steady-state compliance often become obstacles during emergencies, when speed and interoperability matter more than procedural completeness. The 2025 Pandemic Agreement and the 2024 amendments to the International Health Regulations (IHR) now create binding international obligations around surveillance data sharing and regulatory coordination that the U.S. must be positioned to fulfill [4, 5]. If the new documentation standards are designed solely around domestic compliance workflows without accounting for the formats and access protocols required by WHO and partner regulatory agencies, the U.S. risks being in technical compliance with its own regulations while being operationally non-compliant with its international treaty obligations. This is an unintended consequence the agency may not have fully considered [4, 5].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
Based on the analysis above, I offer the following specific recommendations to enhance the effectiveness of the proposed rule:

**Regarding Revised Reporting Obligations and Data Submission Content:**
1.  **Public Accessibility and International Alignment:** The agency should adopt the revised reporting obligations as proposed, but additionally: (1) mandate that a de-identified, aggregated version of the standardized data submissions be made publicly accessible in machine-readable format on a quarterly basis to support independent academic and biosecurity research; (2) align the new reporting format with international frameworks such as the EU's Article 10a supply reporting obligations for medical devices (Regulation EU 2024/1860) to reduce duplicative compliance burdens for multinational MCM developers and facilitate cross-jurisdictional data sharing during emergencies; and (3) establish a formal feedback loop between FDA and academic biosecurity researchers to evaluate whether the new data streams are generating actionable regulatory intelligence, with a mandatory review at 24 months post-implementation [1].
2.  **EUA-Specific Data Templates:** The agency should explicitly incorporate EUA-specific data submission templates as a subset of the revised reporting obligations, ensuring that the standardized formats adopted under this rule are pre-validated for emergency use scenarios. Specifically, I recommend the agency: (1) develop a tiered data submission framework that distinguishes routine compliance reporting from emergency-pathway submissions, with the latter optimized for rapid machine-readable ingestion; (2) align the revised content standards with interoperability frameworks already being advanced under federal health IT rules, such as the HTI-2 standards for data exchange, to avoid creating parallel and incompatible reporting ecosystems; and (3) require that all regulated entities demonstrate readiness to submit EUA-compatible data packages within 72 hours of an emergency declaration, with this capability verified through the new documentation standards proposed in this rule [8, 9].
3.  **Tiered Compliance for Small Entities:** Responding directly to the agency's request for comments on operational impacts and alternatives: the agency should adopt a tiered compliance framework within the revised reporting obligations that: (1) provides a 24-month phased implementation period for entities with fewer than 500 employees or annual MCM-related revenues below a defined threshold, with technical assistance resources provided during the transition; (2) creates a simplified reporting template for pre-IND and Phase I/II stage MCM research that captures the core safety and supply data the agency needs without requiring the full documentation burden applicable to late-stage or approved products; and (3) explicitly exempts or provides safe harbor provisions for federally-funded academic research programs operating under BARDA, NIH, or DARPA grants, where federal oversight mechanisms already provide parallel accountability. These accommodations would preserve the rule's core data quality objectives while protecting the innovation diversity that national biosecurity requires [1].

**Regarding Implementation Timelines:**
4.  **National Security Regulatory Agility Clause:** The agency should adopt the proposed implementation timelines as a baseline but codify a formal 'national security regulatory agility clause' within the final rule. This clause should: (1) authorize the agency head to suspend or extend transition deadlines by up to 180 days upon declaration of a public health emergency or credible biological threat; (2) establish a pre-designated interagency coordination mechanism with the Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs (APNSA), consistent with the White House's existing biosecurity coordination architecture; and (3) require the agency to publish a contingency compliance pathway within 60 days of any such suspension. This mirrors the approach taken in the 2025 Executive Order on biological research safety, which explicitly required OSTP to coordinate with the APNSA on framework revisions within defined but flexible timeframes [2, 3].
5.  **Targeted Technical Assistance:** Responding directly to the agency's request for comments on operational impacts and alternatives: the agency should resist pressure to extend or phase implementation timelines beyond what is proposed, as indefinite phasing has historically functioned as de facto non-implementation. Instead, the agency should pair the proposed timelines with a tiered technical assistance program—differentiated by entity size and existing infrastructure capacity—that provides concrete support for compliance without diluting the binding nature of the deadlines. The agency should also publish interim compliance benchmarks at the 30%, 60%, and 90% marks of each transition period, allowing the agency to identify systemic implementation failures early and intervene before the final deadline, rather than discovering widespread non-compliance after the fact [2, 3].

**Regarding Documentation Standards:**
6.  **International Interoperability as Design Criterion:** The agency should explicitly design the new documentation standards with international interoperability as a stated design criterion, not merely a secondary benefit. Specifically, I recommend the agency: (1) benchmark the proposed standards against WHO's International Health Regulations documentation frameworks and the 2025 Pandemic Agreement's surveillance and data-sharing provisions to ensure compatibility; (2) publish a cross-reference table mapping the new U.S. documentation requirements to equivalent standards used by major regulatory partners (EMA, Health Canada, TGA), enabling mutual recognition pathways to be preserved or accelerated; and (3) establish a standing technical working group with international regulatory counterparts to maintain alignment as standards evolve. The agency should also clarify in the final rule preamble that documentation submitted under these standards will be structured to facilitate rapid sharing under EUA or emergency authorization conditions, reducing the friction that delayed countermeasure access during COVID-19 [4, 5].
7.  **MCM-Specific Documentation Guidance:** The agency should adopt the proposed documentation standards as written, but should additionally issue a companion technical guidance document specifically tailored to MCM developers operating under the Animal Rule, EUA, or accelerated approval pathways. These developers face unique evidentiary challenges—including limited human efficacy data and compressed development timelines—that standard documentation templates may not fully accommodate. A tiered documentation framework, with a core standardized record structure plus pathway-specific annexes, would preserve the standardization benefits while avoiding a one-size-fits-all approach that could inadvertently disadvantage small biodefense developers who lack the regulatory infrastructure of large pharmaceutical firms [6, 7].
8.  **Emergency and International Coordination Section:** The agency should add a dedicated section to the final rule—or at minimum to the accompanying guidance—addressing documentation standards in the context of emergency and international coordination scenarios. This section should: (1) specify that documentation maintained under the new standards must be exportable in formats compatible with WHO's Global Outbreak Alert and Response Network (GOARN) and IHR reporting systems; (2) establish a pre-authorized rapid-access protocol allowing designated international regulatory partners to access relevant records during declared public health emergencies of international concern (PHEICs), consistent with existing information-sharing agreements; and (3) require that regulated entities with products likely to be subject to international emergency authorization maintain a 'regulatory dossier summary' in a standardized, internationally legible format. These provisions would transform the documentation standards from a domestic compliance exercise into a genuine force multiplier for U.S. global health security leadership, consistent with the 2024 Global Health Security Strategy's emphasis on coordinated, multi-level action [4, 5].

[CONCLUSION]
In conclusion, the proposed rule's "revised reporting obligations," "implementation timelines," and "documentation standards" represent a critical and necessary modernization of our regulatory landscape. As a biosecurity expert and researcher, I strongly support these provisions as foundational to improving EUA frameworks, strengthening pandemic preparedness regulatory pathways, and fostering a more agile and robust medical countermeasure development ecosystem. By incorporating the specific recommendations outlined above, the agency can ensure that the final rule not only addresses current compliance gaps but also strategically positions the U.S. to lead in global health security, protect national interests, and respond effectively to future biological threats.

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The International Dairy Foods Association (IDFA) represents the nation's dairy manufacturing and marketing industry, which supports more than 3 million jobs that generate $646 billion in annual economic impact. Our members include processors of fluid milk, cheese, yogurt, ice cream, and other dairy products, ranging from small, family-owned businesses to large multinational corporations. We appreciate the opportunity to comment on the agency's proposed suite of new compliance requirements, including revised reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards.

[BACKGROUND]
IDFA strongly opposes many aspects of this proposal as currently structured because it fails to adequately consider the unique operational complexities of the dairy industry, including seasonal milk production variability and the critical need to enforce long-established standards of identity for dairy products. Moreover, the proposal creates disproportionate burdens on traditional dairy manufacturers compared to plant-based alternative manufacturers, effectively subsidizing the competitive position of products mislabeled with dairy terms.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**Revised Reporting Obligations**
IDFA opposes the revised reporting obligations as proposed because they impose disproportionate operational burdens on dairy manufacturers already managing complex standards of identity compliance, without providing commensurate regulatory benefit — particularly at a moment when FDA is simultaneously proposing to revoke 18 dairy standards of identity, creating a period of acute regulatory uncertainty that makes new data submission mandates especially disruptive. IDFA member companies — processors of fluid milk, cheese, and related dairy products — must simultaneously track compliance with existing standards of identity under 21 C.F.R. Parts 131 and 133 [8], monitor the proposed revocation of 18 standards (including low sodium Cheddar, low sodium Colby, acidified milk, and multiple specialty cheeses), and now absorb revised reporting frequency and format requirements. This triple compliance burden falls on quality assurance, regulatory affairs, and operations teams that are already stretched. For mid-sized cheese manufacturers in particular, reformatting data submissions to meet new agency specifications requires investment in IT infrastructure and personnel training that was not budgeted. The agency's own question regarding 'expected costs' (Agency Question 1) is directly implicated: IDFA estimates that transitioning reporting systems to new formats could cost member companies between $50,000 and $250,000 per facility depending on size, with ongoing annual compliance costs of $15,000–$75,000 per facility for increased submission frequency. This is directly responsive to the agency's request for data on expected costs.

Furthermore, IDFA opposes the revised reporting obligations as proposed because they create an asymmetric compliance burden that disadvantages traditional dairy producers relative to plant-based alternative manufacturers, effectively subsidizing the competitive position of plant-based products that already benefit from lax standards-of-identity enforcement. The agency's revised data submission requirements impose new documentation costs on dairy processors without simultaneously requiring equivalent transparency from plant-based alternative manufacturers regarding the nutritional composition differences between their products and the dairy foods they mimic. IDFA member companies — processors of fluid milk, cheese, yogurt, and other dairy products — operate under decades-old, rigorously enforced standards of identity that mandate specific compositional requirements and labeling disclosures. These standards generate substantial existing compliance documentation. The proposed revised reporting obligations would require IDFA members to restructure and reformat data submissions at significant cost, while plant-based alternative manufacturers — who already benefit from FDA's permissive approach to names like 'oat milk' and 'almond cheese' — face no comparable compositional reporting requirements. The plant-based milk market is growing at a projected CAGR of 9.9% through 2033, meaning this regulatory asymmetry compounds over time as a competitive disadvantage for dairy [7]. IDFA members estimate that reformatting existing compliance data systems to meet revised submission content requirements will require significant IT investment and staff retraining, costs that cannot be passed through in commodity-priced dairy markets.

IDFA opposes any revised reporting framework that diverts agency enforcement resources toward paperwork compliance monitoring rather than toward the more pressing and consumer-harmful problem of plant-based product mislabeling — urging the agency to ensure that new documentation standards actively support, rather than crowd out, standards of identity enforcement against non-dairy products falsely labeled as 'milk' or 'cheese.' IDFA members compete directly with plant-based alternative manufacturers, many of whom label their products using dairy terms ('oat milk,' 'almond cheese,' 'cashew cream cheese') in apparent violation of FDA's standards of identity [8]. The DAIRY PRIDE Act (S. 549, 118th Congress) [1] reflects Congressional recognition that FDA enforcement against these mislabeling practices has been inadequate. If the agency's revised reporting obligations consume significant compliance bandwidth — both for industry and for FDA's own review staff — the practical effect will be to further delay or deprioritize enforcement actions against plant-based mislabeling. IDFA members bear a double burden: they invest in meeting rigorous standards of identity requirements while competitors who ignore those standards face minimal enforcement scrutiny. This is directly responsive to the agency's question on 'alternatives' (Agency Question 3): there is a superior alternative use of both industry and agency resources.

**Implementation Timelines**
The International Dairy Foods Association strongly opposes the proposed fixed implementation timelines as written, because they fail to account for the inherent seasonal variability of milk production, creating an operationally impossible compliance window for dairy processors who must align new standards with fluctuating raw milk supply cycles. IDFA member processors operate under conditions where milk volume, composition (fat, protein, solids), and availability shift dramatically across seasons due to heat stress, calving patterns, and fodder availability [2, 3]. As documented in peer-reviewed research, heat stress during warmer months suppresses milk production and alters composition, meaning that a fixed compliance deadline could fall precisely during a period when processors lack the consistent raw material inputs needed to reformulate, retest, and re-document products to meet new standards of identity [3]. For cheese and fluid milk manufacturers in particular, seasonal composition variability directly affects whether a product can meet identity thresholds at all during certain months, making a single universal deadline technically unachievable for a significant portion of the year.

IDFA opposes the proposed implementation timelines because the agency has failed to adequately estimate the costs imposed on dairy processors who must invest in reformulation, retesting, and re-documentation during periods of peak seasonal production stress, when operational capacity is already constrained—resulting in a significant underestimate of compliance costs that the agency is required to assess. IDFA members—particularly mid-sized cheese and fluid milk processors—face their highest operational burdens during summer months when heat stress reduces milk output and quality, requiring more frequent testing, supplier diversification, and ingredient adjustment to maintain product consistency [2]. Layering new compliance documentation requirements and transition deadlines on top of these existing seasonal pressures will force processors to divert staff, laboratory resources, and capital expenditures away from core production activities at precisely the wrong time. The farmdoc analysis of 2024 milk economics shows that average net milk prices of $21.63 per hundredweight leave thin margins that cannot easily absorb simultaneous compliance investment and seasonal production losses [9]. A compliance deadline that falls in Q2 or Q3 could force some smaller IDFA members to choose between meeting the deadline and maintaining product output.

**Documentation Standards**
In direct response to the agency's request for data on expected costs and alternatives, IDFA urges the agency to adopt a tiered documentation framework that scales new record-keeping requirements to facility size and production volume, rather than imposing uniform standards that place the heaviest relative burden on small and mid-sized dairy manufacturers — the segment most critical to regional milk supply chains and artisan cheese production. IDFA's membership includes both large national dairy processors and smaller regional and artisan producers. The proposed uniform documentation standards do not distinguish between a multi-billion-dollar national cheese manufacturer with dedicated compliance staff and a 15-employee artisan creamery producing farmstead cheese under the same standards of identity. For smaller IDFA members, the cost of redesigning record systems, training staff, and presenting records in new formats is not a marginal operational adjustment — it is a potentially business-threatening expense. Industry data from analogous food sector compliance initiatives suggests that per-business compliance costs are routinely underestimated by agencies; one food industry commenter characterized an agency's $4,273 per-business estimate as 'egregiously' low [7]. If the agency's cost estimates for the proposed documentation standards are similarly understated, smaller dairy manufacturers face an existential compliance burden that larger competitors can absorb — an outcome that would accelerate consolidation in dairy manufacturing and reduce the regional supply diversity that supports fluid milk markets and specialty cheese production [4, 5].

IDFA opposes the proposed documentation standards as written because they impose disproportionate compliance costs on dairy manufacturers without meaningfully advancing standards of identity enforcement — the very regulatory function that protects consumers from mislabeled plant-based alternatives masquerading as milk, cheese, and other dairy products. IDFA member companies — ranging from large national processors to regional cheese makers — already maintain extensive records to demonstrate compliance with FDA's standards of identity for milk, cheese, yogurt, and other dairy products [8]. The proposed new documentation standards would require these same companies to reformat, re-categorize, and re-present existing records in ways that serve no additional enforcement purpose for standards of identity compliance. At the same time, the agency has historically under-enforced standards of identity against plant-based alternative manufacturers who misuse dairy terms. The new documentation burden falls almost entirely on compliant dairy manufacturers, while doing nothing to close the enforcement gap against non-compliant labelers. This creates a perverse regulatory outcome: the regulated parties who are already in compliance bear new costs, while the parties whose labeling practices most urgently need scrutiny face no new documentation obligations [5].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
IDFA urges the agency to fundamentally rethink the structure and timing of this proposal to ensure it supports, rather than undermines, the agency's mission to protect consumers and promote fair competition. Specifically, we recommend the following:

**Regarding Revised Reporting Obligations:**
1.  **Defer Implementation**: The agency should defer implementation of revised reporting obligations until the standards of identity revocation rulemaking is finalized, so that reporting requirements are calibrated to the actual regulatory landscape rather than one in transition.
2.  **Conduct SBREFA Analysis**: The agency should conduct a formal Small Business Regulatory Enforcement Fairness Act (SBREFA) analysis quantifying per-facility costs before finalizing any new submission format or frequency requirements.
3.  **Provide Phase-in Period & Standardized Templates**: The agency should provide a minimum 24-month phase-in period with standardized, machine-readable reporting templates developed in collaboration with industry to reduce duplicative data entry burdens.
4.  **Require Equivalent Reporting from Plant-Based Alternatives**: In response to its own request for comments on expected costs and operational impacts, the agency should conduct a formal regulatory impact analysis that compares the compliance cost burden imposed on standards-of-identity-regulated dairy processors against the documentation requirements applicable to plant-based alternative manufacturers. If the agency proceeds with revised reporting obligations, it must simultaneously require plant-based alternative manufacturers to submit equivalent nutritional composition and ingredient sourcing data so that the agency has a complete, comparable dataset across the competitive landscape.
5.  **Targeted, Risk-Based Reporting**: As an alternative to broad reporting frequency increases, consider targeted, risk-based reporting requirements focused on product categories where mislabeling and standards of identity confusion are most prevalent — including the interface between dairy and plant-based product categories.

**Regarding Implementation Timelines:**
1.  **Adopt Production-Cycle-Aligned Framework**: The agency should replace fixed calendar-date implementation deadlines with a 'production-cycle-aligned' compliance framework that allows processors to demonstrate initial compliance during a full 12-month production cycle rather than at a single point in time.
2.  **Allow Seasonal Production Data**: Specifically, IDFA recommends the agency allow processors to submit seasonal production data as part of their compliance documentation to account for composition variability.
3.  **Provide 24-Month Phase-in**: The agency should provide a minimum 24-month phase-in period to ensure that at least two full seasonal cycles are available for compliance testing and documentation before enforcement begins.
4.  **Revised Regulatory Economic Impact Analysis**: In direct response to the agency's request for comments on expected costs, IDFA urges the agency to conduct a revised Regulatory Economic Impact Analysis that explicitly models seasonal cost variability—not just average annual costs. The revised analysis should: (1) disaggregate compliance cost estimates by season to reflect peak-burden periods; (2) account for the cost of maintaining dual documentation systems during transition; (3) include small and mid-sized processor impact scenarios; and (4) consider a tiered fee or compliance assistance program for processors demonstrating seasonal production constraints. The agency should not finalize implementation timelines until this revised cost analysis is complete and publicly available for comment.

**Regarding Documentation Standards:**
1.  **Tiered Documentation Framework**: The agency should adopt a tiered documentation framework with simplified record formats for facilities below defined production thresholds, consistent with the Small Business Administration's guidance on regulatory flexibility.
2.  **Revised Cost Analysis by Facility Size**: The agency should publish a revised cost analysis that disaggregates compliance cost estimates by facility size and annual production volume, specifically addressing small and mid-sized dairy manufacturers.
3.  **Longer Compliance Timeline for Small Facilities**: The agency should provide a longer compliance timeline — no less than 24 months — for facilities with fewer than 100 employees, consistent with the approach taken in the Food Traceability Rule compliance date extension [4], which recognized that smaller and less-resourced entities need additional time to implement new documentation systems without disrupting ongoing production.
4.  **Technical Assistance Program**: The agency should establish a technical assistance program through which the agency provides model documentation templates specifically designed for dairy standards of identity compliance, reducing the cost of system redesign for smaller members.
5.  **Exempt Existing SOI Records**: The agency should exempt records already maintained under existing standards of identity compliance frameworks from the new reformatting requirements, accepting current record formats as presumptively compliant.
6.  **Simultaneous Enforcement Against Plant-Based Mislabeling**: The agency should simultaneously direct equivalent or greater documentation and enforcement resources toward plant-based alternative manufacturers whose labeling may violate existing standards of identity — ensuring the documentation modernization effort serves its stated purpose of improving compliance monitoring across all regulated parties, not just traditional dairy producers.

[CONCLUSION]
IDFA appreciates the agency's intent to modernize compliance requirements. However, as currently structured, this proposal imposes disproportionate burdens on the dairy industry, distracts from critical standards of identity enforcement needs, and creates a fundamentally unfair competitive environment with plant-based alternatives. We urge the agency to reconsider these provisions, conduct thorough impact analyses that account for dairy-specific realities, and prioritize solutions that ensure clear labeling distinctions for consumers and equitable enforcement across the marketplace.

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
Dear Commissioners and Staff,

My name is Thomas Rivera, and I am an HIV Patient Advocate with over two decades of personal experience living with and managing HIV. As an FDA Patient Representative program participant and a passionate advocate for LGBTQ+ health access, I welcome and strongly support the agency's proposal to modernize and standardize its oversight through revised reporting obligations, specific implementation timelines, and more rigorous documentation standards.

I believe these regulatory changes are not merely administrative updates; they are critical steps toward a future where generic antiretroviral access is equitable, the approval and integration of innovative treatments like long-acting injectables are accelerated, and the voices of patients, particularly those with complex, chronic conditions like HIV, are effectively heard and acted upon in advisory committee processes. My comments today are informed by my lived experience and my commitment to ensuring that regulatory frameworks translate into tangible improvements in patient care and health equity.

[BACKGROUND]
The agency's proposal outlines three core regulatory changes: revised reporting obligations, establishment of specific implementation timelines, and introduction of more rigorous documentation standards. From the perspective of an HIV patient and advocate, each of these provisions offers a vital opportunity to address persistent gaps in data collection and record-keeping that currently hinder access to essential medications and comprehensive care.

Specifically, the revised reporting obligations (as described in the Executive Summary) have the potential to provide unprecedented transparency into formulary coverage, prior authorization barriers, and market entry for generic antiretrovirals – data that is currently fragmented and difficult to access. The implementation timelines (as described in the Key Provisions) are crucial for ensuring the timely and orderly integration of new treatments, including long-acting injectables, into patient care pathways, preventing dangerous disruptions to therapy. Finally, the new documentation standards (as described in the Key Provisions) are essential for generating high-quality, disaggregated data that can inform advisory committee discussions, highlight health disparities, and improve post-market safety surveillance for long-term ART users.

My support for these provisions is conditional upon their design and implementation explicitly addressing the needs of HIV patients, particularly those who are long-term survivors, LGBTQ+ individuals, and those served by safety-net providers.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**Revised Reporting Obligations: Enhancing Generic Antiretroviral Access**
Current reporting obligations often lack the specificity and standardization needed to effectively monitor and address barriers to generic antiretroviral access. I have witnessed firsthand how fragmented data on formulary coverage and prior authorization requirements creates an opaque landscape that is difficult for patients, advocates, and even clinicians to navigate. As of early 2024, states including Colorado, Kentucky, Louisiana, and Texas had not added long-acting injectable cabotegravir/rilpivirine (CAB/RPV) to their ADAP formularies, while others still required burdensome prior authorization — barriers that disproportionately impact LGBTQ+ patients who already face stigma-related delays in seeking care [1]. Without standardized reporting, these disparities remain largely invisible to regulators.

The proposed revised reporting obligations, if designed correctly, could provide the data infrastructure needed to identify and respond to these patterns. However, the operational impact on patients is severe when side effect data is incomplete or non-standardized [8]. We need granular data on formulary coverage for both brand-name and generic antiretrovirals, prior authorization denial rates disaggregated by insurance type and geographic location, and timelines for generic market entry and formulary integration. The complexities highlighted by CMS's revised RxDC reporting instructions for 2023 [7] underscore the need for a clear, standardized approach that yields actionable insights into access challenges.

**Implementation Timelines: Accelerating Long-Acting Injectable Approvals and Integration**
The establishment of specific implementation timelines is crucial for ensuring the timely and orderly integration of innovative treatments, such as long-acting injectables, into patient care pathways. However, regulatory transitions create windows of uncertainty during which insurance plans may use the compliance changeover as a pretext to restructure formularies, remove specialty tiers, or impose new prior authorization requirements on established HIV medications.

I have personally experienced formulary disruptions mid-year that forced me to switch antiretroviral regimens — not for clinical reasons, but because my insurer reclassified my medication during a plan update cycle. For HIV patients, even brief interruptions in therapy can lead to viral rebound, drug resistance, and long-term treatment failure. Formulary coverage shifts are common; between 2023 and 2024, the number of standalone prescription drug plans (PDPs) and low-income subsidy (LIS) benchmark plans decreased [3], and Part D premium trends varied significantly [4]. The proposed implementation timelines, if not paired with formulary stability requirements, risk triggering exactly this kind of disruption at scale.

Moreover, aggressive implementation timelines may disproportionately burden safety-net and LGBTQ+-affirming health centers that serve HIV patients. These providers operate on thin margins and limited administrative capacity [1, 2]. Simultaneous reporting, documentation, and formulary compliance upgrades may force these entities to prioritize administrative compliance over patient-facing services. This would paradoxically reduce access to care for the patients the standards are meant to protect.

**Documentation Standards: Empowering Patient Involvement in Advisory Committees and Improving Long-Term Safety Monitoring**
The introduction of new documentation standards is essential for generating high-quality, disaggregated data that can effectively inform FDA advisory committee discussions and improve long-term safety monitoring for HIV patients. As someone living with HIV for 20 years, I am acutely aware of how current data gaps limit the quality of information available to advisory committees, making patient input less effective.

The UNAIDS 2024 Global AIDS Update documents that globally, treatment cascade outcomes differ significantly by gender and age [5], yet U.S. domestic safety reporting rarely stratifies long-term adverse event data in ways that capture the unique pharmacological burden carried by people who have been on ART for 10, 15, or 20+ years. My own experience managing drug interactions, metabolic side effects, and formulary access challenges reflects a clinical reality that aggregate, unstratified safety data cannot reveal. Without this granularity, advisory committees lack the full picture of real-world patient experiences, including long-term side effects [8], and access barriers.

Furthermore, poor documentation obscures disparities. The National HIV/AIDS Strategy 2024 Progress Report highlights that HRSA and ACL are actively expanding community hubs to address the complex social determinants affecting long-term survivors [6]. If the agency's new documentation and reporting mandates impose significant administrative costs on these community-based providers without corresponding support, the result will be reduced capacity to serve patients, longer wait times, and potential treatment interruptions — which for HIV patients can lead to viral rebound and drug resistance. As an LGBTQ+ health access advocate, I work closely with these providers and understand their operational challenges. The cost is not just financial but measured in patient health outcomes.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To ensure that these proposed regulatory changes achieve their stated goals of modernizing oversight and improving data quality while simultaneously advancing generic antiretroviral access, accelerating long-acting injectable approvals, and empowering patient involvement in advisory committees, I offer the following specific recommendations:

1.  **For Revised Reporting Obligations:**
    *   **Require disaggregated data on generic ART access:** Mandate reporting on formulary coverage, prior authorization denial rates, and generic market entry timelines, disaggregated by insurance type (e.g., commercial, Medicare, Medicaid, ADAP), geographic location, and HIV status. This will allow the agency to identify systemic access disparities.
    *   **Include patient-reported outcome (PRO) data and real-world evidence:** Require manufacturers and pharmacies to submit structured adverse event data disaggregated by duration of ART use (e.g., 10+ years, 20+ years), enabling detection of cumulative toxicity signals that short-term clinical trials cannot capture [5, 6].
    *   **Coordinate with existing HIV surveillance infrastructure:** Coordinate with HRSA's Ryan White HIV/AIDS Program data infrastructure to avoid duplicative reporting burdens while enriching the safety dataset.

2.  **For Implementation Timelines:**
    *   **Establish explicit formulary continuity protections:** Require that regulated entities (including insurers and pharmacy benefit managers) maintain existing formulary coverage for all currently enrolled patients on chronic specialty medications (including antiretrovirals and long-acting injectables) throughout the full transition period. Prohibit mid-transition formulary changes that would remove, restrict, or impose new utilization management on medications that patients were actively receiving prior to the compliance deadline [3, 4].
    *   **Provide extended timelines for small and safety-net providers:** Adopt a tiered implementation timeline that grants extended compliance deadlines — of at least 18 to 24 months beyond the standard deadline — to federally qualified health centers, Ryan White Part B grantees, and other safety-net providers serving high proportions of low-income patients with HIV [1, 2, 5, 6].

3.  **For Documentation Standards:**
    *   **Mandate disaggregated data for advisory committee review:** Explicitly require that documentation frameworks capture disaggregated data by sexual orientation, gender identity, race/ethnicity, and HIV status, as well as duration of ART use. This is a prerequisite for the agency's stated goal of "enhancing its ability to monitor safety and compliance" and for ensuring that patient advocates on advisory committees have access to comprehensive, actionable data [5, 6].
    *   **Standardize documentation of formulary access decisions:** Require that formulary access decisions and prior authorization outcomes be documented in a standardized, machine-readable format so that patient advocates and researchers can identify systemic denial patterns across payers and states.
    *   **Provide technical assistance and tiered compliance for small providers:** Conduct a formal small-provider impact analysis specifically examining Ryan White-funded and FQHC-based HIV care providers before finalizing documentation standards. Establish a tiered compliance framework that scales documentation requirements to provider size and patient volume, with simplified reporting pathways for safety-net providers. Provide technical assistance funding and template tools to help community-based HIV providers meet new standards without diverting clinical staff time from patient care [1, 2, 5, 6].

4.  **For Patient Involvement in Advisory Committees:**
    *   **Leverage improved data quality to enhance patient participation:** Ensure that the improved data quality resulting from these regulatory changes is used to enhance the effectiveness of patient participation in advisory committees, ensuring data presented is interpretable and actionable for patient advocates.

[CONCLUSION]
In conclusion, I strongly support the agency's proposed suite of compliance requirements, including revised reporting obligations, specific implementation timelines, and rigorous documentation standards. When thoughtfully designed and implemented with explicit consideration for the unique needs of HIV patients, these changes have the potential to significantly improve generic antiretroviral access, accelerate the integration of innovative treatments like long-acting injectables, and empower more meaningful patient involvement in FDA advisory committee processes.

I urge the agency to adopt the specific recommendations outlined above to ensure that these regulatory updates not only meet the agency's stated goals but also translate into real-world benefits for the millions of Americans living with HIV and other chronic conditions.

Thank you for the opportunity to comment on this important proposal.

Sincerely,

Thomas Rivera
HIV Patient Advocate
FDA Patient Representative Program Participant

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
Stryker Corporation (Stryker) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the proposed rule FDA-2024-XXXX, which seeks to modernize and standardize oversight of regulated industries through revised reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards. As a leading global medical technology company, Stryker is dedicated to improving healthcare outcomes and patient safety through innovative products, including orthopedic implants, neurotechnology, spine devices, and hospital equipment. Our operations span over 100 countries, subjecting us to complex and evolving domestic and international regulatory frameworks.

While Stryker supports the agency's overarching goal of enhancing data quality and compliance monitoring, we are deeply concerned that certain provisions of the proposed rule, as currently drafted, will impose disproportionate operational burdens, create conflicts with international regulatory harmonization efforts, and potentially delay patient access to critical medical technologies without commensurate patient safety benefits. We urge the agency to consider the practical implications for large-scale manufacturers operating in a global regulatory environment and to adopt a more risk-proportionate and internationally aligned approach.

[BACKGROUND]
The proposed rule FDA-2024-XXXX introduces new compliance requirements focusing on three primary areas: the revision of existing reporting obligations, the establishment of specific implementation timelines, and the introduction of more rigorous documentation standards. The agency's stated aim is to address perceived gaps in current data collection and record-keeping practices to improve the quality of information received and enhance its ability to monitor safety and compliance.

Stryker operates under a multitude of existing reporting and record-keeping mandates, including post-market surveillance obligations for metal-on-metal (MoM) implants, unique device identification (UDI) system compliance, and extensive quality management system (QMS) documentation requirements under 21 CFR Part 820. Our global footprint further necessitates compliance with international frameworks such as the European Union Medical Device Regulation (EU MDR), the United Kingdom Medical Devices Regulations (UK MDR 2024), and UDI systems in various other jurisdictions. It is within this intricate regulatory landscape that we assess the potential impacts of the proposed changes.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**1. Impact of Revised Reporting Obligations**

Stryker is concerned that the proposed revision to reporting frequency and format, particularly for metal-on-metal (MoM) implant post-market surveillance and reporting for neurotechnology and spine devices, will impose significant operational burdens without commensurate patient safety gains.

*   **Metal-on-Metal Implant Post-Market Surveillance (Requesting international implant registry harmonization):** Stryker currently operates under simultaneous PMS obligations across the U.S., EU MDR, and UK MDR 2024, each with distinct reporting cadences, data formats, and content requirements [1]. The proposed revised reporting obligations—if they alter submission frequency or mandate new data fields inconsistent with existing international frameworks—would require Stryker to maintain parallel reporting pipelines for its MoM hip and joint implant product lines. For example, if the agency mandates patient-level outcome data, metal ion testing results, or imaging findings at prescribed intervals, the cost implications are substantial. The UK regulatory impact assessment for the PMSR 2024 modeled a range of compliance costs across device risk classes, demonstrating that high-risk implant manufacturers bear the steepest compliance cost curves. For MoM implants, the marginal cost of additional mandatory content reporting is not trivial [1]. Stryker estimates that expanded content mandates could require hiring additional clinical data coordinators, renegotiating registry data-sharing agreements, and upgrading UDI-linked data systems, costs the agency's proposal does not appear to have fully modeled [1]. Adding a non-harmonized domestic layer would compound this burden, diverting Stryker's regulatory affairs and clinical teams from substantive safety analysis toward administrative reconciliation. Given Stryker's active post-market surveillance obligations for legacy MoM implant systems—including monitoring for adverse tissue reactions, elevated metal ion levels, and revision rates—any reporting redesign that fragments data collection across incompatible formats risks degrading the quality and timeliness of the very safety signals the agency seeks to improve [1].

*   **Neurotechnology and Spine Devices Manufacturing (Proposing risk-proportionate post-market surveillance):** Stryker's neurotechnology and spine device segment requires highly specialized data collection infrastructure that differs materially from our orthopedic implant or hospital equipment lines. Our neurotechnology products—including deep brain stimulation systems, spinal cord stimulators, and interbody fusion devices—are subject to condition-of-approval post-market studies, Breakthrough Device Program commitments, and MoM implant surveillance obligations simultaneously [6, 7]. Increasing submission frequency or mandating new data formats without harmonizing these requirements with existing obligations would require Stryker to maintain parallel reporting tracks, duplicating staff effort and IT infrastructure investment. Based on our 2024 Form 10-K disclosures, Stryker already dedicates substantial resources to regulatory compliance across more than 75 countries [7], and any uncoordinated domestic reporting change risks creating conflicts with our international regulatory convergence strategy, particularly as we align with EU MDR, Japan PMDA, and China NMPA frameworks that are themselves undergoing modernization [6, 7].

**2. Impact of Documentation Standards**

Stryker is deeply concerned that the proposed documentation standards, as applied to UDI system compliance and hospital bed production, will impose duplicative and disproportionate recordkeeping burdens without commensurate public health benefit.

*   **UDI System Compliance (Supporting UDI system improvements & Requesting international UDI harmonization):** Stryker maintains thousands of active device listings across FDA's GUDID database, spanning implantable orthopedic devices (subject to the earliest and most stringent UDI compliance deadlines under 21 CFR § 801.20) [3], neurosurgical instruments, and hospital furniture. Each product line requires distinct labeling configurations, lot/batch tracking, and GUDID data submissions. Introducing new documentation standards governing how these records are 'created, maintained, and presented' would require Stryker to audit and potentially reformat existing GUDID-linked documentation systems across multiple enterprise resource planning (ERP) platforms, quality management systems (QMS), and labeling software environments [2]. The cost of such system-wide remediation—including IT reconfiguration, staff retraining, and third-party validation—could conservatively reach tens of millions of dollars enterprise-wide [2, 8], costs that are not offset by any new safety benefit given that our GUDID submissions are already subject to FDA's existing data quality optimization initiatives. Furthermore, Stryker sells devices in over 100 countries, many of which have adopted or are adopting their own UDI requirements—including the EU MDR UDI system, Saudi Arabia's SFDA Saudi-DI database, and other national registries. GS1 standards, which Stryker uses as its primary UDI issuing agency, are recognized across the U.S., EU, India, Canada, and other major markets, enabling a degree of documentation reuse. If the agency's new documentation standards deviate from the structure and format recognized by GS1 and international counterparts, Stryker would be compelled to create and maintain parallel documentation sets for the same physical device—one for FDA review and separate versions for EU notified bodies, SFDA, and others [2]. This directly contradicts the international regulatory convergence that Stryker's compliance infrastructure is built around and would increase our regulatory affairs staffing requirements substantially [2].

*   **Hospital Bed and Medical Furniture Production:** Stryker's hospital bed and medical furniture division already maintains extensive documentation infrastructure to satisfy UDI traceability mandates, CMS National Coverage Determination requirements (NCD 280.7), and post-market surveillance obligations for metal-on-metal implant product lines [8]. The proposed documentation standards would require Stryker to build parallel or redundant record-keeping systems for its hospital bed product lines, as the new standards do not appear to be harmonized with existing CMS or UDI documentation architectures [8]. Based on industry benchmarks, quality system implementation and maintenance for a single product line can cost between $30,000 and $80,000 annually, and initial conformity assessment documentation overhauls can reach $50,000–$200,000 [8]. Across Stryker's hospital bed and medical furniture portfolio—which spans multiple product families and manufacturing facilities—the aggregate compliance cost burden could reach into the millions of dollars annually, diverting capital from product innovation and patient safety improvements [8].

**3. Impact of Implementation Timelines**

Stryker is deeply concerned that the proposed implementation timelines, as currently structured, will force the company to make domestic compliance investments that directly conflict with—and potentially undermine—ongoing international regulatory convergence efforts, creating a bifurcated compliance architecture that increases costs without improving patient safety outcomes.

*   **International Regulatory Convergence Needs (Requesting international implant registry harmonization & Supporting UDI system improvements):** As a global manufacturer of orthopedic implants, neurotechnology devices, and surgical equipment operating under MoM post-market surveillance obligations and UDI system requirements across dozens of jurisdictions, Stryker must simultaneously satisfy FDA requirements, EU MDR/IVDR mandates, and emerging frameworks from regulators such as ANVISA, Health Canada, and PMDA Japan [4, 5]. The proposed timelines do not account for the sequencing of these overlapping international obligations. If Stryker is required to build out domestic reporting infrastructure, documentation systems, and compliance workflows on the agency's proposed schedule, those investments may need to be rebuilt or substantially modified within 12–24 months as international convergence frameworks—currently under active development through IMDRF, APEC, and bilateral FDA harmonization programs—mature and establish different technical standards [4, 5]. This creates a scenario where Stryker incurs duplicative capital expenditures estimated in the tens of millions of dollars across its global regulatory affairs, IT, and quality systems functions [4, 5]. Stryker's compliance obligations are not discrete or modular—they are deeply interdependent across jurisdictions. Our UDI system, for example, must simultaneously satisfy FDA's GUDID database requirements, the EU EUDAMED system, and emerging UDI frameworks in Brazil (RDC 848/2024) and other markets [4, 5]. Each major regulatory transition requiring system-level changes to our quality management, labeling, and post-market surveillance infrastructure requires 18–30 months of lead time and involves cross-functional teams spanning regulatory affairs, IT, manufacturing, and legal [4, 5]. Compressing these timelines, or stacking them against concurrent international deadlines, creates resource bottlenecks that increase error risk, divert engineering resources from product development, and may delay the introduction of new orthopedic and neurotechnology innovations to U.S. patients [4, 5]. The global regulatory compliance market is projected at over $18 billion in 2024, reflecting the enormous resource burden already placed on industry—the agency should not add to this burden through avoidable timeline misalignment [4, 5].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
Stryker respectfully urges the agency to consider the following recommendations to ensure that the final rule is practical, risk-proportionate, internationally harmonized, and ultimately beneficial for public health without hindering innovation:

**Recommendations for Revised Reporting Obligations (Implant Registry Harmonization & Risk-Proportionate PMS):**
*   **For MoM Implant Post-Market Surveillance:**
    *   Align the revised reporting format and content requirements with internationally recognized standards, specifically the IMDRF PMS framework and the EU MDR Annex III PSUR structure, rather than creating a standalone domestic schema [1].
    *   Convene a technical working group inclusive of large manufacturers with multi-jurisdictional obligations to develop a harmonized data submission template [1].
    *   Explicitly permit manufacturers to satisfy domestic MoM PMS reporting obligations by submitting conforming copies of MDR-compliant Periodic Safety Update Reports (PSURs), supplemented only by U.S.-specific adverse event data fields [1].
    *   For expanded content mandates, publish a detailed regulatory impact analysis disaggregated by device risk class and manufacturer size before finalizing any content expansion requirements [1].
    *   Establish a tiered content reporting framework in which manufacturers with existing, validated registry participation (e.g., the American Joint Replacement Registry) may satisfy expanded content requirements through registry data submission rather than duplicative direct reporting [1].
    *   Provide a minimum 24-month implementation runway for any new content mandates affecting MoM implant surveillance, consistent with the time required to renegotiate data-sharing agreements and upgrade UDI-linked reporting systems [1].
*   **For Neurotechnology and Spine Devices Manufacturing:**
    *   Conduct and publish a formal cost-benefit analysis specifically disaggregated by device category—including neurotechnology and spine devices—before finalizing any frequency or format changes [6, 7].
    *   Grandfather existing post-market study reporting commitments made under Breakthrough Device Program approvals and condition-of-approval orders so that manufacturers are not required to reformat or re-submit data already being collected under agency-approved protocols [6, 7].
    *   Convene a technical working group inclusive of large, multi-segment manufacturers like Stryker to develop a unified electronic submission template that satisfies the new requirements while remaining compatible with international regulatory standards such as IMDRF guidelines, thereby reducing duplicative compliance infrastructure [6, 7].

**Recommendations for Documentation Standards (UDI Improvements & Harmonization):**
*   **For UDI System Compliance:**
    *   Explicitly grandfather documentation formats that already satisfy current GUDID submission standards and 21 CFR Part 801 labeling requirements [3].
    *   New documentation standards should apply prospectively only to new device registrations or material changes to existing listings, not to the entire installed base of compliant records [3].
    *   Publish a detailed cost-benefit analysis—disaggregated by device class and company size—before finalizing any documentation standard that touches UDI records, as required by Executive Order 12866 [3].
*   **For International UDI Harmonization:**
    *   Align any new documentation standards with the IMDRF (International Medical Device Regulators Forum) UDI guidance and GS1 data structures already in use globally [2].
    *   Convene a public workshop with industry and international regulatory counterparts before finalizing documentation standards, and commit to accepting documentation formatted to IMDRF-harmonized standards as presumptively compliant [2].
    *   Clarify in the final rule that documentation meeting EU MDR UDI requirements satisfies parallel FDA documentation obligations wherever substantively equivalent [2].
*   **For Hospital Bed and Medical Furniture Production:**
    *   Conduct a formal cost-benefit analysis that accounts for the existing documentation obligations already imposed on hospital bed manufacturers through CMS, UDI, and FDA Quality System Regulation frameworks before finalizing any new documentation standards [8].
    *   Adopt a 'documentation convergence' approach that explicitly recognizes and credits compliance with existing federal record-keeping regimes, rather than requiring manufacturers to maintain separate, non-interoperable documentation systems [8].
    *   Publish a crosswalk document mapping the proposed standards against existing CMS and FDA requirements so that manufacturers can identify true incremental obligations versus duplicative ones [8].

**Recommendations for Implementation Timelines (UDI Improvements & International Harmonization):**
*   **For International Regulatory Convergence:**
    *   Extend the proposed implementation timelines by a minimum of 24–36 months and explicitly phase compliance milestones to align with the IMDRF work plan cycle and FDA's own International Harmonization Annual Assessment process [4, 5].
    *   Establish a 'convergence checkpoint' mechanism—a formal review period prior to each compliance deadline—at which the agency evaluates whether the domestic standard has been harmonized with leading international frameworks [4, 5]. If convergence has been achieved or is imminent, the agency should allow manufacturers to implement the harmonized standard in lieu of a purely domestic one [4, 5].
*   **For UDI System Improvements (Interdependency with Timelines):**
    *   Publish a revised regulatory impact analysis that explicitly models the interaction between proposed domestic timelines and known international compliance deadlines (EU MDR, ANVISA RDC 848/2024, IMDRF guidance cycles) [4, 5].
    *   Establish a tiered implementation schedule that grants additional transition time to manufacturers demonstrating active participation in multi-jurisdictional compliance programs [4, 5].
    *   Consider accepting compliance with a recognized international harmonized standard as satisfying the domestic requirement during a defined transition window, reducing duplicative investment while maintaining safety oversight [4, 5].

[CONCLUSION]
Stryker is committed to partnering with the agency to develop a regulatory framework that effectively enhances data quality and compliance monitoring without imposing unnecessary burdens that could stifle innovation and delay patient access to life-changing medical technologies. We believe that by adopting a risk-proportionate approach, leveraging existing international harmonization efforts, and conducting thorough cost-benefit analyses, the agency can achieve its objectives while minimizing the operational and economic impact on regulated entities.

We appreciate the agency's consideration of these comments and welcome the opportunity to engage further on these critical issues.

### References
[1] https://deviceassessment.kaiserpermanente.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/2024_External_Annual_Report_MDSA.pdf
[2] https://www.sxanpro.com/post/udi-in-healthcare-what-hospitals-need-to-know-about-unique-device-identification
[3] https://www.fda.gov/medical-devices/unique-device-identification-system-udi-system/udi-compliance-policies-and-udi-rule-compliance-dates
[4] https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43441-025-00817-8
[5] https://globalregulatorypartners.com/global-convergence-and-predictability-anvisa-resets-the-game-for-medical-devices-and-the-2024-2025-regulatory-agenda/
[6] https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/310764/000031076424000024/syk-20231231.htm
[7] https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/310764/000031076425000023/syk-20241231.htm
[8] https://www.starodub.nl/blog/what-are-the-costs-of-medical-device-regulatory-compliance/
