comment_text:
{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
As a pharmaceutical policy researcher who has spent years studying drug pricing transparency, accelerated approval outcomes, and generic drug competition, I strongly support the revised reporting obligations, documentation standards, and implementation timelines proposed by the agency. These critical regulatory updates are essential for addressing persistent data gaps that have long hampered independent academic research, limited public accountability of regulatory processes, and ultimately hindered evidence-based policymaking in the pharmaceutical sector. My comments focus on how these proposed changes directly benefit the evaluation of accelerated approval pathways, the analysis of drug pricing barriers, and the promotion of robust generic competition.

[BACKGROUND]
The agency's proposal introduces a suite of new compliance requirements, specifically focusing on the revision of existing reporting obligations, the establishment of implementation timelines, and the introduction of more rigorous documentation standards. These changes are intended to address perceived gaps in current data collection and record-keeping practices, thereby enhancing the quality of information available for monitoring safety and compliance. For independent academic researchers like myself, these proposed changes represent a pivotal opportunity to improve access to structured, timely, and comprehensive data, which is fundamental for conducting rigorous, public-interest research that complements the agency's own oversight capacity.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
My research program on drug pricing and accelerated approval policy is routinely constrained by the inconsistency and incompleteness of currently available submission data. For example, when evaluating the downstream pricing effects of accelerated approvals, I must manually harmonize data across multiple state transparency programs—such as Minnesota's expanded reporting dashboard (which recorded over 2,600 visits in 2023, indicating high public demand for this information) and California's revised CTRx regulations effective April 1, 2024—because federal-level data lacks the granularity and standardization these state programs are attempting to fill [1]. Revised federal reporting obligations that standardize format and content would dramatically reduce this reconciliation burden and allow my team to redirect resources toward substantive policy analysis rather than data cleaning. This directly responds to the agency's request for comment on operational impacts: for academic researchers, the operational impact of the status quo is a persistent drag on research productivity and policy relevance.

My research group's ability to evaluate accelerated approval policy outcomes is severely constrained by the current inconsistency in how sponsors document surrogate endpoint justifications, confirmatory trial status, and post-approval commitments. When records are submitted in heterogeneous formats—varying across sponsors, therapeutic areas, and approval vintages—systematic analysis requires enormous manual data harmonization effort that consumes research resources and introduces coding error. The new documentation standards would directly reduce this burden, enabling faster, more reproducible policy evaluations [2, 3]. For instance, my co-authored work examining accelerated approval drugs required painstaking reconstruction of trial timelines from disparate docket submissions precisely because no uniform documentation standard existed.

My research on generic drug competition relies on reconstructing market entry timelines, pricing dynamics, and competitive responses from regulatory filings and publicly available agency data. Current inconsistencies in how firms report ANDA submissions, patent certifications, and exclusivity periods create significant gaps in the analytical record. Research using IMS Health National Sales Perspectives data to track the 257 NMEs experiencing initial generic entry between 1995 and 2012 required extensive manual reconciliation precisely because reporting standards were inconsistent across that period. Standardized documentation requirements, implemented on a clear timeline, would directly improve the reliability and reproducibility of this type of competitive analysis going forward [4, 5].

My peer-reviewed work on accelerated approval has documented that the agency has historically lacked timely, publicly accessible data to monitor whether manufacturers are fulfilling their post-market study commitments. The revised reporting obligations present a direct opportunity to close this gap. Currently, when I attempt to evaluate whether accelerated approvals are delivering on their clinical promise, I face significant data access limitations: manufacturers are not required to submit confirmatory trial progress data in standardized, publicly accessible formats, forcing me to rely on ClinicalTrials.gov entries that are often incomplete or delayed. Improved content standards in the revised reporting framework—specifically requiring structured, periodic updates on post-market study milestones—would enable the kind of independent academic oversight that strengthens public confidence in the accelerated approval pathway and informs congressional and agency policy decisions.

As an academic researcher operating under peer-review and publication standards, I am constrained to working with publicly available data. The pharmaceutical industry data access limitations my group faces mean that even if sponsors submit beautifully standardized records to FDA, those records remain inaccessible to us unless the agency simultaneously strengthens its public disclosure practices [8, 9]. My research on accelerated approval drug withdrawals and confirmatory trial delays—work that has directly shaped legislative debate—has depended on painstakingly assembling public docket materials. If the new documentation standards create a two-tier information environment where FDA has rich structured data but the public record remains sparse, the agency will have improved its own monitoring capacity while inadvertently weakening the independent research ecosystem that provides a check on both industry and agency performance.

My research on accelerated approval policy and generic competition has consistently found that the number of generic competitors entering a market is the primary driver of price reductions for consumers. Complex generic products—which represent the highest-growth segment of the generic pipeline—already carry development costs of $1 million to $5 million and timelines of 18 to 36 months before a single ANDA is filed [4, 5]. Imposing new documentation and reporting compliance burdens on an aggressive timeline disproportionately affects smaller generic manufacturers who lack dedicated regulatory affairs departments, potentially chilling ANDA filings precisely in the complex generic space where competition is most needed and least robust. This is a cost the agency's proposal does not appear to have quantified.

My research on accelerated approval policy, drug pricing, and generic competition is directly constrained by the current patchwork of data submission requirements, which allow manufacturers to submit aggregated or selectively formatted data that obscures the granular clinical and commercial information needed for independent evaluation. For example, when evaluating whether accelerated approval confirmatory trials are being completed on schedule, or whether generic entry is being suppressed through strategic pricing, I am forced to rely on incomplete public disclosures or costly data licensing arrangements that are often unavailable to academic researchers without industry funding. Standardized, more granular data submissions—analogous to the 'most granular level' requirement introduced in the RxDC pharmacy benefit reporting context—would directly improve the quality and completeness of the data available for independent policy analysis [6, 7].

My research program depends on accessing and synthesizing regulatory submission data alongside peer-reviewed literature to evaluate policies such as accelerated approval pathways and generic drug competition. When documentation standards for regulatory review diverge from academic publication standards—in terms of data structure, variable definitions, outcome reporting formats, and metadata conventions—it creates a significant translation burden for independent researchers like me. I must currently spend substantial time reconciling inconsistently formatted regulatory records with published trial data, which delays publication timelines and introduces potential for transcription error. The proposed new documentation standards, if designed without reference to established scientific reporting guidelines (e.g., CONSORT, PRISMA, STROBE), risk deepening this incompatibility rather than resolving it [8, 9].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To maximize the public health and research benefits of this proposal, I offer the following specific recommendations:

1.  **Revised Reporting Obligations (Drug Pricing Transparency):** The agency should adopt a machine-readable, standardized data schema for all revised reporting submissions—modeled on the CBER-CDER Data Standards Program's eCTD and FHIR implementation frameworks—and make the resulting datasets publicly accessible in a structured format. Specifically, the agency should require that pricing, clinical outcomes, and post-market surveillance data be submitted in interoperable formats compatible with real-world data standards articulated in FDA's December 2023 guidance on RWD submissions. Public release of anonymized, aggregated submission data should be an explicit component of the revised reporting framework, not an afterthought, so that independent researchers can conduct the kind of oversight analysis that complements the agency's own monitoring capacity. This will reduce the reconciliation burden for academic researchers and allow redirection of resources toward substantive policy analysis.

2.  **Documentation Standards (Accelerated Approval Confirmatory Trials):** The agency should ensure that the new documentation standards require sponsors to submit confirmatory trial status updates, surrogate endpoint validation data, and post-market commitment milestones in structured, publicly accessible, machine-readable formats (e.g., standardized XML or JSON schemas deposited in Regulations.gov dockets). The agency should also mandate that these structured records be linked to the relevant accelerated approval docket numbers so that independent researchers can systematically retrieve and analyze them without relying on FOIA requests or proprietary industry databases. This will enable faster, more reproducible policy evaluations and reduce the manual data harmonization effort currently required.

3.  **Implementation Timelines (Generic Drug Competition):** The agency should establish a phased but binding implementation timeline of no more than 18-24 months for full compliance with the new reporting and documentation standards, with interim milestones at 6 and 12 months. Critically, the agency should publish a public-facing data dictionary and schema for the standardized fields so that academic researchers—who lack the industry data access that well-resourced pharmaceutical firms enjoy—can systematically use the resulting regulatory filings for independent policy analysis. The agency should also require that historical data submitted under old formats be cross-walked to the new standards for a defined lookback period, preserving longitudinal comparability. This will improve the reliability and reproducibility of competitive analysis and avoid analytical gaps.

4.  **Revised Reporting Obligations (Accelerated Approval Post-Market Outcomes):** The agency should explicitly include post-market confirmatory study progress reporting as a required content element in the revised reporting obligations, with submissions due on a semi-annual basis and made publicly available in machine-readable format within 30 days of receipt. This is consistent with the improved reporting compliance the agency documented following CARES Act reporting guidance (FY2024 FDA Pharmaceutical Quality Report), demonstrating that clear, mandatory reporting requirements with defined timelines produce measurable compliance improvements. The agency should also align these content standards with its existing real-world data submission guidance to ensure that post-market evidence generated outside traditional trials can be incorporated into the reporting framework, reducing the evidentiary burden on manufacturers while improving data richness for regulators and researchers alike. This will close the gap in monitoring manufacturer compliance with post-market study commitments and enable independent academic oversight.

5.  **Documentation Standards (Public Disclosure for Research):** In response to the agency's specific request for comments on alternatives: the agency should adopt a parallel public disclosure requirement alongside the new internal documentation standards. Specifically, the agency should require that a standardized summary record—including confirmatory trial registration numbers, enrollment status, projected completion dates, and surrogate-to-clinical endpoint validation milestones—be posted to a publicly accessible FDA database updated at least semi-annually. This is consistent with the transparency framework already established under ClinicalTrials.gov reporting requirements and would impose minimal additional burden on sponsors who are already compiling this information for internal FDA submission. This will prevent a two-tier information environment and strengthen the independent research ecosystem.

6.  **Implementation Timelines (Avoiding Disadvantage to Small Generics):** In response to the agency's specific request for comments on expected costs and operational impacts, I recommend the agency conduct and publish a size-stratified cost analysis distinguishing compliance burdens for large integrated generic manufacturers versus smaller or mid-size ANDA filers. The agency should consider a tiered implementation timeline—for example, 12 months for firms with more than 50 pending ANDAs and 24 months for firms with fewer—to prevent the new standards from functioning as a de facto barrier to entry in complex generic markets. The agency should also provide no-cost compliance assistance, such as standardized templates and guidance documents, to reduce the fixed cost burden on smaller filers. This will avoid disproportionately affecting smaller generic manufacturers and prevent new standards from being a barrier to entry.

7.  **Revised Reporting Obligations (Closing Information Asymmetry):** The agency should adopt the revised reporting obligations as proposed, and should additionally require that all newly standardized data submissions be made available in machine-readable, publicly accessible formats to the maximum extent permitted by law. Specifically, the agency should establish a structured public data repository—modeled on existing CMS data transparency initiatives—where de-identified, granular submission data can be accessed by independent researchers under appropriate data use agreements. This would allow academic researchers like myself to conduct the kind of rigorous, peer-reviewed analysis that informs evidence-based regulatory policy without dependence on industry-provided data. This will close information asymmetry and enable rigorous independent analysis without industry dependence.

8.  **Documentation Standards (Aligning with Peer Review):** The agency should explicitly cross-reference its new documentation standards with established peer-review reporting guidelines and open-science frameworks endorsed by OSTP's 2022 Public Access Memorandum, which recognized the growing role of preprints and standardized data sharing in scholarly communication [8, 9]. Specifically, the agency should: (1) require that clinical and safety data submitted under the new standards use variable naming conventions and outcome definitions consistent with those used in peer-reviewed publications; (2) mandate machine-readable, structured data formats that are compatible with systematic review and meta-analytic workflows; and (3) establish a public-comment mechanism through which academic researchers can flag documentation format conflicts as they arise post-implementation. This will reduce the translation burden for researchers and avoid a bifurcated evidentiary ecosystem.

[CONCLUSION]
In conclusion, the proposed revisions to reporting obligations, documentation standards, and implementation timelines are vital for enhancing the rigor, transparency, and accountability of pharmaceutical regulation. As a pharmaceutical policy researcher, I strongly support these changes as they represent a significant step toward closing critical data gaps that have impeded independent academic analysis of accelerated approval outcomes, drug pricing barriers, and generic competition dynamics. By adopting the recommendations outlined above—particularly those focused on public data accessibility and alignment with established research standards—the agency can ensure that these new requirements not only improve its internal oversight but also empower the broader research community to contribute to evidence-based policy development and public health.

### References
[1] https://www.health.state.mn.us/data/rxtransparency/docs/rxlegrpt2024.pdf
[2] https://www.mcguirewoods.com/client-resources/alerts/2025/1/rethinking-fdas-accelerated-approval-pathway-new-draft-guidances-and-implications-for-drug-companies/
[3] https://www.whitecase.com/insight-alert/accelerated-approval-confirmatory-trials-requirement-may-further-complicate-life
[4] https://www.drugpatentwatch.com/blog/how-to-manage-generic-drug-development-timelines-strategies-for-successv/

[6] https://www.schwartzinsgrp.com/blog/hhs-releases-updated-drug-data-collection-rxdc-reporting-instructions-in
[7] https://info.usi.com/rs/121-VCO-807/images/2024_RxDC_Reporting_Reminder_May_8_2025.pdf?version=0

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The Organic Trade Association (OTA) appreciates the opportunity to comment on the agency's proposed compliance requirements designed to modernize and standardize oversight of its regulated industry (FDA-2024-XXXX). As the leading trade association representing the entire organic supply chain—from small family farms and processors to mid-sized handlers and retailers—OTA strongly supports the rule's overarching goals of improving data quality, enhancing transparency, and strengthening the integrity of regulated industries. Our diverse membership, which includes thousands of certified organic operations nationwide, depends on robust oversight and consistent compliance to maintain the credibility of the 'USDA Organic' seal, a market asset now valued at over $67 billion in annual U.S. sales. We believe that well-designed reporting and documentation standards are essential tools for safeguarding consumer trust and ensuring fair competition within the organic sector.

[BACKGROUND]
The proposed rule focuses on three primary areas: (1) Revised Reporting Obligations, (2) Implementation Timelines, and (3) Documentation Standards. While not specifically targeting the organic industry, these provisions will directly impact certified organic entities already subject to rigorous record-keeping and reporting requirements under the USDA National Organic Program (NOP), particularly those outlined in 7 CFR Part 205 and the recently implemented Strengthening Organic Enforcement (SOE) Final Rule. The SOE rule, which took effect March 19, 2024, established new mandates for organic supply chain integrity, including updated import certificates, expanded certification scope, and enhanced traceability documentation [1], [2]. Layering new compliance requirements on top of this existing framework, especially without explicit coordination with USDA, poses unique challenges and potential duplicative burdens for organic operations. OTA's comments will address the interplay between the proposed rule's provisions and the specific needs and operational realities of the organic industry, with a particular focus on protecting organic labeling integrity, ensuring fair treatment for small organic producers, and advocating for seamless interagency coordination.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
1.  **Revised Reporting Obligations and Organic Supply Chain Integrity:**
    OTA strongly supports the agency's objective of standardizing and improving the quality of data submissions. In the organic sector, inconsistent and incomplete reporting has historically created vulnerabilities for fraud, undermining the credibility of the USDA organic seal and harming legitimate organic producers. The USDA's Strengthening Organic Enforcement (SOE) Final Rule already established a framework for reducing uncertified entities in the supply chain and enhancing traceability [1], [2]. Revised reporting obligations that standardize data submission content would directly reinforce SOE's goals by closing information gaps that bad actors exploit. OTA's member base spans the entire organic supply chain, from small family farms to mid-size processors and retailers. Our members have long suffered competitive harm from fraudulent organic claims enabled by inconsistent and incomplete data submissions to certifying agents and regulators. Without uniform data standards, our members who invest significantly in genuine organic certification are undercut by operators who exploit reporting ambiguities. This standardization is critical to protecting the $67 billion organic market from fraud [1], [2].

2.  **Impact of Revised Reporting Obligations on Small Organic Producers:**
    While we support modernized data collection, OTA urges the agency to calibrate revised reporting obligations to align with the existing annual certification cycle already embedded in National Organic Program (NOP) requirements, rather than imposing additional or more frequent submission windows that would impose disproportionate compliance costs on small organic producers. OTA's membership is heavily composed of small organic producers who already navigate a demanding annual reporting cycle under 7 CFR Part 205, including the preparation and submission of Organic System Plans (OSPs), on-site inspection documentation, and multi-year recordkeeping obligations (records must be maintained for no less than 3 years per 7 CFR § 205) [3]. Any increase in reporting frequency—such as quarterly submissions—would require these operations to dedicate additional staff time, hire consultants, or invest in new data management systems, costs that are not offset by existing support mechanisms. The USDA Organic Certification Cost Share Program (OCCSP) currently reimburses up to 75% of certification costs, capped at $750 per operation, and is funded at $8 million annually [8]. This program was not designed to absorb the incremental administrative burden of more frequent or reformatted reporting obligations, meaning small producers would bear new net costs with no corresponding relief mechanism [3], [8].

3.  **Documentation Standards and Trace-back Verification:**
    OTA strongly supports the proposed documentation standards as a necessary and overdue modernization of organic supply chain oversight. Rigorous trace-back verification requirements are the single most effective operational tool for detecting and deterring organic fraud. The existing patchwork of documentation practices has created exploitable gaps, particularly at import points and among uncertified handlers [4], [5]. The SOE rule's implementation in March 2024 already demonstrated that standardized documentation requirements—including audit trail documentation and supply chain traceability audits—are operationally achievable: OTA's own regulatory affairs team tracked 2,991 new certification applications in 2024 as supply chain actors came into compliance, demonstrating industry capacity to absorb these requirements [4]. Stronger documentation standards under the current proposal would extend this integrity framework further, protecting the price premium that sustains our small organic producer membership.

4.  **Implementation Timelines and USDA-FDA Regulatory Coordination:**
    OTA's membership includes thousands of small and mid-sized organic producers and handlers who operate under dual USDA National Organic Program (NOP) certification requirements and FDA labeling and food safety obligations. When new documentation standards are introduced without adequate lead time, these operators face simultaneous compliance burdens across two regulatory frameworks. The 2023 USDA Strengthening Organic Enforcement (SOE) rule already required significant documentation overhauls — including new import certificates, expanded certification scope, and updated recordkeeping for supply chain actors — and many of our smaller members are still absorbing those compliance costs [7]. Layering new FDA-coordinated reporting requirements on top of an already-stressed compliance infrastructure, on a compressed timeline, risks forcing small certified organic operations out of the market entirely, shrinking the certified organic supply base and undermining the very supply chain integrity the rule seeks to protect [7]. FDA and USDA have a precedent for interagency cooperation, as evidenced by their joint efforts on food loss and waste reduction [6].

5.  **Exclusion of Synthetic Ingredients for Organic Integrity:**
    While the proposed rule focuses on compliance requirements, its stated goal to "modernize and standardize oversight" and "improve quality of information" directly intersects with the fundamental integrity of the USDA organic standard, which rigorously excludes most synthetic ingredients. The integrity of the organic standard, which this rule's oversight mechanisms should support, fundamentally relies on the exclusion of most synthetic ingredients. Allowing synthetics would complicate reporting and documentation, creating ambiguity and undermining the rule's goal of "improving the quality of information" by blurring the lines between truly organic and non-organic components. It would also increase the operational burden of tracking and verifying compliance if exceptions are made. OTA emphasizes that any revised reporting obligations or documentation standards must be designed to uphold this core principle by clearly differentiating and tracking organic vs. non-organic components, without creating loopholes for synthetic inclusion that would complicate oversight and erode consumer trust.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
Based on the analysis above, OTA respectfully submits the following recommendations:

1.  **Align Revised Reporting Obligations with SOE Final Rule:** The agency should align the revised data submission content requirements explicitly with the SOE Final Rule's existing compliance architecture, ensuring that new reporting fields capture transaction-level supply chain data — including certificates of organic operation, organic system plan references, and handler-to-handler transfer documentation [1], [2]. The agency should also establish a centralized, interoperable data repository accessible to certifying agents so that submitted data can be cross-referenced in real time, rather than siloed within individual certification bodies.
2.  **Synchronize Reporting Frequency with NOP Certification Cycle:** The agency should explicitly synchronize any new reporting obligations with the existing annual NOP certification renewal cycle to avoid duplicative administrative burdens. Furthermore, the agency should conduct a formal small-entity cost analysis disaggregated by operation size before finalizing any increase in reporting frequency. If more frequent reporting is deemed necessary, provide a phased implementation timeline of no less than 24 months and expand OCCSP reimbursement caps or create a dedicated administrative cost-share mechanism to offset the new burden on small producers [3], [8].
3.  **Tiered Implementation for Documentation Standards:** The agency should finalize the proposed documentation standards without weakening amendments, but should provide tiered implementation timelines that give small certified operations (under $250,000 gross organic sales annually) an additional 12-18 months to achieve full compliance [8]. The agency should also issue standardized record-keeping templates and guidance documents—modeled on the NOP's existing SOE compliance materials—to reduce the per-operator cost of designing compliant systems from scratch [3].
4.  **Establish Formal USDA-FDA Regulatory Coordination:** The agency should establish a minimum 24-month phased implementation timeline for any new documentation standards that intersect with USDA NOP certification records, with small operations (under $1M annual organic sales) receiving an additional 12-month compliance extension [7]. Prior to the compliance deadline, a formal USDA-FDA interagency working group should be convened to harmonize documentation templates so that a single record can satisfy both agencies' requirements. Clear guidance should be published on how new FDA reporting fields map to existing NOP Organic System Plan documentation to eliminate duplicative recordkeeping burdens [6].
5.  **Uphold Organic Integrity by Excluding Synthetic Ingredients:** The agency should ensure that the implementation of revised reporting obligations and documentation standards upholds the fundamental integrity of the USDA organic standard by rigorously excluding synthetic ingredients not explicitly permitted under NOP regulations. Any reporting or documentation framework must be designed to clearly differentiate and track organic vs. non-organic components, without creating loopholes for synthetic inclusion that would complicate oversight and erode consumer trust.

[CONCLUSION]
OTA reiterates its strong support for the agency's efforts to modernize and standardize compliance requirements. We believe that the recommendations outlined above will help ensure that these new mandates effectively enhance oversight and data quality without inadvertently imposing disproportionate burdens on the diverse membership of the organic industry, particularly the small and mid-sized producers who form its backbone. We urge the agency to collaborate closely with USDA to ensure a coordinated, seamless, and equitable implementation process that protects the integrity of the organic seal and sustains the continued growth of the U.S. organic market.

[End of Comment]

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
Dear Agency Stakeholders,

My name is Patricia Adams, and I am an elderly Medicare patient living on a fixed income. I manage seven prescription medications daily for chronic conditions including hypertension, diabetes, and arthritis. I am writing to express my strong support for the agency's proposed suite of new compliance requirements, FDA-2024-XXXX. However, I urge the agency to ensure these standards are designed with the unique needs and vulnerabilities of elderly patients like me at the forefront, particularly regarding medication accessibility, safety, and affordability.

[BACKGROUND]
The proposed rule outlines three primary areas of change: the revision of existing reporting obligations, the establishment of specific implementation timelines for new mandates, and the introduction of more rigorous documentation standards. As someone whose health and financial stability depend entirely on safe and affordable access to my medications, I see these changes as a critical opportunity to address long-standing patient safety gaps. My comments will focus on how these provisions can be strengthened to ensure larger font drug labeling, facilitate generic drug availability, and simplify medication guides for elderly patients.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**1. Larger Font Drug Labeling (Documentation Standards)**
The "introduction of more rigorous documentation standards" represents a vital opportunity to mandate accessible drug labeling. Currently, the labels on many of my pill bottles use print so small that I cannot reliably distinguish dosage instructions, expiration dates, or drug names without a magnifying glass. This is not a minor inconvenience; it is a patient safety crisis. When I misread a label, I risk taking the wrong dose or confusing two medications that look similar. Research confirms that medication errors among seniors commonly involve taking wrong doses, missing doses, and confusing similar-looking medications, and that complex multi-drug regimens dramatically amplify these risks [1]. One of my medications is a blood thinner, and a substitution error or missed interaction warning could cause a fall or internal bleeding. While the agency proposes new documentation standards to govern how records are created and maintained for regulatory review, these standards must explicitly extend to patient-facing labeling materials, not just internal records.

**2. Facilitating Generic Drug Availability (Revised Reporting Obligations)**
The "revision of existing reporting obligations" for entities, particularly Medicare Part D plan sponsors, is crucial for elderly patients like me. Every year, I face the anxiety of annual formulary changes that can remove or tier-shift one or more of my seven prescriptions with little advance warning. When a drug moves from Tier 2 to Tier 3, my out-of-pocket cost can jump by hundreds of dollars per month – a devastating shock on a fixed Social Security income. Currently, I often learn about formulary changes only after they take effect, leaving me scrambling to request exceptions or switch plans during a limited enrollment window. More frequent and standardized data submissions from Part D sponsors to CMS [6], [7] would allow the agency to detect these shifts earlier and give beneficiaries like me more lead time to adjust, thus helping me navigate formulary changes to find affordable (often generic) medication options.

**3. Simplified Medication Guides (Documentation Standards and Implementation Timelines)**
The "introduction of more rigorous documentation standards" must also address the need for simplified medication guides that clearly explain drug interactions and potential risks. Managing seven medications means I face an exponentially complex web of potential drug interactions. My pharmacist does her best, but the documentation she receives from manufacturers is dense, technical, and not designed for patients. The WHO's Medication Safety in Polypharmacy report identifies that patient data entry and documentation burdens contribute directly to unsafe medication practices, and the Beers Criteria – a tool specifically designed to flag inappropriate medications for older adults – is rarely communicated to patients in accessible form [1]. Without clear, standardized documentation of my full medication list and interaction risks, I have no reliable way to know whether a formulary substitution introduces a dangerous interaction.

In response to the agency's specific question about operational impacts: my primary care physician already spends a significant portion of our 15-minute appointments navigating electronic health record screens rather than talking with me about my medications [4]. If new documentation standards add more required fields or reporting obligations for my doctor's office, I fear our already-limited medication review time will shrink further [5]. A missed medication interaction review is not an abstract compliance failure; for a 74-year-old woman on blood pressure medication and a diuretic, it is a fall waiting to happen [2], [3]. If the "establishment of specific implementation timelines" forces rapid formulary restructuring without a corresponding requirement for interaction screening, I could be caught in a prescribing cascade – where additional drugs are prescribed to treat the adverse effects of other drugs, misinterpreted as new conditions – potentially leading to a fall, hospitalization, and costs that far exceed any savings the regulation generates [2], [3].

The agency specifically requested comments on "expected costs." From my perspective as a Medicare patient on a fixed income, the relevant costs are not just the compliance costs to pharmacies or insurers; they are the costs I bear when a fall occurs because interaction monitoring was not in place. Research in PMC on polypharmacy in elderly populations documents that consequences include "drug interactions, cognitive impairment, weight loss and malnutrition, increased risk of falls" and other serious harms [1]. A fall requiring hospitalization can cost tens of thousands of dollars, much of which falls on Medicare and on me through cost-sharing.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To ensure the proposed rule effectively protects and supports elderly Medicare patients like me, I offer the following recommendations:

*   **For Larger Font Drug Labeling (Documentation Standards):** The agency should expand its "documentation standards" to explicitly require that all patient-facing drug labeling and accompanying written materials use a minimum 12-point font (preferably 14-point for primary dosing instructions), high-contrast color schemes, and plain-language summaries free of medical jargon. The agency should also require that manufacturers certify compliance with these readability standards as part of their documentation submissions. In response to the agency's specific question on alternatives, a tiered compliance timeline should be considered – with large manufacturers required to comply within 12 months and smaller manufacturers within 24 months – to make implementation feasible without delaying patient safety benefits.
*   **For Facilitating Generic Drug Availability (Revised Reporting Obligations):** The agency should require Medicare Part D plan sponsors to submit formulary change data on at least a quarterly basis – not just annually – and mandate that submissions include a standardized, machine-readable beneficiary impact field that flags any drug removal or tier change affecting more than a defined threshold of enrollees. CMS should then be required to publish a plain-language beneficiary alert derived from that data within 30 days of each submission cycle. This directly addresses the agency's question on operational impacts: the operational burden on plans is modest compared to the real-world harm to patients who cannot absorb sudden cost increases.
*   **For Simplified Medication Guides (Documentation Standards and Implementation Timelines):**
    *   In response to the agency's question on operational impacts, I recommend the agency require that "documentation standards" include a mandatory 'Patient Safety Summary' section for any drug approved for conditions prevalent in patients 65 and older. This summary should: (1) list the five most common drug interactions in plain language, (2) flag whether the medication appears on the Beers Criteria list for older adults, and (3) include a large-print or QR-code-accessible version [1].
    *   The agency should also consider requiring pharmacies to provide this summary at the point of dispensing as part of their documentation compliance obligations. This would impose modest operational costs on manufacturers and pharmacies but would generate substantial downstream savings by reducing preventable hospitalizations caused by polypharmacy errors [2], [3].
    *   Furthermore, the "establishment of specific implementation timelines" should require a formal operational impact assessment specifically measuring the effect of new documentation requirements on direct patient contact time in primary care settings serving Medicare populations [4], [5].
    *   The agency should also require that any entity transitioning to new standards under the implementation timeline maintain uninterrupted access to medication interaction screening for existing patients throughout the transition period. Specifically, the timeline should include a 'continuity of care' protection clause prohibiting formulary changes that remove fall-risk-relevant medications without a mandatory pharmacist review using a validated tool such as STOPPFall or STEADI [2], [3].
    *   Finally, the agency should revise its cost analysis to include patient-side costs – including fall-related hospitalizations, emergency department visits, and long-term care placements – as benefits of timely implementation, not merely as background context.

[CONCLUSION]
In conclusion, I strongly support the agency's efforts to modernize and standardize oversight. However, for these new requirements to truly improve patient safety and well-being, they must explicitly address the critical needs of elderly Medicare patients like me. Clear, accessible drug labeling, timely information about formulary changes impacting generic drug availability, and simplified medication guides are not merely preferences; they are essential tools for managing complex medication regimens safely and affordably. I urge the agency to adopt these recommendations to ensure the proposed rule achieves its full potential in protecting our most vulnerable populations.

Thank you for considering my perspective.

### References
[1] https://storage.imrpress.com/IMR/hmed/application/10.12968/hmed.2024.0388.pdf
[2] https://worldfallsguidelines.com/implementation
[3] https://www.gov.scot/publications/polypharmacy-guidance-appropriate-prescribing-making-medicines-safe-effective-sustainable-2026-2029/pages/26/
[4] https://tandemhealth.ai/resources/knowledge/the-hidden-cost-of-documentation-in-healthcare
[5] https://www.acponline.org/advocacy/where-we-stand/2025-acp-priorities/reducing-administrative-burden-in-medicine
[6] https://www.cms.gov/files/document/cy2024-part-d-technical-specifications-02222024.pdf
[7] https://www.cms.gov/files/document/cy2025-part-d-technical-specifications-12032024.pdf

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The Biotechnology Innovation Organization (BIO) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on FDA-2024-XXXX, the agency's proposed suite of new compliance requirements. BIO is the world's largest trade association representing biotechnology companies, academic institutions, state biotechnology centers, and related organizations across the United States and in more than 30 other nations. Our members are engaged in the research and development of innovative healthcare, agricultural, industrial, and environmental biotechnology products. BIO's membership spans the full spectrum of the biopharmaceutical ecosystem, from early-stage startups and small biotechs—many of whom are developing groundbreaking cell and gene therapies (CGTs)—to multinational enterprises.

BIO is deeply concerned that the proposed revisions to reporting obligations, establishment of uniform implementation timelines, and introduction of more rigorous documentation standards, as currently drafted, will impose disproportionate operational burdens and compliance costs on our members, particularly small and emerging biotechs and those developing complex CGTs. Rather than enhancing safety and compliance, these changes risk diverting critical resources from research and development, delaying the advancement of novel therapies for patients, and stifling the innovation pipeline the agency aims to protect.

In this comment, BIO urges the agency to adopt a more nuanced, proportional approach that supports expedited regulatory pathways for breakthrough therapies, establishes clear and appropriate gene therapy durability evidence standards, and implements proportional manufacturing requirements tailored to the unique realities of small biotechs and the rapidly evolving CGT landscape.

[BACKGROUND]
The proposed rule introduces core regulatory changes focusing on three primary areas:
*   **Revision of existing reporting obligations:** This includes changes to the frequency, format, or content of data submissions required from regulated entities.
*   **Establishment of specific implementation timelines:** These timelines dictate the schedule by which entities must transition to the new standards.
*   **Introduction of more rigorous documentation standards:** These standards govern how records are created, maintained, and presented for regulatory review.

BIO's comments primarily address the operational and financial impacts of these provisions on our member companies, particularly those engaged in biopharmaceutical regulatory pathway engagement, clinical trial patient recruitment challenges, and cell and gene therapy manufacturing standards development.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]

**Impact on Cell and Gene Therapy Manufacturing and Development**
BIO is concerned that the proposed revised reporting obligations—particularly changes to submission frequency and data format requirements—will impose disproportionate operational burdens on CGT manufacturers at precisely the moment when the field is scaling from clinical development to commercial manufacturing, potentially undermining the very innovation pipeline the agency seeks to protect. BIO's membership spans early-stage startups to multinational biotechs, many of whom are simultaneously navigating Investigational New Drug (IND) filings, Chemistry, Manufacturing, and Controls (CMC) development, and comparability studies for CGT products. With over 2,500 active INDs for CGTs and approximately 1,300 active INDs for gene therapies currently on file with FDA's Office of Therapeutic Products (OTP), the compliance infrastructure required to meet revised reporting frequencies and reformatted data submissions would fall unevenly across this membership. Smaller sponsors—who constitute a significant share of BIO's CGT members—lack the regulatory affairs bandwidth and IT infrastructure to absorb simultaneous changes to submission format, content, and cadence without diverting resources from manufacturing standards development and clinical trial execution. This is not a theoretical concern: FDA's own January 2026 announcement of flexible CMC requirements for CGTs acknowledged that rigid, one-size-fits-all standards can impede product development for this uniquely complex product class [7].

Furthermore, BIO is deeply concerned that revised reporting obligations (new documentation format and content for CGT manufacturing) will impose disproportionate compliance costs on smaller and emerging biotech companies within our membership, threatening to slow the development of life-saving therapies at a critical inflection point for the sector. The proposed revised reporting obligations—changing frequency, format, and content of data submissions—would require significant investment in new data management infrastructure, staff retraining, and potentially third-party regulatory consulting services. For small and emerging companies, which represent a substantial portion of BIO's membership and the CGT pipeline, these costs are not marginal. The U.S. cell and gene therapy manufacturing market is estimated at $5.29 billion in 2024 and is projected to grow to $44.99 billion by 2034 at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 26.82% [6]. Imposing uniform, high-burden documentation standards at this stage risks creating a compliance barrier that disproportionately disadvantages smaller innovators who lack the regulatory infrastructure of large multinationals, potentially consolidating the market and reducing therapeutic diversity for patients.

BIO is also concerned that changes to the content requirements of data submissions—particularly if they mandate new long-term follow-up (LTFU) reporting formats or safety data structures not aligned with existing FDA guidance—will create duplicative and conflicting obligations for CGT manufacturers, increasing compliance costs without commensurate safety benefit. FDA has long required LTFU studies for CGT products given their potential for prolonged biological activity. BIO members developing gene therapies are already designing and executing LTFU protocols under existing FDA guidance frameworks. If the proposed rule introduces new content requirements for safety data submissions that diverge from or layer on top of existing LTFU expectations—without harmonization—manufacturers will face the costly prospect of maintaining parallel reporting systems: one for existing LTFU commitments and one for the new regulatory mandate. This is particularly acute for BIO members in the CGT space who are simultaneously managing CMS CGT Access Model participation requirements, which impose their own data submission and negotiation timelines (with a pre-implementation period that began May 1, 2024) [1].

Finally, BIO is concerned that changes to submission frequency and format for CGT manufacturing records fail to account for the unique operational realities of CGT manufacturing—including small batch sizes, autologous product variability, and decentralized manufacturing models—and that applying conventional pharmaceutical reporting cadences to CGT will generate voluminous but low-utility data submissions that burden both industry and agency reviewers without meaningfully improving safety oversight. CGT manufacturing is fundamentally different from traditional pharmaceutical manufacturing. Products are often autologous (patient-specific), manufactured in small batches, and subject to inherent biological variability that makes standardized reporting formats poorly suited to capturing meaningful safety and quality signals. BIO's members engaged in CGT manufacturing standards development report that applying conventional reporting cadences—designed for large-batch, chemically synthesized drugs—to CGT products creates a mismatch between the data collected and the data that is actually informative for regulatory decision-making [7]. The CGT manufacturing services market, valued at $7.74 billion in 2024 and projected to reach $76.20 billion by 2035 (CAGR of 23.1%) [6], is increasingly moving toward decentralized and AI-assisted manufacturing models. Reporting obligations designed around centralized, high-volume manufacturing paradigms will be operationally incompatible with these emerging approaches, creating compliance gaps that are artifacts of regulatory design rather than genuine safety deficiencies.

**Impact on Small and Startup Biotech Companies**
BIO is deeply concerned that the proposed uniform implementation timelines fail to account for the fundamentally different operational realities of startup and small biotech companies, and urges the agency to adopt a tiered, size-differentiated compliance schedule rather than a one-size-fits-all transition deadline. BIO's membership includes a significant cohort of early-stage and small biotech companies that are simultaneously managing IND submissions, Phase I/II clinical trial execution, and limited regulatory affairs staffing [2]. Unlike large multinationals, these companies cannot absorb compliance transitions in parallel with active development programs. A rigid, uniform implementation timeline forces small biotechs to divert scarce regulatory personnel and capital away from critical development milestones—such as IND-enabling studies and clinical phase transitions—toward administrative compliance activities. This directly threatens the continuity of patient recruitment in ongoing trials and delays the advancement of novel therapies, including CGT candidates where manufacturing standards are still being actively developed [3].

BIO is also concerned that the proposed implementation timelines, as currently structured, will impose disproportionate and unquantified costs on startup and small biotech companies engaged in biopharmaceutical regulatory pathway engagement, and calls on the agency to conduct and publish a robust small entity cost analysis before finalizing any transition schedule. For BIO's small member companies, the costs of transitioning to new reporting, documentation, and compliance standards within a compressed timeline are not merely administrative—they include the opportunity cost of regulatory affairs staff time redirected from IND and Biologics License Application (BLA) submissions, potential costs of third-party consultants to fill expertise gaps, and the risk of clinical holds or submission delays if compliance activities crowd out core regulatory work. In a sector where 2024 was characterized as a year of 'cautious optimism' following macroeconomic instability [3], and where early-stage asset valuations and deal activity remain sensitive to development timelines, any regulatory-imposed delay carries compounding financial consequences for small biotechs dependent on milestone-driven financing [2].

Furthermore, BIO is concerned that the proposed increase in data submission frequency will impose disproportionate and unquantified compliance costs on our member companies—particularly early-stage startups and small biotechs—that lack the infrastructure to absorb accelerated reporting cycles without diverting resources from core R&D and clinical development activities. For smaller members, a shift to more frequent data submissions—without commensurate IT infrastructure, dedicated regulatory affairs staff, or compliance software—translates directly into opportunity costs measured in delayed IND filings, disrupted clinical trial timelines, and diverted capital. The global pharmaceutical compliance software market was valued at $3.2 billion in 2025 [8], reflecting the mission-critical and expensive nature of these tools. Startups operating on Series A or B funding cannot absorb sudden compliance software upgrades or additional FTEs to manage accelerated submission cadences. Moreover, the biopharmaceutical industry has been documented as 'relatively slow to leverage' structured data technologies due to challenges such as data fragmentation [9], meaning the baseline readiness of many BIO members to comply with increased frequency requirements is lower than the agency may have assumed.

**Impact on Clinical Trials and Patient Recruitment**
BIO is deeply concerned that the proposed documentation standards, as currently drafted, will impose disproportionate operational burdens on clinical trial sites that are already struggling with patient recruitment and retention challenges, thereby threatening the viability of trials—particularly those conducted by smaller biotech members—without commensurate safety or data quality benefits. BIO's membership spans early-stage startups to multinational biotechs, many of which rely on a limited number of clinical trial sites to recruit patients for novel CGTs and other advanced modalities [4]. These sites are already resource-constrained: industry reports identify protocol complexity and administrative burden as leading contributors to site strain and enrollment delays [5]. When new documentation mandates layer additional record-creation and record-presentation requirements onto site staff, those staff hours are diverted from patient-facing recruitment activities—screening calls, community outreach, and informed consent discussions—directly reducing the number of patients enrolled. For BIO's startup members operating on limited runways, a 3–6 month enrollment delay caused by documentation compliance overhead can be existential, potentially forcing trial discontinuation and denying patients access to investigational therapies.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
In light of the significant concerns outlined above, BIO respectfully offers the following recommendations to support expedited regulatory pathways for breakthrough therapies, establish clear gene therapy durability evidence standards, and implement proportional manufacturing requirements for small biotech:

1.  **For Revised Reporting Obligations (Frequency, Format, Content) for CGTs:**
    *   The agency should explicitly exempt or provide a tiered compliance schedule for CGT sponsors based on company size and development stage (e.g., pre-BLA vs. post-approval) [1, 6, 7].
    *   BIO recommends that the agency grandfather existing reporting formats for sponsors with active INDs for a minimum of 36 months post-rule finalization [7].
    *   The agency should convene a CGT-specific working group—inclusive of BIO member companies—to co-develop format standards that align with existing CMC readiness frameworks before mandating new submission templates [6, 7].
    *   The agency should publish a formal cost-benefit analysis disaggregated by company size and product type prior to finalizing any format or frequency changes [6].
    *   In response to the agency's request for comments on alternatives, BIO recommends conducting a formal harmonization review between the proposed new content requirements and existing LTFU guidance, comparability reporting expectations, and CMS CGT Access Model data obligations before finalizing any content changes [1].
    *   The agency should adopt a 'report once, use many' framework that allows a single submission to satisfy multiple regulatory content requirements across agency programs [1].
    *   The agency should issue a companion guidance document clarifying how new content requirements interact with existing CGT-specific safety reporting obligations, with a public comment period of no less than 90 days [1].
    *   The agency should convene a dedicated CGT manufacturing working group—including BIO member companies, contract development and manufacturing organizations (CDMOs), and patient advocacy representatives—to co-develop CGT-specific reporting templates and submission frequency schedules prior to finalizing the rule. These templates should: (1) distinguish between autologous and allogeneic products; (2) allow risk-based reporting frequency tied to manufacturing process maturity rather than fixed calendar intervals; (3) accommodate decentralized and point-of-care manufacturing models; and (4) align with existing FDA draft guidances on CGT CMC to avoid creating conflicting or duplicative obligations [6, 7].
    *   The agency should pilot any new format requirements with a voluntary cohort of sponsors before mandating industry-wide adoption [6, 7].

2.  **For Implementation Timelines (Supporting Proportional Manufacturing Requirements for Small Biotech):**
    *   The agency should establish a formally tiered implementation schedule that grants small businesses (as defined by SBA size standards applicable to the biopharmaceutical sector) and companies with fewer than 500 employees or less than $50 million in annual revenue an extended compliance window of no less than 24 additional months beyond the deadline applicable to large entities [2, 3].
    *   The agency should create a formal 'compliance readiness certification' pathway allowing small biotechs to demonstrate good-faith progress toward compliance in lieu of full implementation by the initial deadline, consistent with how FDA has historically applied enforcement discretion for emerging technology sectors [2].
    *   Prior to finalizing implementation timelines, the agency should publish a disaggregated regulatory impact analysis that separately estimates compliance costs for companies with fewer than 500 employees, including one-time transition costs, ongoing annual compliance costs, and opportunity costs associated with diverted regulatory personnel [2, 3].
    *   BIO recommends the agency convene a small business advisory panel—including BIO member companies at the startup and emerging growth stages—to validate cost estimates before the rule is finalized [2, 3].
    *   The agency should conduct and publish a formal Small Entity Impact Analysis disaggregated by company size and development stage before finalizing any change to submission frequency [8, 9].
    *   The agency should establish a tiered compliance schedule that grants companies with fewer than 500 employees or annual revenues below $50 million an extended phase-in period of no less than 36 months [8].
    *   The agency should provide a safe harbor for companies actively investing in structured data infrastructure, consistent with the digital transformation trajectory described by BioPhorum [8].
    *   The agency should also clarify whether existing FDA electronic submission gateways can absorb increased submission volume without creating processing backlogs that would harm all applicants [9].

3.  **For Documentation Standards (Supporting Expedited Regulatory Pathways and Proportional Requirements):**
    *   The agency should conduct a formal site-level burden analysis—disaggregated by trial phase, therapeutic area, and site size—before finalizing documentation standards, and publish that analysis for public comment [4, 5].
    *   The agency should exempt or provide a simplified compliance pathway for Phase I/II trials sponsored by companies with fewer than 500 employees or annual revenues below $50 million [4, 5].
    *   The agency should permit the use of existing electronic clinical trial management system (CTMS) outputs and electronic Common Technical Document (eCTD)-formatted records as presumptively compliant with the new standards, rather than requiring duplicative paper or reformatted submissions, consistent with the FDA's own 2024 guidance modernization trajectory [5].

[CONCLUSION]
BIO strongly supports the agency's goal of modernizing oversight and enhancing the quality of information it receives. However, the current proposal, if finalized without substantial revisions, risks creating significant and disproportionate burdens on the biotechnology industry, particularly for small and emerging companies and those developing complex CGTs. These burdens could impede the very innovation and patient access the agency seeks to protect.

BIO urges the agency to consider these comments and adopt a more flexible, risk-based approach that acknowledges the diverse operational realities of its regulated entities. By implementing tiered compliance schedules, conducting robust small entity impact analyses, harmonizing new requirements with existing guidances, and developing CGT-specific reporting frameworks, the agency can achieve its objectives without stifling the development of life-saving therapies. We look forward to continuing to work collaboratively with the agency to ensure that any new compliance requirements are both effective and proportionate.

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
AbbVie Inc. ("AbbVie") appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the proposed rule [Proposed Rule Text Title], which seeks to modernize and standardize the oversight of the regulated industry through revised reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards. As a global biopharmaceutical company and the reference product sponsor for Humira® (adalimumab), a critical biologic medicine, AbbVie is deeply concerned that the current proposal does not adequately account for the unique complexities of biologic products, particularly during periods of active biosimilar competition and global regulatory transition. AbbVie is committed to patient safety, compliance, and fostering a regulatory environment that supports innovation and patient access to life-saving medicines. We urge the agency to consider the unique needs of biologics, specifically requesting clear biosimilar naming and labeling guidance, robust post-market safety monitoring standards, and manufacturing change management flexibility, to ensure the final rule is practical, harmonized, and supports public health.

[BACKGROUND]
The agency's proposal outlines core regulatory changes focused on three primary areas: the revision of existing reporting obligations, the establishment of specific implementation timelines for new mandates, and the introduction of more rigorous documentation standards. While we recognize the agency's goal to address perceived gaps in current data collection and record-keeping, these changes, as currently drafted, impose significant and disproportionate compliance burdens on originator biologic manufacturers like AbbVie.

AbbVie is currently navigating the most complex biosimilar market transition in pharmaceutical history, managing the reference product position of Humira against at least 10 FDA-approved adalimumab biosimilars that entered the market beginning July 1, 2023 [7]. This transition requires simultaneous coordination of pharmacovigilance reporting, post-market safety surveillance, manufacturing consistency requirements, and global regulatory submissions across dozens of jurisdictions. The proposed revisions to reporting obligations, documentation standards, and their associated implementation timelines, without modification, will create irreconcilable conflicts with global regulatory requirements, impose unworkable operational burdens, and potentially compromise patient safety and medicine supply stability during this critical period.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]

**1. Lack of Clear Biosimilar Naming and Labeling Guidance Imposed by Revised Data Submission Content Requirements**
AbbVie is concerned that the revised data submission content requirements, as proposed, impose disproportionately burdensome and duplicative reporting obligations on reference product sponsors managing active biosimilar competition, without commensurate benefit to agency oversight — and urges the agency to calibrate these requirements to reflect the distinct regulatory posture of post-patent-expiry biologics.

AbbVie is currently managing one of the most complex biosimilar transition environments in pharmaceutical history, with over 10 FDA-approved adalimumab (Humira) biosimilars now on the U.S. market following patent expiry [7]. The revised content requirements for data submissions would require AbbVie to generate, format, and submit substantially expanded datasets across multiple concurrent regulatory dossiers — including post-market safety reports, manufacturing comparability packages, and labeling updates — at precisely the moment when internal resources are already strained by biosimilar market entry management. The U.S. biosimilars market grew at a CAGR of 21% from 2019 to 2023, reaching $9.3 billion, meaning the volume of parallel regulatory activity surrounding reference products like Humira is unprecedented [6]. Requiring enhanced content submissions across all of these simultaneous filings creates a compounding administrative burden that is not proportionate to the marginal safety or compliance benefit the agency would receive from a well-characterized, long-approved biologic. Moreover, without clear guidance on biosimilar naming and labeling, fragmented or inconsistent content submissions could lead to prescriber and patient confusion, potentially increasing medication errors and undermining confidence in the biosimilar pathway.

**2. Inadequate Post-Market Safety Monitoring Standards Imposed by Revised Reporting Obligations**
AbbVie is deeply concerned that the proposed revised reporting obligations—particularly changes to submission frequency and data format requirements—impose disproportionate compliance burdens during the most operationally complex phase of the Humira biosimilar transition, when AbbVie must simultaneously manage reference product reporting, interchangeability designations, and multi-biosimilar market dynamics across more than a dozen competing adalimumab products.

AbbVie is currently managing the reference product position of Humira against at least 10 FDA-approved adalimumab biosimilars that entered the market beginning July 1, 2023, including products from Sandoz, Celltrion, Viatris/Biocon, Pfizer, Boehringer Ingelheim, and Samsung Bioepis—each with distinct interchangeability designations, concentration formulations (50mg vs. 100mg), and formulary access strategies [7]. The proposed changes to reporting frequency and format would require AbbVie to simultaneously overhaul its pharmacovigilance data infrastructure, safety reporting pipelines, and global regulatory submission coordination systems at precisely the moment when internal resources are already strained by biosimilar transition management. New format mandates would require re-engineering data feeds that currently serve both FDA and international regulatory bodies, creating costly redundancies and risking submission errors during a period of heightened regulatory scrutiny. AbbVie estimates that format and frequency changes to existing reporting systems during an active biosimilar transition period could require six to eighteen months of IT system reconfiguration, legal review of revised submission templates, and cross-functional retraining, representing tens of millions of dollars in unbudgeted compliance expenditure. These changes could disrupt established safety monitoring systems, potentially delaying signal detection for a critical biologic with decades of post-market data [8].

**3. Lack of Manufacturing Change Management Flexibility Imposed by Documentation Standards and Implementation Timelines**
AbbVie is concerned that the proposed documentation standards, as currently drafted, fail to account for the unique complexity of biologic manufacturing consistency requirements and will impose disproportionate compliance costs on companies managing multi-product biologic portfolios—including Humira biosimilar transition operations—without commensurate safety or data quality benefits. Additionally, AbbVie is concerned that the proposed implementation timeline for the new documentation standards is operationally unworkable for companies with validated biologic manufacturing systems, and that forcing rapid system changes during the Humira biosimilar transition period creates unacceptable risks of manufacturing consistency failures and potential drug supply disruption—an unintended consequence the agency does not appear to have considered.

AbbVie's biologic manufacturing operations require continuous, real-time documentation of physicochemical, biophysical, and bioactive/functional potency characterization across multiple manufacturing sites and product lines simultaneously. As we manage the Humira biosimilar transition, we are simultaneously maintaining originator product consistency records, supporting biosimilar reference product comparability packages, and coordinating global regulatory submissions across 30+ jurisdictions. The proposed documentation standards appear to have been designed with small-molecule or single-product contexts in mind. Applying uniform documentation formatting and record-presentation requirements to our biologic manufacturing consistency data—which includes complex analytical characterization datasets, process validation records, and comparability study outputs—would require us to reformat and re-architect existing validated electronic systems. FDA's own ICH Q13 guidance framework, implemented in 2023 for continuous manufacturing of therapeutic proteins, already establishes a sophisticated, risk-based documentation paradigm for biologics [4]. The proposed rule's one-size-fits-all documentation standards risk creating direct conflicts with these existing, internationally harmonized frameworks, forcing duplicative or contradictory record-keeping obligations.

Furthermore, biologic manufacturing consistency is not merely a paperwork exercise—it is a validated, integrated system where documentation is embedded within manufacturing execution systems (MES), laboratory information management systems (LIMS), and electronic batch records that have been validated under 21 CFR Part 11 and current GMP requirements [3]. Any change to how records are created, maintained, or presented in these systems constitutes a manufacturing change that itself requires documentation, validation, and potentially regulatory notification or prior approval. During AbbVie's active Humira biosimilar transition period, our manufacturing and quality systems are already under heightened change control scrutiny. Introducing a mandatory documentation system overhaul simultaneously with biosimilar market entry management creates compounding operational risk. The biological product manufacturing market is projected to grow at 16.8% CAGR through 2032, reflecting the sector's increasing complexity—yet the proposed timeline does not appear to account for the lead time required to validate system changes in this environment. Based on our experience with eCTD v4.0 readiness planning, transitioning a data-centric submission architecture for a portfolio of AbbVie's scale requires 18–24 months of preparation, system validation, and staff retraining—far exceeding what the proposed timeline appears to contemplate [1].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
AbbVie urges the agency to consider the following recommendations to address the unique complexities of biologics and biosimilar transition, ensuring the final rule is practical, harmonized, and supports patient safety and access:

**1. For Biosimilar Naming and Labeling Guidance (related to Revised Reporting Obligations - Content):**
*   Establish a tiered data submission content framework that distinguishes between (1) products in active clinical development or early post-approval phases, where enhanced content requirements are most justified, and (2) mature reference biologics with extensive post-market safety records and active biosimilar competition, where streamlined submission content — cross-referencing existing approved dossiers — should be permitted.
*   Develop specific naming and labeling guidance for biosimilars that emphasizes clear differentiation between the reference product and its biosimilars, aligning with international best practices and promoting patient and prescriber confidence.
*   Allow reference product sponsors to satisfy revised content requirements through structured cross-references to previously accepted submissions rather than requiring full re-documentation of well-established data.

**2. For Post-Market Safety Monitoring Standards (related to Revised Reporting Obligations - Frequency/Format):**
*   Grandfather existing reporting formats and frequencies for reference biological products that are actively managing biosimilar market transitions for a minimum of 36 months from the rule's effective date.
*   Establish a phased implementation schedule that staggers format changes from frequency changes, allowing systems upgrades to occur sequentially rather than simultaneously.
*   Explicitly address in the final rule how revised reporting obligations interact with existing BPCIA post-market commitments and interchangeability designation reporting requirements to avoid duplicative or conflicting submissions.
*   Convene a working group inclusive of originator manufacturers, biosimilar sponsors, and pharmacovigilance experts to co-develop harmonized, risk-based post-market safety monitoring templates before mandating new formats, ensuring that the revised obligations do not inadvertently create duplicative or conflicting submission requirements across FDA, FTC, and international regulatory bodies.

**3. For Manufacturing Change Management Flexibility (related to Documentation Standards and Implementation Timelines):**
*   Provide a minimum 36-month phased implementation timeline for biologic manufacturers, with an initial 12-month period dedicated to guidance clarification and system design, followed by a 24-month validated implementation window.
*   Explicitly exempt or create a separate compliance pathway for biologic manufacturing consistency documentation that is already governed by ICH Q8, Q9, Q10, Q11, and Q13 guidelines.
*   Conduct a formal harmonization analysis comparing the proposed documentation standards against WHO, ICH, EMA, and CTD requirements before finalizing any provisions applicable to biologics.
*   Establish a safe harbor provision protecting manufacturers from enforcement action during the transition period provided they are operating under an agency-approved implementation plan.
*   Explicitly clarify whether documentation system changes required to comply with this rule will themselves trigger manufacturing change reporting obligations under existing BLA/NDA post-approval change regulations, and if so, provide a streamlined notification pathway.
*   In response to the agency's specific request for alternatives, AbbVie recommends the agency consider a risk-tiered documentation standard that applies enhanced requirements only to novel biologics or manufacturing processes, while allowing established, validated biologic manufacturing systems to continue operating under existing ICH-harmonized documentation frameworks.
*   Publish a quantitative Regulatory Impact Analysis (RIA) for the biologic manufacturing sector, consistent with the agency's own RIA practices demonstrated in prior rulemakings, before finalizing these standards [3].

[CONCLUSION]
AbbVie appreciates the agency's efforts to modernize and standardize regulatory oversight. However, we are deeply concerned that the proposed rule, as currently drafted, imposes disproportionate and unworkable compliance burdens on originator biologic manufacturers, particularly during the complex and resource-intensive period of biosimilar transition. We urge the agency to consider the unique nature of biologics and biosimilar competition by providing clear biosimilar naming and labeling guidance, implementing robust and harmonized post-market safety monitoring standards, and offering greater flexibility for manufacturing change management. These adjustments are essential to ensure the final rule is practical, supports patient safety, maintains medicine supply stability, and avoids unintended consequences that could stifle innovation or impede patient access to critical biologic therapies.

### References

[3] https://www.fda.gov/economics-staff/regulatory-impact-analyses-ria
[4] https://www.wuxibiologics.com/h1-2024-regulatory-updates/
[6] https://alirahealth.com/education-hub/global-biosimilars-landscape-in-2024-trends-challenges-and-opportunities/
[7] https://biosimilarscouncil.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/04022024_IQVIA-Humira-Tracking-Executive-Summary.pdf
[8] https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12185648/
