{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
October 20, 2024
The Honorable [Administrator's Name/Secretary's Name]
[Agency Address]
[City, State, Zip Code]

**Re: Comment on FDA-2024-XXXX: [Proposed Rule Title]**

Dear Administrator [Name],

I am writing to express my profound support for the proposed suite of new compliance requirements, specifically the "revised reporting obligations" and "documentation standards," as outlined in FDA-2024-XXXX. My name is Karen Lopez, and I am the parent of two children with severe, life-threatening peanut and tree nut allergies. I also serve as a dedicated advocate for school food allergen policies and champion access to affordable epinephrine auto-injectors.

For families like mine, the accuracy and reliability of food allergen information are not abstract compliance issues; they are daily life-and-death necessities. The current patchwork of inconsistent documentation and reporting practices directly endangers my children every single day, whether they are at school, eating a packaged snack, or simply relying on a food label.

[BACKGROUND]
The agency's proposal to modernize and standardize oversight through "revised reporting obligations," "implementation timelines," and "documentation standards" is a critical step towards closing dangerous information gaps. These core regulatory changes are not merely administrative updates; they are fundamental to ensuring that allergen labeling claims are trustworthy, that schools have the data needed to protect students, and that life-saving medications are both affordable and well-regulated. I will detail my perspective on how each of these provisions impacts the food allergy community and offer specific recommendations to strengthen their effectiveness.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**1. Impact of "Documentation Standards" on Precautionary Allergen Labeling (PAL) and Label Accuracy:**
My daily allergen avoidance vigilance depends entirely on the accuracy of what is printed on food labels. When I read "no peanuts" or "made in a peanut-free facility," I am making a life-or-death decision based on that claim. Right now, there is no uniform standard governing how manufacturers document their allergen controls, cross-contact prevention protocols, or ingredient sourcing records. This means two products with identical label claims may be backed by vastly different levels of rigor. FSIS's August 2024 allergen verification sampling program — which found that establishments producing RTE products with allergen-absence claims needed targeted verification — confirms that the gap between what labels say and what manufacturers can actually document is real and consequential [1]. For my family, that gap is not an abstract compliance problem; it is the difference between a safe school lunch and an anaphylactic emergency.

The current system also lacks standardized documentation for precautionary allergen labeling (PAL), such as "may contain peanuts." These statements are often used inconsistently, with no standardized evidentiary threshold, causing immense confusion and contributing to the estimated 1.8–2% of the U.S. population with peanut allergy alone [6]. As a parent, I spend hours each week reading labels, cross-referencing manufacturer websites, calling company hotlines, and navigating this chaos. The operational burden on allergen-avoidant families due to poor-quality submissions and inconsistent labeling is measurable in emergency room visits and anaphylaxis events. The cost of a single epinephrine auto-injector exceeds $300 without insurance assistance, and the cost of an emergency room visit following an anaphylactic reaction can reach tens of thousands of dollars [6]. These are real economic costs borne by allergic families that are rarely counted in industry cost-benefit analyses. Standardized documentation that improves label accuracy would reduce these downstream costs. The World Bank's research on food safety management systems (FSMS) demonstrates that standardized protocols and critical control point monitoring reduce operational variability and improve product consistency — meaning documentation standards can actually reduce manufacturers' long-run costs by preventing recalls and liability.

**2. Impact of "Revised Reporting Obligations" on School Allergen Management and Epinephrine Access Laws:**
As a parent of two children with severe food allergies and a school food allergen policy advocate, I strongly support revised reporting obligations that standardize allergen data submissions from school food service programs. Currently, I must personally review breakfast, lunch, and snack menus daily—a burden confirmed by existing school allergy policies that place the entire responsibility on parents to identify allergen risks and arrange alternative meals when schools cannot accommodate their child's needs [3]. This is not a minor inconvenience; it is a daily, high-stakes exercise that consumes significant time and creates anxiety because the underlying data I rely on—ingredient disclosures, supplier substitution notices, precautionary labeling—is inconsistently formatted and reported across different school districts and food vendors. Standardized, more frequent reporting obligations would give me and school administrators access to reliable, up-to-date allergen information, reducing the risk of a life-threatening anaphylactic reaction.

The existing annual reporting cycle — where USDA's Food and Nutrition Service receives inspection data only once per year by November 15 — means that a school with repeated allergen-handling failures in January may not surface in any accessible report until nearly a year later [7]. As a parent managing daily allergen avoidance vigilance for two children, this lag leaves me unable to identify systemic problems at my children's school in time to intervene. More frequent, standardized data submissions would give me and other parents actionable, timely information rather than retrospective snapshots. I have seen firsthand that school nutrition directors and staff are often under-resourced and undertrained on food allergy protocols. When new documentation requirements are introduced without accompanying guidance, the result is inconsistent self-reported data — a problem explicitly acknowledged in NYC's 2024 Food Standards Compliance report, which noted that compliance data "is not verified" and that agencies "continue to work to enhance processes to assess accuracy." If the revised reporting obligations produce similarly unverified, inconsistently formatted data, the agency will have increased the administrative burden on schools without improving the quality of information that parents and advocates rely upon.

**3. Impact of "Implementation Timelines" on Generic Epinephrine Auto-injector Approval and Accessibility:**
As an active epinephrine auto-injector access campaigner, I strongly support the proposed implementation timelines for transitioning to new reporting and documentation standards, provided the agency establishes sufficiently short, enforceable deadlines that compel manufacturers to make affordability and access data publicly available without delay. Vague or extended transition windows — as seen in the EU MDR experience, where legacy device manufacturers were granted repeated deadline extensions stretching years beyond original targets — would directly harm families like mine who depend on timely, accurate information to make life-or-death purchasing and coverage decisions [8].

My advocacy work requires me to track which epinephrine auto-injector manufacturers are complying with affordability program requirements, such as those established under Colorado's HB23-1002, which mandated manufacturer procedures by January 1, 2024. When documentation standards are inconsistent or transition timelines are loosely enforced, I cannot reliably verify whether manufacturers are meeting their obligations. This creates dangerous information gaps: I may not know whether a lower-cost generic auto-injector meets the same safety documentation standards as a brand-name device, or whether a manufacturer's affordability program is genuinely accessible. For a family managing daily allergen vigilance and the high cost of maintaining multiple epinephrine auto-injectors, these gaps are not abstract — they translate into delayed or foregone prescriptions.

**4. Operational Burden and Compliance Costs:**
The agency has specifically requested comments on expected costs and operational impacts. While I understand that industry stakeholders will argue that new documentation and reporting requirements impose significant operational burdens, I urge the agency to consider the substantial costs already borne by allergic consumers. The high cost of EpiPens already strains family and school budgets. If the agency's new requirements impose significant compliance costs on school districts, those districts may respond by cutting corners on allergen management programs, reducing training, or limiting the variety of allergen-safe meal options—outcomes that would directly harm my children and the thousands of students like them. This cost-shifting to schools creates a dangerous unintended consequence.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
In response to the agency's specific requests for comments on expected costs, operational impacts, and alternatives, I offer the following recommendations:

**Regarding "Documentation Standards" and Precautionary Allergen Labeling:**
*   Finalize and strengthen the proposed documentation standards with specific requirements that:
    *   Mandate allergen control records be maintained in a format that is auditable and traceable to specific production lots, so that in the event of a recall or adverse event, the documentation chain is unambiguous.
    *   Require that allergen-absence label claims be supported by documented verification records — not merely self-attestation — consistent with the FSIS allergen verification sampling model launched in August 2024 [1].
    *   Establish a minimum retention period for allergen-related records that aligns with the shelf life of the products in question, ensuring records are available if a delayed adverse reaction is investigated.
    *   Explicitly require that allergen content data — including cross-contact risk, shared production line disclosures, and precautionary allergen labeling (PAL) justifications — be included as mandatory fields within the new documentation standards. Require manufacturers to maintain and submit records substantiating any PAL claim, so that the agency can audit whether such warnings are evidence-based or merely liability-driven.
*   Publish a plain-language summary of what documentation standards mean for consumer label reliability, so that families managing food allergies understand what protections are now in place.

**Regarding "Revised Reporting Obligations" for Schools and Allergen Management:**
*   Require that revised reporting obligations include mandatory, standardized allergen disclosure fields in all school meal data submissions, updated in real time when ingredient sourcing changes. Adopt a uniform digital format — compatible with school nutrition management systems — that flags the top nine allergens (including peanuts and tree nuts) at the ingredient level, not just the menu-item level.
*   Require that any mid-cycle supplier substitution triggering an allergen change be reported within 24 hours, not at the next scheduled reporting interval.
*   Require that school food safety inspection data — including allergen-specific incidents, labeling complaints, and epinephrine administration events — be submitted at minimum twice per school year (aligned with the two-inspection-per-year standard already referenced in USDA Food and Nutrition Service guidance) and made publicly accessible in a searchable, standardized format within 30 days of submission [7]. Explicitly include allergen-related findings as a distinct data category.
*   Develop standardized reporting templates with explicit allergen-incident fields that reduce ambiguity for school nutrition staff completing submissions.
*   Partner with the Institute of Child Nutrition — which already produces school food allergy fact sheets and resources for nutrition directors — to develop training materials that accompany the new documentation standards [4].
*   Phase in the most complex new documentation requirements over two school years to allow schools to build capacity without sacrificing data quality in the transition period.

**Regarding "Implementation Timelines" for Generic Epinephrine Auto-injectors:**
*   Establish firm, non-extendable implementation deadlines of no more than 12 months for manufacturers of epinephrine auto-injectors to comply with the new reporting and documentation standards. Explicitly reject the model of rolling deadline extensions used in the EU MDR transition, which allowed legacy device manufacturers to remain on the market under outdated documentation regimes for years [8].
*   Require that compliance status under the new standards be posted in a publicly accessible, plain-language database so that patient advocates and caregivers can independently verify manufacturer compliance without filing formal information requests [9].

**Regarding Operational Burden and Compliance Costs:**
*   Conduct a tiered cost analysis that distinguishes between costs borne by large food manufacturers versus small school districts.
*   Require that food manufacturers and distributors supply pre-formatted, machine-readable allergen data as part of their school meal contracts, so that schools are not absorbing the data-collection burden.
*   Explore whether USDA State Administrative Expense funds — which GAO has noted are used to support child nutrition program oversight — could be directed toward helping districts implement new reporting systems without cutting allergen safety programs [7].
*   Conduct a full social cost accounting that includes costs borne by allergic consumers — including medical costs, epinephrine auto-injector costs, and the economic value of time spent on allergen avoidance vigilance — not only costs borne by regulated industry.
*   Consider a tiered compliance timeline that gives smaller manufacturers additional time to implement documentation systems, while holding large manufacturers with existing food safety management infrastructure to the full standard immediately.

[CONCLUSION]
In conclusion, the proposed revisions to reporting obligations, documentation standards, and implementation timelines are vital for protecting the health and safety of children with severe food allergies. By supporting standardized precautionary allergen labeling, strengthening school epinephrine access laws through robust data, and ensuring transparent oversight of generic epinephrine auto-injector approval, this rule has the potential to significantly reduce the risks and burdens currently faced by millions of families. I urge the agency to adopt these recommendations and finalize a rule that prioritizes public health and safety above all else.

Thank you for your time and consideration of these critical issues.

Sincerely,

Karen Lopez
Parent of Child with Severe Food Allergies
[Karen Lopez's Contact Information - e.g., Email, Phone]

### References
[1] http://www.fsis.usda.gov/news-events/news-press-releases/constituent-update-july-19-2024
[3] https://www.uccacostams.org/media/psjjx0qi/23-24-food-safety-allergy-policy.pdf
[4] https://www.foodallergy.org/resources/fams-expert-recommendations-pdf
[6] https://www.congress.gov/bill/118th-congress/house-bill/8385/text
[7] https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/MTOPI/bulletins/3bcff32
[8] https://www.freyrsolutions.com/blog/revisiting-the-eu-mdr-timeline-extension
[9] https://www.rimsys.io/blog/quick-reference-guide-global-udi-requirements-and-timelines

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
[Date]
[Agency Contact Name/Address, if available, otherwise generic]

Re: [Docket Number - FDA-2024-XXXX] Comments on Proposed Rule: Modernization of Data Collection and Record-Keeping Requirements

Dear [Agency Contact Name/Designation, e.g., Commissioner],

On behalf of the Personal Care Products Council (PCPC), we appreciate the opportunity to submit comments in response to the proposed rule regarding modernizing data collection and record-keeping requirements under Docket No. FDA-2024-XXXX. PCPC is the leading national trade association representing the global cosmetics and personal care products industry, with a membership that spans thousands of companies, including a significant cohort of small and mid-sized manufacturers. Our mission is to promote safety, innovation, and trust in the industry.

PCPC is deeply concerned that the proposed suite of new compliance requirements, particularly the revision of existing reporting obligations, the establishment of specific implementation timelines, and the introduction of more rigorous documentation standards, fails to adequately account for the ongoing, complex implementation of the Modernization of Cosmetics Regulation Act of 2022 (MoCRA). Our members are currently in the active buildout phase of MoCRA-mandated systems—including facility registration, product listing, and adverse event reporting infrastructure—and layering additional, non-harmonized obligations on top of an already compressed compliance calendar creates an untenable administrative and financial burden, particularly for small businesses.

[BACKGROUND]
The proposed rule seeks to modernize and standardize oversight by revising reporting obligations, establishing specific implementation timelines, and introducing documentation standards. As stated in the Executive Summary, the proposal aims to "address perceived gaps in current data collection and record-keeping practices" and "improve the quality of information it receives, thereby enhancing its ability to monitor safety and compliance."

PCPC's comments focus on the intersections between these proposed changes and the critical ongoing compliance activities required under MoCRA, specifically:
*   The revision of existing reporting obligations, which will change the frequency, format, or content of data submissions, affecting adverse event reporting system buildout and small manufacturer compliance capacity.
*   The establishment of specific implementation timelines that dictate the schedule by which entities must transition to the new standards, affecting MoCRA compliance timelines and facility registration.
*   The introduction of new documentation standards to govern how records are created, maintained, and presented for regulatory review, affecting the Cosmetic Ingredient Review (CIR) program and overall MoCRA implementation coordination.

We urge the agency to adopt a phased, tiered approach that aligns any new requirements with MoCRA's implementation schedule, provides clear facility registration guidance, and establishes proportional requirements for small cosmetics manufacturers.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**1. Cumulative Operational Burden and Conflicting Timelines with MoCRA Implementation**
PCPC member companies, many of which are small and mid-sized manufacturers, are currently in the active buildout phase of adverse event reporting systems required for the first time under MoCRA. These companies are investing significant capital and staff resources to design, test, and validate data collection pipelines, train personnel, and integrate with FDA's reporting portals. The proposed revised reporting obligations—changing submission frequency, format specifications, or required data fields—would force companies to redesign systems that are not yet fully operational, effectively requiring two rounds of costly system development within a compressed window [1].

FDA itself acknowledged the operational complexity of simultaneous multi-requirement compliance when it exercised enforcement discretion on MoCRA's facility registration and product listing compliance deadline, extending it by six months from December 29, 2023, to July 1, 2024 [2, 3]. This decision is direct agency acknowledgment that the regulated industry, particularly smaller entities, lacks the operational bandwidth to absorb multiple simultaneous compliance demands. Layering new implementation timelines and documentation standards on top of an already compressed MoCRA compliance calendar creates compounding operational burdens that our smaller members, who lack dedicated regulatory affairs staff, are structurally unable to absorb simultaneously. Mid-sized PCPC members estimate that a single adverse event reporting system buildout costs between $150,000 and $500,000 in technology, legal, and personnel resources; a mid-stream format or frequency change could add 30–60% to those costs.

The proposed documentation standards introduce a third, parallel recordkeeping architecture that does not align with the record formats and retention schedules our members have already invested in developing for MoCRA compliance [8, 9]. For small manufacturers in particular—who lack dedicated compliance staff—maintaining two or more non-harmonized documentation systems simultaneously is not operationally feasible.

**2. Disproportionate Impact on Small Cosmetics Manufacturers**
PCPC's membership includes a significant cohort of small and emerging cosmetics manufacturers who are already stretching limited compliance staff to meet MoCRA's core implementation deadlines. Our coordination role in MoCRA implementation has made clear that many small members lack dedicated regulatory affairs personnel; a single compliance officer may be responsible for facility registration, adverse event reporting system buildout, and ingredient safety documentation simultaneously. Adding revised reporting obligations with new frequency, format, or content requirements on top of this stack—without size-based accommodation—risks overwhelming these members' compliance capacity entirely. The U.S. Chamber of Commerce Small Business Index (Q4 2024) found that 39% of small businesses reported an increase in time or resources spent on regulatory compliance in the past six months, up from 33% the prior quarter—a trend that is accelerating precisely as MoCRA compliance demands peak [6]. For small cosmetics manufacturers, this is not an abstract statistic; it translates directly into delayed product launches, diverted R&D resources, and potential market exit.

The proposed increase in reporting frequency—moving toward more frequent or near-real-time adverse event submissions—will impose disproportionate operational costs on small cosmetics manufacturers without producing commensurate safety benefits at this stage of MoCRA implementation. Increased submission frequency requires automated data pipelines, dedicated compliance personnel for ongoing data review and submission, and IT systems capable of real-time data aggregation from retail, e-commerce, and direct consumer channels. These are not capabilities that small manufacturers can build quickly or cheaply [1].

**3. Duplication and Conflict with CIR Documentation**
PCPC directly funds and coordinates with the CIR Expert Panel for Cosmetic Ingredient Safety, which conducts open, publicly accessible safety assessments of cosmetic ingredients. The CIR program already produces detailed administrative memoranda, draft safety reports, resource documents (e.g., the December 2024 Margin of Exposure Resource Document), post-meeting announcements, and panel minutes—all of which are publicly available and subject to open comment periods [4, 5]. Requiring CIR to reformat, re-document, or re-present this existing body of work to meet new agency documentation standards would divert CIR staff and scientific resources away from active ingredient reviews, slowing the pipeline of safety assessments at precisely the moment when MoCRA implementation is demanding accelerated regulatory engagement. The CIR program is currently modernizing its own administrative infrastructure, as reflected in the September 2023 Post Meeting Announcement noting "a collective eye to modernizing CIR," and imposing external documentation mandates mid-modernization risks disrupting that process [4].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
In direct response to the agency's request for comments on expected costs, operational impacts, and alternatives, PCPC offers the following specific recommendations:

**1. Align Reporting Obligations with FDA AEMS Consolidation and MoCRA Timelines**
*   **Recommendation:** The agency should explicitly synchronize the effective date of any revised reporting obligations (frequency, format, content) with the FDA's AEMS consolidation rollout timeline for cosmetics.
*   **Justification:** This aligns new requirements with the FDA's own data modernization strategy and avoids forcing manufacturers to design, redesign, and integrate reporting systems multiple times within a compressed period. It directly addresses the operational burden of duplicative system buildouts.
*   **Actionable Steps:**
    *   Delay finalization of revised format and frequency requirements until AEMS technical specifications for cosmetics are published and stable.
    *   Issue a safe harbor period of no less than 24 months from AEMS cosmetics module launch before enforcing revised submission standards.
    *   Convene a technical working group including PCPC and small manufacturer representatives to co-develop submission templates that satisfy both MoCRA requirements and the new reporting obligations simultaneously.

**2. Adopt Tiered and Phased Implementation Timelines**
*   **Recommendation:** The agency should structure implementation timelines using a tiered compliance schedule that sequences new requirements in non-overlapping windows relative to active MoCRA milestones.
*   **Justification:** This addresses the demonstrated operational complexity of simultaneous multi-requirement compliance, particularly for small and mid-size manufacturers who are still building out MoCRA-mandated systems. It provides the necessary lead time for industry to adapt and reduces the risk of widespread inadvertent non-compliance.
*   **Actionable Steps:**
    *   Publish a consolidated compliance calendar that maps all pending MoCRA obligations alongside any new requirements so regulated entities can plan holistically.
    *   Provide a minimum 18-month runway from final rule publication before any new documentation or reporting standards take effect.
    *   Establish a small business accommodation track—consistent with the Small Business Regulatory Enforcement Fairness Act—that extends deadlines by at least 12 additional months for manufacturers with fewer than 500 employees or under $10 million in annual cosmetic sales.

**3. Implement Proportional Reporting Requirements for Small Manufacturers**
*   **Recommendation:** Establish a formally tiered reporting structure that scales revised reporting obligations by manufacturer size, reducing frequency and simplifying format requirements for small cosmetics manufacturers.
*   **Justification:** This addresses the disproportionate compliance burdens on small manufacturers who are simultaneously navigating MoCRA's foundational requirements (facility registration, product listing, adverse event reporting) with limited resources. It ensures that reporting requirements are proportional to the size and capacity of the regulated entity.
*   **Actionable Steps:**
    *   Establish a tiered reporting frequency structure: large manufacturers (annual gross sales exceeding $10M) submit quarterly; small manufacturers submit annually, with all manufacturers required to submit within 15 business days for any serious adverse event regardless of tier.
    *   Phase in any new content requirements for small manufacturers at least 18–24 months after the effective date for large manufacturers.
    *   Publish a dedicated Small Entity Compliance Guide—analogous to FinCEN's BOI Small Entity Compliance Guide model [7]—that translates revised obligations into plain-language, step-by-step instructions.
    *   Provide a small business exemption or extended phase-in of no less than 36 months for any frequency increase beyond annual reporting for small manufacturers.

**4. Establish a Compliance Readiness Certification Alternative to Hard Deadlines**
*   **Recommendation:** Adopt a 'compliance readiness certification' alternative to hard deadline enforcement, modeled on FDA's own enforcement discretion approach during MoCRA rollout.
*   **Justification:** This allows entities demonstrating documented good-faith compliance progress to avoid penalties during a defined transition window, distinguishing between entities making genuine investments and those engaged in willful non-compliance. It reduces unintended market disruption and protects small manufacturers [2, 3].
*   **Actionable Steps:**
    *   Establish a 'Compliance Readiness Certification' pathway under which regulated entities may submit a documented compliance implementation plan—including milestones, resource commitments, and projected completion dates—within 90 days of final rule publication.
    *   Entities with accepted plans would receive a defined enforcement discretion period (recommended: 12–24 months post-deadline) during which they are shielded from penalty provided they meet documented milestones.
    *   PCPC offers to work with the agency to develop standardized plan templates that reduce administrative burden while providing the agency with meaningful compliance trajectory data.

**5. Harmonize Documentation Standards with MoCRA and Recognize CIR Equivalency**
*   **Recommendation:** Conduct an explicit harmonization review to ensure the proposed documentation standards are fully compatible with MoCRA's existing recordkeeping requirements before finalizing any new mandates, and explicitly recognize CIR's existing documentation framework as satisfying the new standards for ingredient safety data.
*   **Justification:** This addresses the concern that the proposed standards impose duplicative and inconsistent recordkeeping requirements that directly conflict with MoCRA obligations, diverting resources from substantive safety activities. It also avoids requiring unnecessary reformatting or duplication of CIR's already-public, scientifically rigorous documentation [4, 5, 8, 9].
*   **Actionable Steps:**
    *   Adopt a single, unified record format that satisfies both the proposed rule and MoCRA obligations, reducing duplicative compliance infrastructure.
    *   Sequence the effective date of any new documentation standards to follow—not precede or overlap with—the completion of MoCRA's core implementation milestones, including the final GMP rule.
    *   Issue a safe harbor provision or equivalency determination for CIR-generated records, including its open meeting records, published safety assessments, and publicly available administrative memoranda.
    *   Conduct a formal Small Business Regulatory Enforcement Fairness Act (SBREFA) analysis before finalizing documentation standards.

[CONCLUSION]
The Personal Care Products Council supports the agency's goal of modernizing data collection and record-keeping to enhance safety and compliance. However, the current proposal, as drafted, risks creating significant, unintended consequences for the cosmetics industry—particularly for small and mid-sized manufacturers—by layering new, non-harmonized requirements on top of the already complex, ongoing implementation of MoCRA.

We urge the agency to adopt a more strategic, phased, and tiered approach that prioritizes the successful implementation of MoCRA, provides clear guidance, and establishes proportional requirements for small businesses. By doing so, the agency can achieve its modernization objectives without imposing undue burdens that could stifle innovation, reduce consumer choice, or force small manufacturers out of the market. We remain committed to working collaboratively with the agency to develop practical, effective solutions that advance public health and support a thriving cosmetics industry.

Sincerely,

[PCPC Representative Name and Title]
Personal Care Products Council

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
As a health policy and regulation expert, I appreciate the opportunity to submit comments on the proposed rule FDA-2024-XXXX, which outlines new compliance requirements designed to modernize and standardize the oversight of the regulated industry. My research and experience at the academic-government interface, particularly concerning FDA institutional design, public health law, and evidence-based approaches to critical public health challenges such as opioid prescribing and tobacco regulation, lead me to strongly support this proposal.

[BACKGROUND]
The proposed rule focuses on three primary areas: the revision of existing reporting obligations, the establishment of specific implementation timelines for new mandates, and the introduction of more rigorous documentation standards. The agency's stated goal is to address perceived gaps in current data collection and record-keeping practices, thereby improving the quality of information it receives and enhancing its ability to monitor safety and compliance. I view these provisions as foundational infrastructure improvements that will significantly strengthen FDA's capacity to protect public health across its diverse regulatory portfolio.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
My analysis of the proposed rule's provisions reveals several critical impacts relevant to regulatory modernization, enhanced post-market safety, and evidence-based policy:

*   **Revised Reporting Obligations:** As a health policy and regulation expert who studies FDA institutional design, I strongly support the revised reporting obligations, particularly the agency's move toward standardized electronic submission formats such as the E2B(R3) standard adopted under AEMS, because these reforms represent a structurally sound modernization of FDA's data infrastructure that will meaningfully improve the agency's capacity to detect safety signals and enforce compliance—goals that decades of public health law scholarship have identified as chronically underfunded and technically fragmented. My research on FDA regulatory policy depends critically on the quality, consistency, and accessibility of the data the agency collects and publishes. Under the prior fragmented reporting regime, my team routinely encountered incompatible data formats, inconsistent submission timelines, and gaps in adverse event coverage that made longitudinal analysis unreliable. The AEMS platform's consolidation of adverse event reports, consumer complaints, and whistleblower submissions into a single standardized system—as described in FDA's January 2024 electronic submission guidance—directly improves the evidentiary foundation upon which academic research, including my own, is built. Standardized reporting obligations are not merely an administrative convenience; they are a prerequisite for the kind of rigorous, reproducible policy analysis that informs Congressional oversight, judicial review, and future rulemaking. In my research on FDA's outbreak response capacity, I have documented how reporting lags between adverse event occurrence and agency awareness create windows during which preventable harm accumulates. The 2024 Diamond Shruumz incident, in which FDA and CDC responded to a series of illnesses linked to hemp-derived products sold through loosely regulated retail channels, illustrates precisely the kind of scenario where more frequent, standardized reporting from regulated entities could have accelerated detection [1]. As a researcher studying the academic-government interface, I am acutely aware that when agencies accept industry arguments about 'operational burden' without rigorous scrutiny, the result is systematically delayed safety action—a pattern well-documented in the public health law literature. The FDA's own FY2024 Report on Pharmaceutical Quality demonstrates that standardized reporting—such as the CARES Act amount reporting improvements for CY2023 and CY2024 following final guidance issuance—produces measurably better data quality and compliance rates, validating the core premise of this rulemaking [6].

*   **Implementation Timelines:** As a health policy and regulation expert who regularly bridges academic research and government regulatory practice, I strongly support the proposed implementation timelines as a necessary structural mechanism to drive accountability, but urge the agency to build in phased milestone checkpoints rather than a single compliance deadline, to ensure that the quality of transition—not merely its speed—is what gets measured. My research on FDA institutional reform and public health law consistently shows that regulatory transitions fail not because of bad intent but because of inadequate sequencing. When the agency sets a single hard deadline without intermediate benchmarks, regulated entities—and the academic-government partnerships that support them—are forced into last-minute, compressed compliance efforts that undermine data quality, the very outcome this rule seeks to improve. In my work advising on regulatory modernization, I have observed that agencies that front-load compliance pressure without staged milestones generate a surge of low-quality documentation submissions near the deadline, creating exactly the data integrity problems the rule is designed to solve. This phased approach is consistent with best practices documented in EHR implementation research, which found that 'timeline discrepancies and understaffing' are among the leading causes of failed regulatory transitions when all parties are not brought into structured partnership early [8, 9].

*   **Documentation Standards:** As a health policy and regulation expert whose research depends on analyzing how regulatory documentation requirements shape public health law complexity, I strongly support the agency's proposed documentation standards because standardized, high-quality records are foundational to the kind of legal epidemiological research that informs evidence-based regulatory reform. My research program at the intersection of FDA regulatory policy and public health law relies critically on the quality and consistency of records produced through regulatory review processes. When documentation standards are inconsistent or agency-specific, it creates a fragmented evidentiary landscape that makes comparative legal research across regulatory domains extraordinarily difficult. The proposed documentation standards would, if well-designed, generate a more uniform corpus of regulatory records that my research team and others could use to study how documentation practices correlate with compliance outcomes, safety signals, and enforcement patterns. Without standardization, I am currently forced to apply significant methodological workarounds to account for variation in how records are created and presented — a burden that reduces the rigor and generalizability of my findings [2, 3]. My research at the academic-government interface consistently reveals that FDA's oversight capacity is only as strong as the data infrastructure underlying it. When documentation standards are inconsistent or insufficiently rigorous, the agency cannot reliably detect emerging safety problems, evaluate real-world product performance, or build the institutional knowledge base necessary for durable regulatory reform. The FDA's own CBER-CDER Data Standards Program 2024 Annual Assessment acknowledges that the 'efficient and effective review of regulatory submissions' depends directly on the identification, development, testing, adoption, and maintenance of study data standards — a lifecycle that requires precisely the kind of rigorous documentation baseline this proposal would establish [4, 5].

*   **Operational Impacts and Costs:** Responding directly to the agency's request for comments on operational impacts: I support the proposed documentation standards and find that, from an academic research perspective, the operational burden of these standards is justified and likely underestimated in its positive externalities. However, I caution the agency that poorly scoped documentation requirements risk generating 'documentation burden' that degrades rather than improves data quality, a phenomenon well-documented in healthcare settings that the agency should proactively design against [2, 3]. My research on FDA institutional knowledge and regulatory reform has consistently shown that agencies underestimate how documentation requirements, when poorly designed, produce voluminous but low-utility records. The long-term institutional knowledge benefits of rigorous documentation standards substantially outweigh near-term compliance costs. From my perspective as a health policy researcher who studies FDA institutional reform, one of the most persistent vulnerabilities of regulatory modernization efforts is that they are adopted without a sufficiently robust public record of their expected benefits — making them susceptible to being characterized as purely cost-imposing mandates when political or industry pressure mounts. The proposed documentation standards, if properly justified, represent an investment in FDA's institutional knowledge infrastructure that compounds over time: better baseline data enables better safety signal detection, more reliable real-world evidence generation, and more defensible regulatory decisions. The PMC analysis of real-world evidence in FDA approvals for labeling expansion demonstrates that the quality of underlying data directly determines whether FDA can act on post-market evidence — a capability that is increasingly central to the agency's modern regulatory model [4, 5]. Without rigorous documentation standards, real-world evidence programs remain aspirational rather than operational. I also support the revised reporting obligations and urge the agency to resist pressure to weaken submission frequency requirements, because the public health costs of delayed or infrequent reporting—as demonstrated by the 2024 Diamond Shruumz outbreak response—substantially outweigh the operational burden concerns raised by regulated entities, and because the agency's own cost-benefit framing in this proceeding underweights the value of early signal detection [1].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
Based on my analysis, I offer the following specific recommendations:

*   **For Revised Reporting Obligations:**
    *   The agency should explicitly commit, within the final rule, to making the standardized data collected under the revised reporting obligations publicly accessible through a structured, machine-readable public dashboard—modeled on the AEMS Public Dashboard already in development—with clearly defined update frequencies and data dictionaries [1]. This would allow academic researchers, public health law centers, and independent watchdogs to serve as a force-multiplying layer of safety surveillance.
    *   The agency should also specify in the rule's preamble that data format standards will be harmonized across all FDA centers (CDER, CDRH, CFSAN, etc.), not siloed by product category, to enable cross-center trend analysis that the current system structurally prevents.
    *   In response to the agency's specific request for comments on expected costs and operational impacts, I recommend the agency publish a formal cost-benefit analysis that explicitly monetizes the public health costs of reporting delays—including hospitalization rates, outbreak investigation costs, and litigation exposure—alongside industry compliance costs. The final rule should establish a tiered submission frequency framework: higher-risk product categories (e.g., dietary supplements, novel food ingredients, combination medical devices) should face more frequent mandatory reporting intervals, while lower-risk categories may qualify for extended timelines. This risk-stratified approach addresses legitimate operational burden concerns without compromising the agency's core safety mission [1].

*   **For Implementation Timelines:**
    *   The agency should restructure the implementation timeline into at least three distinct phases: (1) a planning and capacity-assessment phase with a formal agency check-in, (2) a pilot or partial-compliance phase with feedback mechanisms, and (3) a full-compliance phase.
    *   The agency should also publish a technical assistance roadmap for entities—including academic institutions and research collaborators—that lack dedicated compliance infrastructure [8, 9].

*   **For Documentation Standards:**
    *   The agency should explicitly align its new documentation standards with the formal reporting standards for legal research in public health studies called for by scholars at the Center for Public Health Law Research (Temple University) [2, 3]. Specifically, the agency should: (1) require that documentation include structured metadata fields that enable downstream legal epidemiological analysis; (2) publish a companion methodological guide explaining how the documentation standards were designed and what data elements they are intended to capture; and (3) commit to periodic public release of aggregated, de-identified documentation data to support independent academic research.
    *   The agency should adopt the proposed documentation standards as a foundational floor, not a ceiling. Specifically, FDA should: (1) align the new standards with the ongoing CBER-CDER Data Standards Program framework to ensure interoperability across centers; (2) publish a clear crosswalk between the new documentation requirements and existing electronic submission standards so regulated entities can map compliance obligations without duplicative effort; and (3) commit to a structured post-implementation review at 24 months to assess whether the standards are generating the data quality improvements anticipated, with findings made publicly available to support independent academic evaluation [4, 5].
    *   In response to the agency's specific question on operational impacts, I recommend the agency commission or reference the AHRQ systematic evidence review framework on measuring documentation burden before finalizing these standards [2, 3]. Specifically, the agency should: (1) define 'documentation burden' explicitly in the regulatory text and establish a mechanism for regulated entities to flag when requirements are generating excessive burden relative to informational value; (2) pilot-test the documentation standards with a representative sample of regulated entities before full implementation, measuring both compliance costs and the analytical utility of the resulting records; and (3) build in a two-year post-implementation review with public reporting on whether the documentation standards are achieving their stated goal of improving information quality.

*   **For Overall Cost-Benefit and Stakeholder Support:**
    *   In the final rule preamble, FDA should: (1) publish a transparent, quantified cost-benefit analysis that includes not only direct compliance costs to industry but also the estimated value of improved safety signal detection, reduced post-market safety failures, and enhanced regulatory decision quality — drawing on available health economics literature on the cost of preventable adverse events; (2) explicitly connect the documentation standards to FDA's Real-World Evidence Program and Data Standards Program as part of a coherent institutional knowledge strategy, so the rule is understood as infrastructure investment rather than isolated compliance burden; and (3) commit to publishing periodic public assessments of how the improved data quality is translating into regulatory outcomes, creating an accountability mechanism that also builds the evidentiary record for the rule's long-term defense [4, 5]. This approach directly addresses the agency's solicitation for comments on expected costs by reframing the cost question within a full benefit accounting.

[CONCLUSION]
In conclusion, I strongly support the proposed rule FDA-2024-XXXX. These structural reforms are essential for FDA's regulatory modernization, enhanced post-market safety authority, and the advancement of evidence-based approaches in all regulated areas, including critical public health challenges like opioid prescribing and tobacco regulation. By improving data quality, standardizing reporting, and establishing rigorous documentation, FDA will be better equipped to detect emerging threats, evaluate product performance, and make informed regulatory decisions that protect the public. The long-term public health benefits of these improvements will far outweigh the initial compliance costs, and I urge the agency to finalize this rule with the enhancements outlined above to maximize its public health impact.

### References
[1] https://www.fda.gov/food/outbreaks-foodborne-illness/core-2024-annual-report
[2] https://effectivehealthcare.ahrq.gov/sites/default/files/related_files/documentation-burden-prepub-technical-brief.pdf
[3] https://effectivehealthcare.ahrq.gov/sites/default/files/related_files/documentation-burden-prepub-disposition-comments.pdf
[4] https://www.fda.gov/media/177177/download
[5] https://www.fda.gov/media/185592/download
[6] https://www.fda.gov/media/188153/download
[8] https://www.thehealthcareexecutive.net/article/navigating-regulatory-changes-healthcare-2024-guide-executives/
[9] https://www.meditologyservices.com/navigating-the-ripples-of-change-hipaas-2023-2024-evolution-and-preparation-strategies-for-healthcare-organizations/

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The Washington State Department of Health (WA DOH) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the proposed rule, Docket FDA-2024-XXXX, regarding revised reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards. As a state agency responsible for protecting public health across a diverse range of regulated industries, including drug and device establishments, cannabis product safety, shellfish sanitation, and food safety, WA DOH strongly supports the agency's efforts to modernize and standardize oversight. Our expertise in implementing and enforcing public health regulations at the state level provides unique insights into the operational impacts and best practices for successful regulatory transitions.

[BACKGROUND]
WA DOH is submitting comments specifically on the proposed rule's core regulatory changes: the revision of existing reporting obligations, the establishment of specific implementation timelines for new mandates, and the introduction of more rigorous documentation standards. We believe that, with thoughtful design and implementation, these changes have the potential to significantly enhance public health protection, improve data quality, and reduce duplicative regulatory burdens through strengthened state-federal coordination.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
WA DOH has identified several key areas where the proposed changes intersect with our regulatory responsibilities, presenting both opportunities for enhancement and potential operational challenges.

**Cannabis Product Safety Regulation and Implementation Timelines:**
WA DOH strongly supports the establishment of specific implementation timelines for new cannabis product safety standards, and urges the agency to adopt a phased timeline structure that explicitly accounts for the regulatory gap created by cannabis's current federal scheduling status. Because cannabis remains a Schedule I controlled substance under federal law (pending DEA rescheduling proceedings initiated in 2024), FDA has not exercised its full regulatory authority over cannabis products, leaving WA DOH to develop, implement, and enforce safety standards largely in isolation from the federal infrastructure that supports other food and drug safety programs [4]. Clear, binding implementation timelines in the proposed rule would give WA DOH a federal anchor point to align our state standards, reducing the patchwork compliance burden on regulated entities operating across state lines and strengthening our ability to enforce consistent safety benchmarks. Without defined federal timelines, WA DOH must continually manage a regulatory gap that creates consumer safety risks and industry confusion.

However, WA DOH also recommends the agency build in explicit compliance ramp-up provisions for cannabis product testing and documentation requirements. State-level data, including New York's experience showing a 0.56% lot failure rate among tested cannabis products, demonstrates that a functioning testing infrastructure takes significant time to mature and that premature hard deadlines can undermine, rather than enhance, product safety outcomes [3]. Implementing rigorous documentation standards on an accelerated timeline risks overwhelming both state agency review capacity and regulated industry compliance infrastructure simultaneously. If the agency sets implementation timelines that are too compressed, WA DOH anticipates a surge in incomplete or inaccurate submissions that will strain our inspection and enforcement resources, divert staff from other critical public health functions (including shellfish sanitation monitoring and food safety inspections), and potentially drive smaller cannabis operators toward non-compliance or market exit—outcomes that reduce overall product safety oversight. Responding directly to the agency's request for comments on operational impacts, WA DOH estimates that a compressed timeline (under 18 months) would require WA DOH to hire and train additional compliance review staff, at a cost we estimate in the range of $500,000–$1.2 million in the first implementation year, without a corresponding increase in federal funding support.

**Shellfish Sanitation and Climate Change Impacts on Documentation and Reporting:**
WA DOH strongly supports the revised reporting obligations as a necessary modernization of data collection standards, and urges the agency to design the new requirements in a manner that is interoperable with the National Shellfish Sanitation Program (NSSP) framework to avoid duplicative reporting burdens on state shellfish sanitation programs. WA DOH administers Washington State's shellfish sanitation program as a cooperative partner under the NSSP—the federal-state framework recognized by FDA and the Interstate Shellfish Sanitation Conference (ISSC). Our program already collects and submits bacteriological sampling data, growing area classification reports, and shoreline survey results on schedules dictated by NSSP Model Ordinance requirements [5]. If the proposed revised reporting obligations introduce new frequency, format, or content requirements that diverge from NSSP standards, WA DOH staff would be required to maintain parallel data pipelines—one for NSSP compliance and one for this rulemaking—effectively doubling administrative labor without producing additional public health value. Our shellfish program operates with a fixed workforce responsible for monitoring hundreds of classified growing areas along Washington's coastline, and any non-harmonized reporting mandate would divert staff time from field sampling and classification work that directly protects public health.

Furthermore, WA DOH supports the proposed modernization of documentation standards but urges the agency to explicitly account for the compounding operational costs that climate change is imposing on state shellfish sanitation programs when setting implementation timelines and estimating expected costs—a direct response to the agency's request for comments on expected costs and operational impacts. Washington's shellfish growing areas are experiencing accelerating climate-driven stressors—including ocean acidification, harmful algal bloom events of increasing frequency and geographic range, and altered precipitation patterns that affect fecal coliform loading in coastal watersheds [5]. These stressors are already requiring WA DOH to increase the frequency of water quality sampling, conduct more frequent reclassification reviews, and issue more emergency harvest closures than historical baselines would predict. Layering new documentation and reporting requirements on top of this expanding workload during the proposed implementation window will strain program capacity precisely when field monitoring demands are at their highest. The agency's cost estimates for state implementers appear to assume a static baseline workload, which does not reflect the operational reality facing coastal shellfish programs in the Pacific Northwest.

**Drug and Device Establishment Inspections and Documentation Standards:**
WA DOH strongly supports the proposed documentation standards for drug and device establishment inspections, as standardized record-keeping requirements will directly improve the quality and consistency of state-level inspection outcomes and reduce duplicative regulatory burden when coordinating with FDA on shared inspection targets. WA DOH conducts drug and device establishment inspections under state authority and frequently coordinates with FDA on CGMP compliance reviews [1]. Currently, inconsistent documentation formats across establishments create significant friction during joint or sequential inspections—state inspectors must reconcile records presented in varying formats, increasing per-inspection labor hours and delaying compliance determinations. Standardized documentation standards would allow WA DOH inspectors to more efficiently review records, cross-reference FDA inspection findings (as reported in FDA's FY 2024 Annual Report on Inspections of Establishments), and reduce the administrative overhead associated with translating facility-specific record formats into state reporting systems. This directly addresses the agency's question on operational impacts: for a state agency conducting inspections with limited FTE resources, documentation standardization is a force multiplier.

However, WA DOH supports the proposed documentation standards but urges the agency to carefully calibrate implementation timelines and associated compliance costs to avoid disproportionate burden on smaller in-state drug and device establishments, which could inadvertently reduce the number of licensed facilities and shrink Washington State's regulated manufacturing base. Washington State is home to a range of drug and device establishments, including small and mid-sized manufacturers. The agency's question on expected costs is directly relevant here: WA DOH's inspection program depends on a viable, licensed establishment population to justify inspection resource allocation and fee structures. As illustrated by California's CDPH model—which requires annual legislative reporting on licensing and inspection costs [2]—compliance cost escalation can suppress facility registration and licensure. If new documentation standards impose significant upfront IT or records management investments, smaller Washington establishments may delay compliance, operate out of conformance, or exit the market. This would reduce WA DOH's inspection caseload, undermine public health oversight, and create gaps in the state's drug and device supply chain.

**Interstate Food Safety Compacts and Revised Reporting Obligations:**
WA DOH strongly supports the revised reporting obligations as a necessary modernization of data collection standards, and urges the agency to design the new submission formats to be explicitly compatible with interstate food safety compact data-sharing frameworks, which will reduce duplicative reporting burdens on state agencies while improving national food safety surveillance. WA DOH participates in interstate food safety compacts that require coordinated data submissions across multiple jurisdictions. Currently, our agency must maintain parallel reporting systems—one for federal compliance and one for compact partner states—because federal data formats are not harmonized with compact reporting templates [6], [7]. The proposed revision to reporting frequency, format, and content presents a direct opportunity to resolve this inefficiency. If the new federal format is designed without compact interoperability in mind, WA DOH will face increased operational burden: our food safety staff will be required to reformat and re-enter the same underlying inspection and surveillance data into multiple systems, diverting resources from frontline food safety activities including shellfish sanitation monitoring and cannabis product safety regulation.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
Based on our analysis of the proposed rule's impacts on WA DOH's regulatory functions, we offer the following specific recommendations to enhance public health protection and improve state-federal coordination:

1.  **For Cannabis Product Safety Implementation Timelines:**
    *   Adopt a three-phase implementation timeline: (1) an initial 12-month period for regulated entities to conduct gap assessments and update internal documentation systems; (2) a 24-month period for full compliance with revised reporting obligations and documentation standards; and (3) a 36-month review checkpoint at which the agency evaluates compliance rates and adjusts enforcement priorities. This approach, similar to corrective action frameworks used in Canadian cannabis inspection programs, would reduce operational disruption while maintaining safety standards [3].
    *   Explicitly coordinate these timelines with the ongoing DEA rescheduling process—given that the prior administration published a notice of proposed rulemaking to move cannabis to Schedule III and the DOJ has announced procedural updates to expedite that process [4]—so that federal cannabis product safety timelines do not conflict with or duplicate emerging FDA jurisdiction. WA DOH recommends the agency publish a clear statement of how these timelines interact with anticipated changes in federal scheduling status.

2.  **For Shellfish Sanitation Documentation and Reporting:**
    *   Formally coordinate with FDA's NSSP program office and the Interstate Shellfish Sanitation Conference before finalizing revised reporting formats and frequencies for shellfish-related data. Specifically, the agency should (1) adopt data field definitions and submission formats that are compatible with existing NSSP electronic reporting infrastructure; (2) allow state shellfish programs to satisfy new federal reporting obligations through a single submission to a shared federal data repository rather than separate agency-specific portals; and (3) grandfather existing NSSP-compliant reporting schedules as meeting the new frequency requirements where those schedules already satisfy the underlying public health rationale. This approach would achieve the agency's stated goal of improving data quality without imposing redundant operational costs on state partners [5].
    *   Extend the implementation timeline for new documentation standards by a minimum of 18–24 months to allow state programs to absorb new requirements without reducing field monitoring capacity.
    *   Provide dedicated federal technical assistance funding or grant mechanisms to help state shellfish programs build the data management infrastructure needed to comply with revised reporting formats.
    *   Commission a formal assessment of climate-related workload trends in state shellfish programs before finalizing cost estimates, so that the regulatory impact analysis reflects actual rather than historical baseline conditions [5].

3.  **For Drug and Device Establishment Documentation Standards:**
    *   Explicitly design the new documentation standards to be interoperable with state health agency inspection workflows, not solely FDA investigator processes [1].
    *   Publish a companion guidance document—analogous to FDA's 2017 FDARA-mandated guidance on device establishment inspection processes—that specifies how state partners can accept and rely upon standardized records during their own inspections, thereby avoiding duplicative record requests to establishments [2].
    *   Publish a tiered implementation timeline that grants small establishments (e.g., those below a defined revenue or employee threshold) an extended compliance window of no less than 24 months, with phased milestones [2].
    *   Provide model documentation templates at no cost to reduce the burden of format development.
    *   Conduct a formal small entity impact analysis and share results with state health agencies prior to finalizing the rule, so states can anticipate and plan for any shifts in their regulated establishment populations [2].

4.  **For Interstate Food Safety Compacts and Reporting Obligations:**
    *   Convene a working group with interstate food safety compact member states—including WA DOH—prior to finalizing the data submission format specifications [6], [7].
    *   Include a provision in the final rule explicitly requiring that the new reporting schema be published in a machine-readable, open standard format (e.g., JSON or XML) that compact states can map directly to their own reporting systems.
    *   Establish a phased implementation timeline of no less than 18 months to allow compact member states to update their data infrastructure accordingly.

[CONCLUSION]
The Washington State Department of Health strongly supports the agency's goals of modernizing and standardizing regulatory oversight through revised reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards. By carefully considering the operational realities and unique challenges faced by state public health agencies—particularly those navigating emerging regulatory landscapes like cannabis, climate change impacts on established programs like shellfish sanitation, and the ongoing need for efficient state-federal partnership in areas like drug and device inspections and food safety compacts—the agency can finalize a rule that truly enhances public health protection while minimizing unnecessary burden on state partners and regulated entities. We appreciate the opportunity to provide these comments and look forward to continued collaboration.
