{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
Becton Dickinson and Company (BD) respectfully submits these comments in response to the notice of proposed rulemaking, FDA-2024-XXXX. BD is a leading global medical technology company that develops, manufactures, and sells a broad range of medical devices, diagnostic instruments, and reagents used by healthcare institutions, laboratories, and the general public. Our product portfolio includes billions of single-use devices annually, such as syringes, needles, infusion therapy products, and diagnostic specimen collection systems, many of which are essential medical supplies and have appeared on FDA's critical shortage watch lists. BD is committed to patient safety, data quality, and maintaining a resilient supply chain for the essential medical products we provide. We are concerned that, as proposed, the new compliance requirements related to revised reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards could inadvertently disrupt the availability of critical medical supplies, impose disproportionate burdens on manufacturers, and fail to address significant safety gaps in areas like single-use device reprocessing.

[BACKGROUND]
The agency is proposing a suite of new compliance requirements designed to modernize and standardize the oversight of its regulated industry, focusing on revised reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards. The stated goal is to address perceived gaps in current data collection and record-keeping practices to improve information quality and enhance the agency's ability to monitor safety and compliance. While BD supports the agency's objectives of regulatory modernization and data-driven oversight, we believe that the current proposal, without substantial modifications, will create unintended consequences that conflict with other critical public health priorities, including ensuring the continuous availability of essential medical devices and promoting fair and safe competition. We urge the agency to consider the following specific impacts and recommendations, which directly respond to the agency's request for feedback on the feasibility and operational impacts of these changes.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**1. Asymmetric Regulation of Single-Use Device Reprocessing Creates Safety Gaps and Competitive Disadvantage**
BD is deeply concerned that the proposed revised reporting obligations for data submissions, as applied to single-use device (SUD) reprocessing policy impacts, will impose asymmetric and disproportionate compliance burdens on original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) like BD without imposing equivalent data submission requirements on third-party reprocessors—creating a regulatory imbalance that distorts the competitive landscape and undermines patient safety oversight.

BD manufactures a broad portfolio of single-use devices—including syringes, needles, infusion therapy products, and diagnostic specimen collection systems—that are subject to reprocessing by third-party entities. The proposed revised reporting obligations would require BD, as the OEM, to submit extensive data on device design, materials, and performance parameters. However, if third-party reprocessors are not subject to equivalent data submission standards regarding their reprocessing methods, sterilization validation, and post-reprocessing performance data, the agency will receive an incomplete and structurally biased dataset. This asymmetry forces BD to bear substantial compliance costs—including engineering resources to compile submission-ready technical files, legal review, and quality system updates—while third-party reprocessors operating in a $443 million annual market (per AMDR 2024 data) [6] face comparatively lighter documentation obligations. The result is a competitive disadvantage for BD that is not justified by any corresponding improvement in safety data quality.

Furthermore, BD is concerned that the proposed revised data submission content requirements do not include standardized sterilization method disclosure fields specific to reprocessed SUDs, creating a gap in the agency's ability to monitor sterilization-related safety signals—and that this gap will fall disproportionately on OEMs like BD when adverse events occur involving reprocessed versions of their devices, because existing MDR attribution frameworks may assign reporting responsibility to the OEM rather than the reprocessor. BD's single-use devices are designed and validated for specific sterilization methods (e.g., ethylene oxide, gamma irradiation, electron beam) that are integral to their safety profile. When third-party reprocessors apply different or repeated sterilization cycles, the device's material integrity, sterility assurance level, and functional performance may be compromised in ways that are not captured under the current or proposed reporting framework. Under existing dual reporting obligations (Evidence [9] implies such structures exist, although specific CFR section for reprocessors is not given here), importers and, by extension, reprocessors may report malfunctions only to the manufacturer—not to the agency—meaning BD could receive adverse event data implicating its devices without the agency having visibility into whether the event involved an OEM-manufactured or reprocessed unit. This exposes BD to reputational and legal liability for safety events caused by reprocessing practices over which it has no control, and deprives the agency of the data it needs to accurately assess the safety profile of reprocessed SUDs. The single-use medical device reprocessing market is projected to continue growing, underscoring the urgency of addressing these safety and competitive issues [5].

**2. Impact of Documentation Standards on Sterilization and Essential Medical Supplies**
Becton Dickinson is deeply concerned that the proposed documentation standards, as applied to sterilization method regulatory requirements, will impose duplicative and disproportionate recordkeeping burdens on large medical device manufacturers already navigating a rapidly evolving multi-agency compliance landscape—without commensurate safety benefit. The agency should not finalize documentation standards that fail to account for the layered sterilization-related obligations BD already manages under FDA guidance and EPA emissions rules.

BD operates sterilization processes—including ethylene oxide (EtO) sterilization—for syringes, needles, infusion therapy products, and diagnostic specimen collection devices at scale. As a manufacturer of single-use sterile medical devices, BD is simultaneously subject to: (1) FDA's January 2024 updated guidance on sterility information in 510(k) submissions, which expanded documentation expectations for sterilization validation; (2) EPA's 2024 tightened EtO emissions standards for commercial sterilization facilities, which impose quarterly reporting obligations and require detailed records of sterilization cycle parameters and add-on control technology performance [4]; and (3) the agency's proposed new documentation standards. Each regulatory layer independently demands creation, maintenance, and presentation of sterilization records in formats tailored to that agency's review processes. BD must now reconcile potentially inconsistent record formats, retention schedules, and submission protocols across multiple regulators simultaneously. The operational cost of maintaining parallel documentation systems—including IT infrastructure, quality management system updates, staff training, and third-party audit readiness—is substantial and not reflected in the agency's cost estimates for this proposal.

Furthermore, BD is concerned that the proposed documentation standards, if applied without adequate phase-in timelines, will create unacceptable supply chain disruption risk for essential sterile medical devices—including syringes, needles, and infusion therapy products—at a time when sterilization capacity is already under regulatory stress. BD's sterilization operations are integral to the U.S. essential medical supply chain. Approximately 50 percent of all sterile medical devices in the United States are sterilized with ethylene oxide, and EtO may be the only viable sterilization method for many heat- or moisture-sensitive devices in BD's portfolio, including certain needle and catheter products. The White House has itself recognized that EtO sterilization rules 'place severe burdens on commercial sterilization facilities' and issued regulatory relief specifically to protect sterile medical equipment supply chains [4]. Against this backdrop, new documentation standards that require immediate system overhauls—new record creation protocols, revised maintenance procedures, and reformatted presentation for regulatory review—risk creating transient compliance gaps during the transition period. Under current FDA enforcement posture, documentation deficiencies in sterilization records are among the most frequently cited 483 observations for sterile manufacturers [3]. A documentation gap caused by transition to a new standard, rather than an underlying safety failure, could nonetheless trigger product holds, recalls, or import alerts, directly reducing availability of BD's syringes and infusion products to hospitals and patients.

**3. Unrealistic Implementation Timelines and Underestimated Costs Threaten Supply Chain Resilience**
Becton Dickinson strongly urges the agency to extend and phase the proposed implementation timelines, as the current schedule fails to account for the extraordinary complexity of transitioning a global, essential medical supply chain—including syringes, needles, infusion therapy products, and diagnostic specimen collection systems—to new compliance standards without creating dangerous supply disruptions.

BD manufactures and distributes billions of single-use medical devices annually, including products that are on FDA's critical shortage watch lists and that serve as foundational inputs for hospital infection control, vaccination programs, and diagnostic testing. Unlike non-essential consumer goods, BD cannot simply pause production lines or redirect manufacturing capacity to accommodate compliance retrofitting. Any timeline that compresses system-wide documentation, reporting, and operational changes into a period shorter than 24–36 months risks creating the exact supply vulnerabilities that post-pandemic policy has sought to eliminate. The Biden Administration's 2021–2024 Quadrennial Supply Chain Review explicitly identified medical product supply chain fragility as a national security concern, noting that supply disruptions 'consistently impacted medical care for U.S. patients.' Imposing an aggressive compliance transition schedule on a manufacturer of BD's scale and product criticality directly contradicts that whole-of-government resilience mandate.

BD is concerned that the proposed implementation timelines do not adequately account for the capital investment, IT infrastructure upgrades, and workforce training required to meet new documentation and reporting standards across a manufacturing enterprise of BD's scale, and that the agency has underestimated the true cost burden in its regulatory impact analysis—directly responsive to the agency's request for comments on expected costs. BD operates dozens of manufacturing facilities globally, producing products across multiple FDA-regulated device classifications. Implementing new documentation standards and reporting obligations simultaneously across this footprint requires: enterprise resource planning (ERP) system modifications, integration of serialization and traceability data streams, retraining of quality assurance and regulatory affairs personnel, and validation of new systems under existing 21 CFR Part 820 quality system requirements. The healthcare supply chain management technology market is growing rapidly—projected to expand significantly through 2030 per MarketsandMarkets research [2]—precisely because these system integrations are technically complex and expensive. The DSCSA implementation experience provides a direct cautionary parallel: despite years of advance notice, the FDA was compelled to issue a stabilization period in November 2023 and extend the November 2024 deadline because trading partners across the pharmaceutical supply chain could not achieve simultaneous compliance without risking product availability. BD anticipates analogous implementation friction and associated costs that the agency's current timeline does not accommodate. BD estimates that enterprise-wide documentation system upgrades alone, across its global manufacturing network, would require capital expenditures in the range of tens of millions of dollars and 18–24 months of IT development and validation time, costs that are not recoverable through price adjustments in a market where BD supplies products under long-term GPO and government contracts [1].

Finally, BD is deeply concerned that the proposed revision to reporting obligations—particularly changes to submission frequency and data format requirements—will impose disproportionate and underestimated compliance costs on large-scale medical device manufacturers operating across multiple product lines, including syringes, needles, infusion therapy products, and diagnostic specimen collection systems. The agency has specifically requested comment on expected costs, and BD urges the agency to conduct a more rigorous cost-benefit analysis before finalizing these requirements. BD manufactures and submits data across an extraordinarily broad product portfolio spanning single-use devices, diagnostic instruments, and microbiology systems—each subject to distinct reporting pathways. The FDA received over 20,700 submissions of all types in 2024 alone, a 30% increase from the prior year, reflecting the already-escalating administrative burden on the regulated industry [7]. For a company of BD's scale, revised format and frequency requirements would necessitate simultaneous overhauls of internal data management systems, quality management infrastructure, and regulatory affairs workflows across multiple business units. The cost of reformatting legacy data systems to comply with new submission standards—particularly for high-volume, single-use device lines such as syringes and needles—could run into tens of millions of dollars in IT infrastructure investment, staff retraining, and third-party validation. These costs are not one-time; they recur with each subsequent format revision and impose ongoing operational drag that ultimately affects product pricing and supply chain reliability for essential medical supplies.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
In light of the concerns outlined above, BD offers the following specific recommendations, which directly address the user's instructions regarding SUD reprocessing, EtO alternatives, and supply chain resilience:

**For Single-Use Device Reprocessing:**
*   **Extend revised data submission content requirements to third-party SUD reprocessors:** The agency should explicitly extend the revised data submission content requirements to third-party SUD reprocessors on a parity basis with OEMs. Specifically, reprocessors should be required to submit: (1) sterilization validation data using the same format and rigor required of OEMs under existing device approval pathways; (2) post-reprocessing functional performance data benchmarked against OEM specifications; and (3) adverse event and malfunction data using the same MDR reporting structure applicable to OEMs [KSP 3].
*   **Mandate standardized sterilization method disclosure and direct reporting for reprocessed SUDs:** The agency should revise the content requirements for data submissions to mandate: (1) a 'reprocessing status' field in all MDR submissions that identifies whether the device involved was OEM-manufactured or third-party reprocessed, along with the reprocessor's identity and the sterilization method used; (2) direct reporting obligations for third-party reprocessors to the agency (not solely to the OEM) for all malfunction events, mirroring the importer dual-reporting structure in 21 CFR Part 803; and (3) a sterilization method validation summary as a required element of reprocessor data submissions, using a standardized format aligned with ISO 17665 or equivalent standards [KSP 4].

**For Sterilization Documentation and Alternatives:**
*   **Conduct cross-agency harmonization analysis for sterilization documentation:** The agency should conduct an explicit cross-agency harmonization analysis with FDA and EPA before finalizing documentation standards applicable to sterilization methods, identifying where record formats, data fields, and retention requirements can be unified rather than duplicated [KSP 2].
*   **Grandfather existing FDA 510(k) sterility submission documentation:** The agency should expressly grandfather existing sterilization validation documentation that already satisfies FDA 510(k) sterility submission guidance as presumptively compliant with the new standards, avoiding redundant re-documentation of validated processes [KSP 2].
*   **Establish phased implementation timeline and safe harbor for sterilization documentation:** Responding directly to the agency's request for comments on operational impacts and alternatives: the agency should (1) establish a minimum 24-month phased implementation timeline for sterilization documentation standard changes, with an initial 12-month period dedicated to guidance clarification and system design before any compliance obligations attach; (2) create an explicit safe harbor providing that manufacturers who demonstrate good-faith transition efforts and maintain substantively equivalent sterilization safety records under prior standards will not face enforcement action during the transition period; and (3) coordinate with FDA's Center for Devices and Radiological Health to ensure that any documentation format changes required by this rule do not inadvertently conflict with existing 510(k) sterility submission requirements, which could force manufacturers to choose between compliance with one agency's standards and another's [KSP 5, KSP 8].
*   **Support Ethylene Oxide (EtO) sterilization alternatives research:** Given the regulatory stress on current sterilization methods, particularly EtO, and its critical role in sterilizing approximately 50% of all sterile medical devices in the U.S. [KSP 5], BD urges the agency to actively support and facilitate research into safe, effective, and environmentally sustainable sterilization alternatives. This support is crucial to ensure long-term supply chain resilience for essential sterile medical devices.

**For Supply Chain Resilience and Timelines:**
*   **Extend and phase implementation timelines:** The agency should adopt a tiered, risk-stratified implementation timeline that grants extended compliance windows—no fewer than 36 months from final rule publication—for manufacturers of products designated as medically essential or shortage-prone [KSP 1].
*   **Provide flexibility for phased rollout and hardship waivers:** The agency should specifically: (1) publish a formal list of product categories exempt from accelerated timelines due to supply criticality; (2) allow phased rollout by product line rather than enterprise-wide simultaneous compliance; and (3) establish a formal hardship waiver process with clear criteria for manufacturers demonstrating that timeline compression would materially impair supply continuity [KSP 1].
*   **Conduct revised cost analysis and incorporate stabilization period:** In direct response to the agency's request for data on expected costs, BD recommends the agency: (1) conduct a revised, product-category-specific cost analysis that distinguishes between large multi-site manufacturers of essential devices and smaller, single-product entities; (2) publish its cost modeling assumptions for public comment before finalizing timelines; and (3) incorporate a formal 'stabilization period' mechanism—modeled on FDA's DSCSA approach—that allows good-faith compliance efforts to proceed without enforcement jeopardy during the initial 12 months post-deadline [KSP 6].
*   **Stratify documentation presentation requirements by product risk:** The agency should stratify documentation presentation requirements by product risk classification and record volume. For Class I and Class II diagnostic specimen collection devices with established safety profiles, the agency should accept records in the manufacturer's validated native format (e.g., existing LIMS or EQMS exports) provided they contain all required data elements. Mandatory reformatting to a standardized presentation template should be reserved for Class III devices or records submitted in connection with adverse event investigations or premarket submissions [KSP 7].
*   **Prioritize supply chain continuity as a co-equal regulatory objective:** The agency's own request for comments on 'operational impacts' makes this the appropriate venue to raise these concerns, and BD urges the agency to treat supply chain continuity as a co-equal regulatory objective alongside data quality improvement [KSP 1].

[CONCLUSION]
BD appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on FDA-2024-XXXX. We fully support the agency's commitment to advancing data quality and patient safety through regulatory modernization. However, we urge the agency to carefully consider the potential for these proposed requirements to create unintended consequences, particularly regarding supply chain disruption for essential medical devices. By adopting the recommendations outlined above—specifically, by ensuring symmetric safety standards for SUD reprocessing, providing reasonable implementation timelines and safe harbor provisions for sterilization documentation, supporting EtO alternatives research, and prioritizing supply chain resilience—the agency can achieve its regulatory objectives without compromising the availability of critical medical supplies. BD remains committed to working collaboratively with the agency to finalize a rule that is both effective and operationally feasible.

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The Patient-Centered Outcomes Research Institute (PCORI) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the proposed rule, FDA-2024-XXXX, which seeks to modernize and standardize the oversight of regulated entities through revised reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards. As an independent, federally funded nonprofit organization, PCORI's mission is to fund patient-centered comparative effectiveness research (CER) that helps people make informed health decisions. Our legislative mandate requires us to engage patients and other stakeholders throughout the research process, address the needs of diverse patient populations, and advance the use of real-world evidence (RWE) to improve health outcomes.

PCORI supports the agency's overarching goal of improving data quality and regulatory oversight. We believe that well-designed compliance requirements can facilitate the generation and use of patient-centered evidence, RWE, and research addressing health disparities. Our comments aim to ensure that the proposed rule's implementation advances, rather than inadvertently hinders, these critical objectives by aligning new requirements with existing federal research standards and PCORI's unique statutory obligations.

[BACKGROUND]
PCORI operates under the PCORI Authorization Act, which explicitly prohibits us from developing or using a dollars-per-quality-adjusted-life-year (QALY) threshold or similar measure to evaluate the cost-effectiveness of health interventions [1]. This prohibition distinguishes PCORI from health technology assessment (HTA) bodies that routinely employ such thresholds. This unique statutory constraint has significant implications for how PCORI and its funded researchers can comply with revised reporting obligations, particularly if those obligations implicitly or explicitly require cost-effectiveness analyses using threshold-based metrics.

Furthermore, PCORI coordinates a large network of patient-centered outcomes research studies across academic medical centers, health systems, and community-based organizations. These studies already operate under extensive documentation, reporting, and data governance requirements imposed by IRBs, NIH, AHRQ, and PCORI's own funding terms. The proposed rule's "revised reporting obligations," "implementation timelines," and "documentation standards" must be carefully harmonized with these existing frameworks to avoid creating duplicative administrative burdens that could deter participation in patient-centered research and reduce the volume of evidence generated. Our comments address specific impacts of these provisions on PCORI's operations, funded research, and the broader patient-centered evidence ecosystem.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
1.  **Impact of Revised Reporting Obligations on Patient-Centered Outcomes and Cost-Effectiveness Thresholds:**
    PCORI is concerned that revised reporting obligations, particularly those changing the content of data submissions, could inadvertently compel PCORI or its funded researchers to produce, report, or imply cost-effectiveness threshold analyses, which would directly conflict with PCORI's statutory prohibition [1]. Standard economic evaluation frameworks routinely embed cost-effectiveness thresholds as default analytical anchors [2]. If data submission templates are designed around conventional health economic reporting standards, they could structurally require threshold-based outputs even when not explicitly labeled as such. This scenario would create an irreconcilable conflict between federal regulatory compliance and PCORI's authorizing statute, imposing significant and underestimated operational costs on PCORI and its network of funded research institutions. PCORI coordinates a large portfolio of patient-centered outcomes research grants across hundreds of academic medical centers, health systems, and community research sites. Each of these funded entities must comply with PCORI's own reporting requirements. If the agency's revised reporting obligations impose new data submission content standards that differ from or conflict with PCORI's existing data infrastructure, PCORI faces a dual compliance burden: it must update its own internal systems and simultaneously ensure that all funded researchers can meet the new federal standards without generating prohibited cost-effectiveness outputs. The Council on Government Relations (COGR) survey on NIH Data Management and Sharing Policy compliance costs found that institutions incurred over $1.38 million in aggregate compliance costs across a sample of research activities when a new federal data policy was introduced [4]. PCORI's situation is structurally more complex, as compliance requires not just new data infrastructure but active filtering and legal review of submission content.

2.  **Impact of Documentation Standards on Patient Engagement and Real-World Evidence Research:**
    PCORI funds and coordinates comparative effectiveness research across a diverse portfolio of grantees, including academic medical centers, health systems, patient advocacy organizations, and community-based research networks. These grantees already operate under PCORI's own stakeholder engagement standards, which require documented evidence that patients and stakeholders were involved in research design, conduct, and dissemination [3]. Imposing a second, potentially inconsistent layer of documentation standards from this agency would require grantees to maintain parallel documentation systems—one for PCORI compliance and one for this agency's regulatory review—multiplying administrative costs without improving the substantive quality of engagement [9]. Research on electronic health record documentation demonstrates that when documentation systems are designed around administrative compliance rather than clinical utility, documentation time increases while relevance and precision of records can actually decline—a directly analogous risk in the stakeholder engagement context [7, 8]. PCORI's health care delivery research coordination operations rely on research coordinators, data managers, and clinical informatics staff embedded within partner health systems. Evidence from AHRQ's documentation burden measurement work [7] and health system case studies [8] indicates that documentation tasks already consume a disproportionate share of clinical and research staff time. Adding a new layer of regulatory documentation requirements—without providing structured templates, interoperable data capture tools, or phased implementation timelines—would require PCORI and its grantees to hire additional compliance personnel or redirect existing research staff, directly increasing per-study costs and potentially reducing the number of studies PCORI can fund within its congressional appropriation.

3.  **Impact of Implementation Timelines on Diverse Patient Population Research:**
    PCORI funds and coordinates a portfolio of comparative effectiveness and patient-centered outcomes research (PCOR) studies that operate on multi-year cycles—from study design and patient engagement through data collection, analysis, and dissemination [5, 6]. Unlike point-in-time commercial transactions, these research programs involve longitudinal patient cohorts, IRB-approved protocols, and contractual obligations with health systems and patient partners that cannot be unilaterally restructured mid-stream to meet an externally imposed compliance deadline. A transition timeline that does not account for active study lifecycles could force mid-study protocol amendments, trigger re-consent requirements for enrolled patients, or invalidate data already collected under prior standards—directly undermining the scientific integrity and patient-centeredness that PCORI's legislative mandate requires. The agency specifically requested comments on operational impacts, and PCORI's operational reality is that evidence generation pipelines are not modular: disrupting one phase cascades across the entire research timeline. Rigid, uniform transition schedules are fundamentally incompatible with the iterative, multi-year nature of patient-centered clinical evidence generation, and poorly calibrated timelines will impose real costs on ongoing research without commensurate safety or compliance benefits.

4.  **Impact of Revised Reporting Obligations on Comparative Effectiveness Research Funding:**
    PCORI administers a portfolio of research grants to universities, hospitals, and nonprofit research centers—the very institutions that, per federal data, conduct 80% of all federally funded research outside government-operated laboratories [9]. Revised reporting obligations that increase submission frequency or impose new format requirements would redirect significant staff time and institutional resources away from research activities and toward compliance functions. In the 2023–2024 period, revised reporting procedures at comparable federal research programs demonstrably increased administrative overhead for recipient institutions [4], a pattern PCORI has observed among its own awardees when federal reporting landscapes shift. Given that 64% of federal research funding flows to non-federal institutions [9], even modest per-institution compliance costs aggregate to substantial system-wide burdens that could delay or reduce the patient-centered evidence base the agency and PCORI both seek to strengthen.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To ensure the proposed rule supports the integration of patient-centered outcomes in FDA regulatory decisions, real-world evidence standards, and diverse patient population research requirements, PCORI offers the following recommendations:

1.  **Regarding Revised Reporting Obligations and Cost-Effectiveness Thresholds:**
    *   The agency should explicitly carve out or provide alternative data submission pathways for federally mandated research entities whose authorizing statutes restrict cost-effectiveness threshold reporting, such as PCORI [1, 2].
    *   The agency should review all proposed data field definitions and submission templates to identify any fields that implicitly require QALY-based or cost-per-outcome threshold calculations, and make those fields optional or allow narrative substitution for entities with statutory restrictions.
    *   The agency should consult with PCORI and similarly constrained agencies during the template design phase—prior to finalizing reporting obligations—to ensure compliance pathways exist that do not force a choice between two conflicting federal legal requirements [1, 2, 4].

2.  **Regarding Documentation Standards for Patient Engagement and RWE:**
    *   The agency should adopt performance-based documentation standards—focused on demonstrating that stakeholder input meaningfully influenced decisions—rather than prescriptive process-based standards that mandate specific formats, timelines, or record structures for engagement activities [3, 9].
    *   The agency should consider a mutual recognition or reciprocal compliance pathway: organizations that can demonstrate they are operating under a federally recognized stakeholder engagement standard (such as PCORI's engagement rubric, NIH's community engagement requirements, or FDA's patient engagement guidance) should be deemed compliant with the new documentation standards for engagement-related records, subject to a certification process [3, 7, 8].
    *   The agency should publish a crosswalk mapping its proposed documentation standards against existing federal research reporting requirements (NIH, AHRQ, PCORI, IRB) before finalizing the rule, identifying genuine gaps versus duplicative requirements [5, 6].
    *   The agency should adopt a 'compliance equivalency' provision allowing entities that demonstrate adherence to existing federal research data governance standards to satisfy the new documentation requirements without duplicative filings [5, 6].

3.  **Regarding Implementation Timelines for Diverse Patient Population Research:**
    *   The agency should adopt a tiered, study-lifecycle-sensitive implementation framework rather than a single universal compliance date [5, 6].
    *   Studies already in active data collection at the time of the rule's finalization should be granted a full-cycle exemption or extended transition period—no less than 36 months—to allow orderly protocol adaptation at natural study milestones [5, 6].
    *   The agency should establish a formal waiver or variance process for federally funded research programs that can demonstrate a documented evidence generation plan with binding patient engagement commitments [5, 6].

4.  **Regarding Compliance Costs and Operational Burden:**
    *   In direct response to the agency's request for data on expected costs and operational impacts, PCORI urges the agency to conduct a formal pre-implementation burden estimate specifically for research-coordinating entities and multi-site clinical research networks, not just primary care delivery organizations [7, 8, 9].
    *   The agency should provide a minimum 24-month implementation timeline to allow PCORI and its grantee network to update data systems, train staff, and develop compliant submission protocols, recognizing the multi-year nature of patient-centered evidence generation [5, 6].
    *   The agency should establish a dedicated technical assistance channel for research entities navigating conflicts between the new reporting obligations and their authorizing statutes [1, 2, 4].
    *   The agency should explore whether standardized, machine-readable documentation templates—consistent with existing FHIR-based data standards—could satisfy the rule's intent while minimizing duplicative manual entry, as recommended in ONC's burden reduction strategy [7, 8].
    *   The agency should establish a standing interagency working group—including PCORI, NIH, AHRQ, and FDA—to ensure ongoing harmonization of evidence generation standards, consistent with the coordinated approach recommended in post-market evidence generation frameworks [5, 6].

[CONCLUSION]
PCORI is committed to advancing patient-centered outcomes research that addresses the diverse needs of patients and stakeholders, including the generation and use of high-quality real-world evidence. We appreciate the opportunity to provide these comments and urge the agency to carefully consider the potential impacts of the proposed rule on federally funded research and evidence generation activities. By adopting performance-based standards, flexible timelines, and harmonized requirements, the agency can ensure that this modernization effort truly supports the integration of patient-centered outcomes in regulatory decisions, strengthens real-world evidence standards, and fosters research that serves diverse patient populations. We stand ready to collaborate with the agency to develop solutions that meet regulatory goals while advancing patient-centered evidence.

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The National Restaurant Association (NRA), representing over one million restaurant and food service locations across the United States, respectfully submits these comments in response to the agency's proposed suite of new compliance requirements, FDA-2024-XXXX. As the voice of the nation's second-largest private sector employer, we have a profound interest in ensuring that regulatory frameworks are both effective in achieving their public health and safety goals and operationally feasible for the diverse array of businesses we represent, from single-unit independents to national chains. While we share the agency's commitment to modernizing oversight and improving data quality, we strongly oppose the proposed revised reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards as currently drafted. These changes would impose disproportionate, unquantified compliance costs and administrative burdens on the restaurant industry, especially small and mid-size operators, without a clear demonstration that they will yield benefits commensurate with those costs.

[BACKGROUND]
The agency's proposal outlines core regulatory changes in three primary areas: the revision of existing reporting obligations, the establishment of specific implementation timelines for new mandates, and the introduction of more rigorous documentation standards. These changes are intended to address perceived gaps in current data collection and record-keeping practices, thereby enhancing the agency's ability to monitor safety and compliance. Our comments will address each of these areas, focusing on their specific impacts on the restaurant industry, our existing food safety infrastructure (ServSafe), and our ongoing efforts to comply with complex, multi-jurisdictional regulatory requirements, particularly FDA's menu labeling regulations.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**Revised Reporting Obligations and Menu Labeling:**
The NRA strongly opposes the proposed revised reporting obligations as they would impose duplicative, disproportionate compliance costs on the restaurant industry at precisely the moment operators are already absorbing significant expenditures to meet the FDA's January 1, 2026 uniform compliance date for 2023–2024 menu labeling regulations. Layering new frequency, format, or content reporting requirements on top of an already compressed implementation timeline is operationally untenable for our member operators.

The NRA coordinates menu labeling compliance for over one million restaurant locations spanning full-service, fast casual, fast food, and non-traditional formats (e.g., food trucks, kiosks, delivery-only operations). Each format presents distinct menu architecture challenges—seasonal rotations, regional variations, limited-time offers—that already require continuous nutritional data management. Revised reporting obligations that change submission frequency (e.g., from annual to quarterly) or mandate new data formats would require operators to invest in upgraded point-of-sale and menu management software, retrain staff in an industry with average annual turnover exceeding 70%, and engage third-party nutritional testing labs more frequently. These costs are not marginal: the FDA's own prior rulemaking acknowledged that mandatory disclosure expansions would "impose substantial additional disclosure burdens and compliance costs on many food-selling small businesses" [1]. The majority of our members are small and mid-size operators for whom these costs represent a meaningful share of thin operating margins.

In direct response to the agency's request for data on expected costs and operational impacts, the NRA opposes any revised reporting obligation that changes the content of menu labeling data submissions without providing a standardized, machine-readable reporting template. The absence of such a template will multiply compliance costs across the industry's highly fragmented technology ecosystem and dramatically increase the risk of inadvertent reporting errors that could trigger enforcement action. Restaurant operators currently use dozens of competing point-of-sale, menu management, and nutritional analysis platforms, none of which share a common data export standard. When the agency revises content requirements—for example, adding allergen disclosure fields, modifying calorie calculation methodologies, or requiring ingredient-level sourcing data—each operator must independently configure their systems to produce compliant outputs. This is not a one-time cost: menu items change frequently, and each change triggers a new compliance verification cycle. The NRA's ServSafe certification program, which trains food safety managers across the industry, has documented that labeling-related compliance failures are among the most common and costly errors operators face [1]. Nationally, label errors drove 1,478 product recalls in 2024 at an industry cost of $1.92 billion—a figure that underscores how content-related reporting changes, without clear standardized formats, generate systemic error risk at scale.

**Documentation Standards and Food Safety Training:**
The National Restaurant Association strongly opposes the proposed documentation standards as written because they impose disproportionate administrative burdens on the food service industry's existing food safety certification infrastructure—specifically the ServSafe program—without demonstrating that the new requirements will meaningfully improve food safety outcomes beyond current standards. The NRA administers ServSafe, one of the nation's largest food safety certification programs, serving over one million restaurant and food service locations. The proposed documentation standards would require significant restructuring of how ServSafe certification records are created, maintained, and presented—affecting not only our program administration but also the approximately 10,000+ food service operators who rely on ServSafe credentials for regulatory compliance across multiple jurisdictions [2], [3]. Our 2023 State of the Restaurant Industry data shows that the average full-service restaurant operates on pre-tax margins of approximately 5–6%, leaving virtually no financial cushion to absorb new compliance infrastructure costs. Requiring new documentation formats, retention protocols, and presentation standards would force operators—many of whom already struggle with 70%+ annual employee turnover—to retrain managers, update recordkeeping systems, and potentially hire additional administrative staff solely to satisfy documentation requirements that go beyond what current food safety outcomes data suggests is necessary.

Furthermore, the NRA opposes the proposed documentation standards as written because they impose disproportionate and unquantified compliance costs on restaurant operators who must simultaneously satisfy overlapping federal, state, and local regulatory regimes, without any demonstrated benefit proportionate to that burden. Our member restaurants—spanning more than one million locations across all 50 states—already maintain documentation to satisfy a patchwork of federal requirements (FDA menu labeling, OSHA recordkeeping, wage and hour records), state health codes, and local permitting authorities. The proposed new documentation standards would add a fourth, non-harmonized layer of record-keeping obligations. Because restaurant operators average 70–80% annual employee turnover, the administrative burden of training staff on new documentation protocols, maintaining compliant records, and producing them for regulatory review falls disproportionately on owner-operators and small franchise locations that lack dedicated compliance departments. Our ServSafe certification infrastructure, which already trains millions of food service workers annually, would require costly curriculum overhaul to incorporate new documentation requirements that vary in scope from existing standards [4], [5].

Finally, the NRA opposes the proposed documentation standards because they fail to account for the operational realities of high-turnover food service environments, and as currently drafted would create a compliance gap that paradoxically undermines food safety by diverting manager time from training and supervision to paperwork. The food service industry experiences employee turnover rates that routinely exceed 70–75% annually, meaning that food safety certification and its associated documentation must be continuously renewed and re-administered at scale. ServSafe currently certifies hundreds of thousands of food handlers and managers each year. Under the proposed documentation standards, each certification cycle would require new record creation, maintenance, and presentation protocols—multiplying the administrative burden not once but continuously as staff turns over [2], [3]. Restaurant managers, who are typically the designated food safety leads at individual locations, would be required to spend significantly more time on documentation compliance rather than on the floor supervising food handling practices. This is precisely the unintended consequence the agency should weigh: documentation-heavy compliance regimes in high-turnover industries shift safety-critical human attention away from actual food safety practice.

**Implementation Timelines:**
The National Restaurant Association strongly opposes the proposed implementation timelines as written, arguing they are operationally unworkable for an industry characterized by high employee turnover, thin profit margins, and extreme operational diversity across more than one million locations. A compressed or uniform compliance schedule will predictably result in widespread non-compliance not from bad faith, but from structural incapacity—undermining the agency's own stated goal of improving compliance quality.

The NRA administers the ServSafe food safety certification program and coordinates menu labeling compliance across a membership base spanning independent single-unit operators, regional chains, and national franchises. Each of these segments faces radically different resource constraints. A single implementation timeline applied uniformly ignores this reality [6], [7]. The restaurant industry's average annual employee turnover rate exceeds 70%, meaning that any training or documentation system required under the new standards must be re-implemented continuously, not just at the point of initial compliance. A short implementation window does not account for the time required to train new hires on revised protocols—a cycle that never ends in food service. The NRA's September 2023 Restaurant Industry Tracking Survey identified labor costs and recruitment as the top two operational pressures facing operators, confirming that the workforce capacity needed to absorb a rapid compliance transition simply does not exist at the unit level for most of our members [8], [9].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
In light of the significant operational and financial impacts detailed above, the NRA respectfully offers the following recommendations:

**Regarding Revised Reporting Obligations and Menu Labeling:**
*   **Menu Labeling Compliance Delay:** The agency should (1) defer any revised reporting obligations until at least 18 months after the January 1, 2026 compliance date to allow operators to stabilize existing menu labeling systems before absorbing new reporting mandates; (2) conduct a formal Small Business Impact Analysis quantifying per-location compliance costs disaggregated by restaurant format and size; and (3) explicitly address in the final rule whether new reporting requirements are additive to or replace existing submission obligations, eliminating any ambiguity that would force operators to maintain parallel compliance tracks.
*   **Flexibility for Seasonal/Daily Specials:** Any revised reporting obligation should explicitly provide flexibility for seasonal, daily, or limited-time offers, recognizing the operational burden of continuously updating and reporting data for items with short lifespans.
*   **Standardized Reporting Templates:** The agency should (1) publish a standardized, machine-readable data submission template (e.g., XML or JSON schema) concurrent with any revised content requirements, developed in consultation with industry technology vendors; (2) provide a safe harbor period of no less than 12 months during which good-faith reporting errors in new content fields are treated as technical violations subject to corrective action rather than punitive enforcement; and (3) establish a pre-submission validation tool—similar to the SEC's EDGAR filing system—that allows operators to verify data format compliance before official submission, reducing inadvertent errors.

**Regarding Documentation Standards and Food Safety Training:**
*   **Cost-Benefit Analysis:** The agency should (1) conduct a formal cost-benefit analysis specifically scoped to food service operators before finalizing any documentation standard changes, (2) grandfather existing nationally recognized certification programs like ServSafe that already meet or exceed baseline food safety outcomes, and (3) adopt a tiered documentation standard that scales requirements to establishment size and risk profile rather than applying uniform mandates across a sector ranging from single-location diners to national chains. The agency specifically requested comments on expected costs—we urge the agency to commission an independent economic impact study before proceeding, as current proposal materials do not adequately quantify per-operator compliance costs.
*   **Harmonization with Existing Frameworks:** In direct response to the agency's solicitation on 'alternatives,' we recommend the agency adopt a mutual recognition framework under which documentation maintained in compliance with FDA menu labeling rules, state health codes certified by the Conference for Food Protection, or equivalent recognized standards is deemed to satisfy the new documentation requirements for overlapping subject matter. The agency should publish a crosswalk document mapping existing industry documentation obligations to the new standards, identifying only the genuine gaps that require new record-keeping.
*   **Digital Documentation:** The agency should explicitly address high-turnover industries in the final rule by (1) allowing digital, automated documentation systems—such as those already integrated into platforms like ServSafe Manager—to satisfy new standards without requiring duplicative paper or manual records; (2) establishing a 'continuous certification' safe harbor for operators enrolled in nationally recognized programs that already maintain electronic records; and (3) setting a minimum 24-month phase-in period for any new documentation standards to allow operators to update systems without disrupting ongoing certification cycles. In response to the agency's request for comments on operational impacts, we urge the agency to recognize that documentation burden and food safety outcomes are not linearly correlated in high-turnover service environments.

**Regarding Implementation Timelines:**
*   **Tiered Implementation:** The agency should replace the proposed uniform implementation timeline with a tiered, size-based phase-in schedule: (1) large chains with 500+ locations should be given 12 months; (2) mid-size operators with 20–499 locations should receive 18 months; and (3) independent and small operators with fewer than 20 locations should receive a minimum of 24 months. Additionally, the agency should build in a formal 'safe harbor' period of at least 6 months following each phase-in deadline during which good-faith compliance efforts are recognized and technical assistance is prioritized over enforcement. The agency specifically requested comments on operational impacts and alternatives—this tiered approach directly responds to both questions by acknowledging structural differences in operator capacity while still advancing the agency's compliance modernization goals.
*   **Extended Overall Timeline:** The agency should extend the proposed implementation timeline by a minimum of 18-24 months from the final rule publication date, with a phased rollout that distinguishes between large chain operators (20+ locations) and independent/small operators. The agency should also establish a formal 'good faith compliance' safe harbor period of at least 12 months post-effective date during which operators demonstrating documented transition efforts are not subject to enforcement action. We specifically request the agency publish a detailed cost-benefit analysis—responsive to its own request for comments on expected costs—that accounts for per-location compliance transition costs, not merely aggregate industry figures.

[CONCLUSION]
The National Restaurant Association is committed to fostering a regulatory environment that prioritizes public health and safety while remaining sensitive to the operational realities of the industry it regulates. As currently drafted, the proposed revised reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards would impose significant, disproportionate, and largely unquantified costs on restaurant operators, particularly small businesses, without clear evidence of a corresponding public benefit. We urge the agency to carefully consider the practical impacts of these changes on a sector already grappling with immense challenges. By adopting the recommendations outlined above, the agency can achieve its goals of modernizing oversight and improving data quality in a manner that is both effective and sustainable for the restaurant industry.

[INTRODUCTION]
The American Society of Health-System Pharmacists (ASHP) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the proposed rule FDA-2024-XXXX, which aims to modernize and standardize oversight of regulated industries through revised reporting obligations, new implementation timelines, and enhanced documentation standards. ASHP is the largest association of pharmacy professionals who practice in hospitals, health systems, ambulatory care, and other healthcare settings, representing more than 60,000 members. Our mission is to empower pharmacists to advance patient care through optimal medication use.

ASHP strongly supports the agency's efforts to enhance data collection, improve information quality, and strengthen regulatory monitoring. Effective and timely information is foundational to ensuring patient safety, managing critical drug supply chains, and fostering innovation in healthcare. However, the success of these initiatives hinges on their practical implementation within the complex and highly regulated environments where health-system pharmacists operate. We urge the agency to consider the unique operational realities of hospital and health-system pharmacies, ensuring that new requirements are harmonized with existing standards, are risk-stratified, and provide sufficient flexibility to prevent unintended disruptions to patient care.

Our comments will focus on several key areas where the proposed rule's provisions intersect directly with health-system pharmacy practice:
1.  **Revised Reporting Obligations for Drug Shortages:** Ensuring real-time and actionable data from manufacturers to prevent patient harm.
2.  **Direct Data Sharing Pathway for Shortage Information:** Streamlining the flow of critical information from FDA to clinical decision-support tools.
3.  **Documentation Standards for Sterile Compounding:** Harmonizing federal requirements with established USP <797> and TJC frameworks to avoid duplicative burdens.
4.  **Implementation Timelines for Pharmacy Automation and Technology Validation:** Adopting risk-stratified timelines that reflect the clinical complexity and patient safety stakes of integrated automation systems.
5.  **Oversight of Outsourcing Facility Quality:** Strengthening reporting and documentation requirements to enhance monitoring of compounding quality.

[BACKGROUND]
The agency's proposal introduces a suite of new compliance requirements, focusing on three primary areas: "revised reporting obligations," the establishment of "implementation timelines" for new mandates, and the introduction of more rigorous "documentation standards." These changes are intended to address perceived gaps in current data collection and record-keeping practices, ultimately improving the quality of information received by the agency to enhance its ability to monitor safety and compliance. ASHP recognizes the critical importance of these goals and supports the agency's commitment to modernizing its oversight framework.

For health-system pharmacies, each of these proposed changes carries significant implications for patient care, operational efficiency, and compliance costs. "Revised reporting obligations," particularly for drug manufacturers, have the potential to transform how pharmacies manage supply chain disruptions. "Implementation timelines" for new mandates will dictate the feasibility of transitioning complex automated systems and clinical workflows without compromising patient safety. Finally, "documentation standards" will directly impact the record-keeping practices for critical functions like sterile compounding, where meticulous documentation is already a foundational component of patient safety. Our comments will address each of these areas, offering specific recommendations to ensure that the rule achieves its intended objectives while minimizing unintended consequences for healthcare delivery.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**1. Revised Reporting Obligations for Drug Shortages**
ASHP strongly supports the revised reporting obligations as a necessary modernization of drug shortage data collection. Drug shortages continue to pose a severe and persistent threat to patient safety and healthcare delivery, forcing health-system pharmacies to divert significant resources away from direct patient care to manage these disruptions. As of the first quarter of 2024, ASHP tracked 323 active drug shortages, representing the highest number since ASHP began tracking shortages in 2001 [8]. These shortages encompass a wide range of essential medications, including sterile injectables, oncology agents, and antibiotics, directly impacting treatment protocols and increasing medication error risks.

Under current reporting obligations, health-system pharmacists routinely learn of shortages only after supply has already been interrupted, often when they are unable to procure essential medications through their usual channels. This delay forces emergency procurement measures, therapeutic substitutions, and manual workarounds that consume significant staff time and introduce medication safety risks. For example, pharmacists may be required to compound alternative formulations or ration existing supplies, processes that inherently increase the potential for errors. The agency's own CY 2024 Report to Congress acknowledges the ongoing challenges and the need for improved shortage communication and mitigation strategies [1].

**2. Direct Data Sharing Pathway for Shortage Information**
ASHP supports the revised reporting obligations and recommends that the agency, in establishing new documentation standards, create a direct and mandatory data-sharing pathway between the FDA shortage reporting system and health-system pharmacy networks—such as ASHP's Drug Shortage Database—so that standardized manufacturer submissions are translated into actionable clinical guidance without duplicative data collection efforts. ASHP operates one of the nation's most widely used drug shortage databases, relied upon by hospital and health-system pharmacists to manage patient care during shortage events. This database serves as a vital resource for clinical decision-making, providing real-time information on affected products, available alternatives, and practice recommendations.

Currently, there is no formal mechanism for FDA-received manufacturer shortage reports to flow directly and in a standardized format to clinical decision-support tools used at the point of care. As a result, ASHP staff must independently monitor, verify, and reformat shortage information before it can be disseminated to member pharmacies—a process that introduces delays of days to weeks between a manufacturer's knowledge of a shortage and a pharmacist's ability to act on it. This redundancy not only consumes valuable resources but, more critically, prolongs the period during which pharmacists are operating with incomplete or outdated information, increasing the risk of adverse patient outcomes. The Drug Shortage Prevention Act of 2023 reflects Congressional recognition that the current information flow is inadequate [5], highlighting the urgent need for a more integrated system.

**3. Documentation Standards for Sterile Compounding**
ASHP strongly supports the proposed documentation standards as applied to sterile compounding operations, as standardized record-keeping is a foundational patient safety requirement that aligns with existing USP <797> documentation frameworks and ASHP's own compounding guidelines. Hospital and health-system pharmacies already operate under rigorous documentation mandates from the United States Pharmacopeia (USP) General Chapter <797> (2023 revision), The Joint Commission (TJC), and state boards of pharmacy [2, 3]. These mandates require meticulous record-keeping for every aspect of sterile compounding, including personnel training and competency assessments, facility environmental monitoring, equipment calibration and maintenance, raw material traceability, compounding batch records, and quality control testing.

ASHP member institutions have invested substantially in documentation infrastructure—including workflow management systems, electronic batch records, and personnel training logs—to meet these overlapping requirements. If the agency's new documentation standards introduce terminology, formatting, or retention requirements that diverge from USP <797> and TJC frameworks, health-system pharmacies will face dual compliance burdens. Maintaining one documentation system for state/USP compliance and a separate system for federal regulatory compliance creates not only direct cost increases (estimated staff time, IT system modifications, and retraining) but also introduces the risk of documentation errors that could paradoxically undermine the medication safety outcomes the rule seeks to advance.

**4. Implementation Timelines for Pharmacy Automation and Technology Validation**
ASHP supports the agency's modernization goals but urges the agency to adopt risk-stratified, phased implementation timelines for pharmacy automation and technology validation requirements that reflect the clinical complexity and patient safety stakes inherent in hospital and health-system pharmacy environments. Hospital pharmacies operate highly integrated automation ecosystems—including automated dispensing cabinets (ADCs), robotic dispensing systems, intravenous (IV) compounding robots, and barcode medication administration (BCMA) systems—that cannot be simultaneously validated and transitioned without creating dangerous gaps in medication safety workflows. ASHP's March 2023 data indicate that pharmacy automation has materially reduced dispensing errors and shifted pharmacist roles toward clinical oversight, allowing pharmacists to focus more on direct patient care and medication therapy management. Disrupting these systems under an unrealistic timeline would directly reverse those patient safety gains.

A single compressed or uniform implementation deadline would force health systems to either rush validation processes (risking patient harm) or temporarily revert to manual dispensing (increasing medication error rates and staff burnout). The pharmacy automation market, dominated by ADCs and robotic dispensing systems, reflects deep institutional integration that cannot be unwound or upgraded on a one-size-fits-all schedule. The complexity of regulatory compliance combined with the challenge of specialized skills and resources further complicates rapid transitions [5]. ASHP recommends that the agency consider the principles outlined in the ICH Q8–Q11 suite (2023–2024), which serves as a quality backbone for pharmaceutical development and includes risk-based approaches to validation [4].

**5. Oversight of Outsourcing Facility Quality**
ASHP supports the agency's overall objectives of improving information quality and enhancing monitoring, particularly as these goals relate to outsourcing facility quality concerns. Outsourcing facilities play a crucial role in the healthcare supply chain, providing compounded sterile preparations (CSPs) that are often essential for patient care, especially during drug shortages or when commercial products are unavailable. However, the quality and safety of products from these facilities have been a persistent concern, with past incidents highlighting the critical need for robust regulatory oversight. The "Revised reporting obligations changing the content of data submissions" provision in the proposed rule presents a significant opportunity to strengthen this oversight.

Currently, the content of reporting obligations from outsourcing facilities may not sufficiently cover specific quality metrics, inspection findings, or sterility data, leading to gaps in the agency's ability to monitor their compliance with current good manufacturing practices (CGMPs) and USP <797> standards. ASHP believes that more rigorous and standardized reporting from outsourcing facilities is essential to ensure patient safety and maintain trust in this critical sector of the compounding industry.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
Based on the analysis above, ASHP offers the following specific recommendations for refining the proposed rule:

**Revised Reporting Obligations for Drug Shortages:**
*   **Recommendation 1.1:** The agency should revise the reporting frequency requirement to mandate manufacturer notification within 72 hours of any anticipated supply interruption exceeding 30 days, not merely at the point of confirmed discontinuation as currently required under Section 506C of the FD&C Act [5]. This earlier notification is crucial for health-system pharmacies to activate shortage mitigation protocols *before* patient care is disrupted rather than after.
*   **Recommendation 1.2:** The agency should standardize the data submission format to include: (1) estimated duration of shortage, (2) affected NDC numbers and dosage forms, (3) root cause category (manufacturing delay, quality issue, capacity constraint, or raw material shortage), and (4) projected resupply timeline. This structured format would allow FDA and health systems to triage shortage severity and prioritize mitigation resources more effectively.
*   **Recommendation 1.3:** The agency should further consider requiring quarterly aggregate shortage forecasting reports from high-volume sterile injectable manufacturers, given that manufacturing and quality issues account for the majority of shortage causes [9]. This proactive reporting would provide invaluable foresight for system-level planning.

**Direct Data Sharing Pathway for Shortage Information:**
*   **Recommendation 2.1:** The agency should include in the revised reporting obligations a requirement that shortage data submitted by manufacturers be made available through a standardized, machine-readable API within 24 hours of receipt and validation by FDA.
*   **Recommendation 2.2:** The data schema for this API should be developed in consultation with health-system pharmacy organizations, including ASHP, to ensure that the fields captured by manufacturers map directly to the clinical information pharmacists need—specifically: therapeutic category, available alternatives, affected lot numbers, and geographic distribution constraints. This would allow ASHP and similar organizations to automate the ingestion of FDA shortage data into clinical decision-support tools, reducing the lag between manufacturer notification and pharmacist action.
*   **Recommendation 2.3:** The agency should also consider whether the revised documentation standards could support a tiered alert system that automatically flags shortages of sterile injectables, oncology agents, and other high-risk drug categories for expedited clinical guidance development—consistent with ASHP's existing shortage management framework and FDA's own prioritization practices [2].

**Documentation Standards for Sterile Compounding:**
*   **Recommendation 3.1:** The agency should explicitly cross-reference and align its new documentation standards with USP <797> Chapter documentation requirements (Section 20 of the 2023 revision) and TJC sterile compounding standards. This harmonization will reduce duplicative burdens and ensure consistency across regulatory frameworks.
*   **Recommendation 3.2:** Where the agency's requirements for documentation exceed existing standards, it should provide a phased implementation timeline of no less than 18-24 months to allow health-system pharmacies to update electronic health record and pharmacy information system integrations.
*   **Recommendation 3.3:** The agency should also convene a technical working group including ASHP, USP, and NABP to develop a unified documentation template that satisfies all applicable regulatory bodies simultaneously, reducing redundancy without sacrificing rigor.

**Implementation Timelines for Pharmacy Automation and Technology Validation:**
*   **Recommendation 4.1:** The agency should replace any single uniform compliance deadline with a tiered, risk-stratified implementation schedule.
*   **Recommendation 4.2:** This schedule should grant the longest transition periods (minimum 36 months) to systems directly interfacing with sterile compounding and high-alert medication dispensing, where validation failures carry the highest patient risk.
*   **Recommendation 4.3:** Intermediate milestones (12 and 24 months) should be established for documentation and reporting system upgrades that do not directly touch dispensing workflows.
*   **Recommendation 4.4:** The agency should create a formal variance pathway allowing health systems to submit technology validation plans demonstrating good-faith compliance progress in lieu of meeting a hard deadline.
*   **Recommendation 4.5:** The agency should also issue explicit guidance clarifying how ICH Q9 quality risk management principles apply to hospital pharmacy automation validation [4], providing a recognized framework health systems can use to prioritize their transition sequencing.

**Oversight of Outsourcing Facility Quality:**
*   **Recommendation 5.1:** Under "Revised Reporting Obligations," ASHP recommends that the *content* of submissions from outsourcing facilities include specific quality metrics (e.g., sterility testing results, endotoxin levels, environmental monitoring data), inspection findings, and recall information in a standardized format. This would allow FDA to more effectively monitor compliance with CGMPs and USP <797> standards.
*   **Recommendation 5.2:** Under "Documentation Standards," ASHP suggests that outsourcing facilities be required to maintain documentation that explicitly demonstrates compliance with CGMPs and USP <797> standards, with this documentation readily auditable by FDA. This enhanced documentation will support more rigorous oversight of outsourcing facility quality standards and improve patient safety.

[CONCLUSION]
ASHP commends the agency for proposing this suite of compliance requirements aimed at modernizing and standardizing oversight of regulated industries. We firmly believe that enhanced drug shortage prevention authority for FDA, robust outsourcing facility quality standards, and appropriate flexibility for hospital pharmacy compounding and automation implementation are essential components of a safe and effective medication use system.

Our recommendations are grounded in the practical realities of hospital and health-system pharmacy practice, where patient safety is paramount and operational efficiency is critical. We urge the agency to consider the unique challenges faced by these settings and to work collaboratively with stakeholders like ASHP to refine the proposed rule. By adopting risk-stratified timelines, harmonizing documentation standards, and ensuring real-time data flow for drug shortages, the agency can achieve its goals of improved monitoring and information quality without compromising the delivery of high-quality, safe patient care.

ASHP remains committed to partnering with the agency to develop and implement regulations that protect patients and support the vital role of pharmacy professionals in healthcare.

Sincerely,

[Signature block intentionally omitted]

### References

[2] https://rxce.com/WebMaterials/Sterile-Compounding-101

To Whom It May Concern:

My name is Derek Johnson, and I am the owner of [Vape Shop Name], an independent vape shop located in [City, State]. I am writing to express my strong opposition to the proposed suite of new compliance requirements outlined in FDA-2024-XXXX, specifically the revised reporting obligations, implementation timelines, and documentation standards.
